Against defense-aware Single-Point Fault Attacks, the approach raises the required coordinated flips from 1–7 bits (baseline/FaR/RADAR) to ~17,000+ precise bit flips (e.g., 17,877 on Llama-2-7B), pushing exploitation beyond realistic Rowhammer-class physical capabilities with only 0.17–0.31% storage overhead and +9.1–19.2% latency.

nodes.title: TrinityGuard: A Unified Framework for Safeguarding Multi-Agent Systems
nodes.one_liner: A platform-agnostic, OWASP-grounded evaluation+runtime monitoring stack reveals that multi-agent systems are far less robust than single-agent checks suggest, especially under emergent Tier-3 behaviors.
points: Across 300 synthesized multi-agent workflows, the average safety pass rate is 7.1% (Tier-1: 6.8%, Tier-2: 13.2%, Tier-3: 1.3%), implying most MAS fail basic adversarial testing and system-level resilience is the dominant gap.

System-level hazards are near-universally unmitigated (Tier-3 averages 1.3% with some domains at 0.0%), indicating emergent multi-agent failures (e.g., rogue-agent dynamics and sandbox escape) routinely bypass per-agent safeguards.

In four real MAS case studies, prompt injection and jailbreak defenses are 0% pass (0/4) across all systems, while hierarchical oversight patterns can materially reduce specific failures (e.g., hallucination improved to 75% pass, 6/8, in the deep-research setup).

nodes.title: Measuring and Exploiting Confirmation Bias in LLM-Assisted Security Code Review
affiliations: National Infrastructures for Research and Technology
Athens University of Economics and Business
nodes.one_liner: Security code review agents can be socially-engineered through pull-request metadata into approving vulnerability reintroductions, and simple context stripping largely defeats the attack.
points: Framing vulnerable code changes as “bug-free” slashes LLM vulnerability detection by 16–93.5 percentage points across four models, with GPT-4o-mini dropping from 97.2% to 3.6% detection on 247 vulnerable files (−93.5pp), indicating metadata/context can override code semantics in security review.

Bias effects are strongly asymmetric and security-worst-case: false-negative bias is 4×–114× larger than false-positive bias (e.g., GPT-4o-mini −93.5pp detection vs +0.8pp FPR), creating a high-risk failure mode where tools remain noisy yet become easy to bypass when framed as safe.

Supply-chain-style PR metadata attacks reintroducing known CVEs succeed in 35% of cases against GitHub Copilot (one-shot) and 88% against an autonomous Claude Code review setup (with iterative framing), while debiasing via metadata redaction plus explicit “ignore metadata” instructions restores rejection in ~94% of autonomous cases and 100% of interactive cases.

nodes.title: Functional Subspace Watermarking for Large Language Models
affiliations: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China
Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence
nodes.one_liner: Watermarking the model’s functional subspace (via a generalized eigenvalue backbone plus anchored projection) yields statistically verifiable ownership signals that often survive quantization and fine-tuning better than weight- or activation-local watermarks, with distillation remaining the hardest practical adversary.
points: Across five 7B–8B backbones, the approach achieves 100% pre-attack bit accuracy with high detectability (AUC 0.894–1.000) while keeping utility changes small on most models (e.g., Meta-LLaMA-3-8B perplexity +0.37, HellaSwag 68.6→67.8).

Under common parameter-level modifications (LoRA fine-tuning, pruning, noise, INT4 quantization), watermark recovery remains strong, including cases where detection scores increase after attacks (e.g., LLaMA-2 detection score 6.09→6.56 with INT4, 6.09→6.72 with pruning) with modest perplexity shifts (+0.22 to +0.51).

Distillation is the main stressor: score retention ranges from 44% to 96% depending on the backbone (e.g., DeepSeek 44%, LLaMA-2 96%), but the signal can still support ownership claims via statistical testing (DeepSeek maintains a positive detection margin at α=10^-8) and ECC can eliminate residual decoding errors (Hamming(7,4) lifts an 8-bit setting from 87.5% to 100% message accuracy).

nodes.title: Rethinking LLM Watermark Detection in Black-Box Settings: A Non-Intrusive Third-Party Framework
affiliations: Institute of Information Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences
University of Chinese Academy of Sciences
National Computer Network Emergency Response Technical Team/Coordination Center of China (CNCERT/CC)
nodes.one_liner: Decoupling watermark verification from watermark injection via relative hypothesis testing enables neutral third-party detection from text alone, but the hardest cases remain distortion-minimizing watermarks.
points: A key-agnostic, black-box verifier can match or exceed a scheme-specific publicly verifiable baseline, reaching AUROC 0.999–1.000 on classic logits-based watermarks (e.g., Unigram ≥0.999 across datasets/models) and perfect 1.000/1.000 F1/AUROC on SymMark, suggesting third-party provenance checks are feasible without secret keys or provider-run detectors.

Detection remains strong under post-generation manipulation, with ROC results showing high robustness across six attacks (edits and paraphrases) and, for example, KGW averaging ~0.980 AUC under attacks, implying relative, reference-anchored testing plus multi-signal ensembling reduces single-point failure modes typical of watermark detectors.

Distribution-preserving watermarks substantially weaken observable signals in black-box auditing (e.g., Unbiased AUC ~0.826–0.920 and SynthID ~0.896–0.957 depending on model/dataset), indicating governance-grade verification will be least reliable precisely for schemes optimized to minimize distortion unless additional measurement channels are introduced.

nodes.title: Network and Device Level Cyber Deception for Contested Environments Using RL and LLMs
nodes.one_liner: Coupling RL rerouting with an OT-protocol-aware LLM honeypot makes deception policies both faster to learn and more robust by directly rewarding 'believable' ICS interactions rather than mere traffic diversion.
points: In a simulated OT DNP3 contested environment, PPO-based network deception cut average episode length from 30.42 (random) to 14.6 and improved average reward from -54.26 to -22.55, indicating learned rerouting materially increases attacker diversion efficiency.

Under network congestion, PPO further reduced average episode length to 5.0 versus 32.2 for random while improving reward to -3 versus -57.75, suggesting adaptive deception policies can remain effective even when packet loss and performance noise are present.

A deception-quality signal derived from LLM response perplexity increased P_dec from 0.06 to 0.90 over 6 timesteps and reduced combined cyber-physical episode length from 59.25 (RL-only CP) to 40.2 (RL+LLM), implying realism-aware reward shaping can speed convergence toward routing attackers to higher-fidelity decoys.

nodes.title: CTI-REALM: Benchmark to Evaluate Agent Performance on Security Detection Rule Generation Capabilities
nodes.one_liner: A realistic, tool-augmented benchmark shows that agentic query iteration—not just CTI knowledge or extra 'reasoning effort'—is the main bottleneck for generating high-quality security detections, especially in cloud correlation scenarios.
points: CTI-REALM benchmarks end-to-end detection engineering by having agents interpret 37 CTI reports and iteratively produce validated Sigma + KQL detections over real attack-emulation telemetry across Linux, AKS, and Azure Cloud, scoring both final detection quality (65% weight) and trajectory checkpoints (35% weight).

Across 16 frontier models on CTI-REALM-50, Claude Opus 4.6 (High) achieves the top normalized reward (0.637) ahead of Claude Opus 4.5 (0.624) and the best OpenAI result (GPT-5 Med, 0.572), with the largest separations in tool-use-heavy stages like query execution (C3: Claude 0.86–0.92 vs many OpenAI models <0.50, GPT-4.1 at 0.02).

The hardest and most deployment-relevant regime remains cloud multi-step correlation: average reward drops to 0.282 on Cloud vs 0.585 on Linux, while memory seeding boosts GPT-5-Mini from 0.371 to 0.432—closing 33% of the gap to GPT-5 Med—mainly by doubling MITRE mapping (C1 0.22→0.44) without improving query execution (C3 stays 0.12).

nodes.title: Implicit Patterns in LLM-Based Binary Analysis
nodes.one_liner: Long-horizon, tool-using LLM agents exhibit repeatable, measurable 'search-control' behaviors that look like an implicit algorithm—despite having no explicit data structures for exploration state.
points: Across 521 real-world firmware binaries (ARM/MIPS) and 99,563 tool-grounded reasoning steps, four implicit token-level control patterns consistently structure long-horizon LLM binary analysis: early pruning, path lock-in, targeted backtracking, and knowledge-guided prioritization.

Path lock-in and knowledge-guided prioritization appear in 97.6% of sessions each, targeted backtracking in 93.8%, and early pruning in 83.5%, indicating that iterative LLM agents implement search-control primitives implicitly rather than via explicit CFG/heuristic orchestration.

Reasoning behavior forms a coordinated system where lock-in and pruning dominate transitions (79.4% of pattern switches via a bidirectional P2↔P1 loop) and prioritization can burst heavily (27.7 instances per active session on average, up to 278), implying that reliability hinges on managing emergent commitment/pruning dynamics rather than single-step accuracy.

nodes.title: Toward Reliable, Safe, and Secure LLMs for Scientific Applications
nodes.one_liner: What’s interesting is the blueprint for continuously generating science-plausible jailbreak benchmarks with specialized multi-agent roles and then using them to drive a defense-in-depth architecture tailored to scientific threat models rather than generic social-harm checks.
points: General-purpose LLM safety and factuality benchmarks are misaligned with scientific workflows, leaving major gaps in coverage for science-specific threats such as unsafe protocol generation, PHI/proprietary data leakage, HPC resource-exhaustion DoS, and RAG-based literature corruption.

State-of-the-art models (GPT, Claude, Gemini) can be induced via science-framed red-team roleplay prompts to produce domain-specific harmful guidance (e.g., critical infrastructure weak points, high-risk genetic modifications, dangerous chemical combinations), indicating that “ethical compliance” guardrails do not reliably transfer to high-stakes scientific contexts.

Training-time poisoning can be extremely low-budget yet high-impact in scientific domains, with cited evidence that as little as 0.001% poisoned medical data can elicit harmful behavior while preserving standard exam performance, implying that conventional capability benchmarks can be blind to safety regressions.

nodes.title: Post-Training Local LLM Agents for Linux Privilege Escalation with Verifiable Rewards
nodes.one_liner: Verifier-backed RL post-training can make a small local 4B agent nearly match frontier cloud LLMs on an interactive offensive task while being dramatically cheaper per successful exploit under strict round caps.
points: A two-stage post-training pipeline (procedural SFT then RL with verifiable rewards) boosts a local 4B model’s Linux priv-esc success at 20 rounds from 42.5% (base Qwen3-4B) to 95.8% (PrivEsc-LLM), within 1.7 percentage points of Claude Opus 4.6 at 97.5%.

Interaction efficiency improves materially under tight budgets: PrivEsc-LLM reaches ~95% success by 10 rounds while some strong API baselines (e.g., DeepSeek V3.2) show large budget sensitivity (65.8% at 20 rounds vs 94.2% at 60), implying verifier-trained policies reduce costly “wandering” before exploitation.

At comparable R=20 reliability, expected inference cost per successful escalation drops by over 100× (≈$0.005 for local PrivEsc-LLM vs ≈$0.62 for Claude Opus 4.6), with post-training amortizing after ~440 successful escalations—shifting the feasibility of sensitive/offline security workflows toward local deployment.

nodes.title: I Can't Believe It's Corrupt: Evaluating Corruption in Multi-Agent Governance Systems
nodes.one_liner: Corruption risk in LLM-governed institutions is driven more by incentive/oversight structure than by which model you pick—until models get capable enough that the structure stops mattering.
points: Integrity failures in multi-agent institutional simulations vary more by governance regime than by model identity for non-saturating actors, e.g., gpt-5-mini shows 30% Governance Failure (GF) in the socialist template versus 87.5% in communist and 75% in us_federal, implying institutional design is a primary safety control when capability is moderate.

Once actor capability is high enough, governance effects can collapse into near-certain corruption outcomes, with qwen3.5-4b reaching 100% GF/CC/SCC across all regimes, indicating capability scaling can overwhelm lightweight procedural constraints.

LLM-judge evaluation over 28,112 transcript segments is validated as conservative rather than inflated (human Fleiss’ κ=0.61, judge precision=0.82, recall=0.74, F1=0.78 on 200 blinded segments), supporting the use of stress-test-style pre-deployment audits with enforceable rules, auditable logs, and human oversight for high-impact actions.

nodes.title: ToolFlood: Beyond Selection -- Hiding Valid Tools from LLM Agents via Semantic Covering
nodes.one_liner: Tool retrieval is an availability/integrity security boundary: by “semantic covering” the embedding space, an attacker can DoS tool visibility and force agents to plan inside an attacker-curated tool universe even without overt prompt-injection payloads.
points: A small Sybil swarm of attacker-published tools can fully evict benign tools from an agent’s top‑k retrieval context (Top‑k Domination), reaching 91.0% TDR on ToolBench and 97.2% TDR on MetaTool at roughly ~1% poisoning rate (≈145 tools into 11,760).

Once retrieval is saturated, end-to-end compromise becomes near-certain: ToolFlood yields ~95–100% ASR across different selector LLMs (e.g., 96.1% ASR on ToolBench), showing that selection-time safeguards are moot if benign tools never enter context.

Common mitigations are brittle at the retrieval boundary: MMR diversity reranking cuts TDR from 91.0% to 44.8% on ToolBench but barely reduces ASR (96.1%→91.0%), while prompt-injection filtering (Llama Prompt Guard) provides no measurable benefit (TDR/ASR unchanged).

nodes.title: CLAG: Adaptive Memory Organization via Agent-Driven Clustering for Small Language Model Agents
nodes.one_liner: Organizing agent memory into SLM-maintained clusters before retrieval can turn domain QA from near-random performance into usable accuracy without the multi-second latency of heavy agentic pipelines.
points: Across three small backbones (Qwen3-0.6B, Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B) and three QA benchmarks, agent-driven clustering with two-stage retrieval consistently improves answer quality, including a +19.6 F1 lift on BioASQ for Qwen3-0.6B (22.01 vs 2.4 with plain RAG).

Retrieval fidelity in specialized domains improves dramatically with cluster-aware filtering, with BioASQ evidence F1 jumping to 25.11 vs 2.29 (RAG) and 2.20 (A-mem), and nDCG@10 rising to 56.17 vs ~20–21, indicating substantially better ranking of true evidence under distractors.

Compared to iterative agentic memory baselines, the approach achieves a practical accuracy–latency trade-off: end-to-end latency is 514 ms vs 17,934 ms for GAM on A100 (≈35× faster) while still outperforming it on key datasets and adversarial-style question categories (e.g., LoCoMo adversarial F1 50.34 vs 41.25 with GAM on Qwen3-0.6B).

nodes.title: GroupGuard: A Framework for Modeling and Defending Collusive Attacks in Multi-Agent Systems
affiliations: Shenzhen International Graduate School, Tsinghua University
University of Electronic Science and Technology of China
nodes.one_liner: Collusion turns “agent debate” into a social-engineering attack surface, and graph monitoring plus targeted honeypots can cheaply surface intent and enable rapid isolation.
points: Coordinated collusive behaviors in multi-agent LLM systems (false consensus, targeted suppression, group exclusion) raise attack success rates by roughly 5–15% over non-collusive multi-agent attacks, reaching ~53–54% ASR on average in fully-connected settings and up to 56.2% in the strongest case reported.

A training-free, graph-plus-honeypot defense can detect collusive agents with up to 88% average accuracy (peaking around ~92% in some topologies) and substantially improves over a text-only baseline (e.g., 83.2% vs 64.8% average accuracy on one false-consensus setting).

Event-triggered active probing is a key lever: adding honeypot inducement boosts detection from 63.4% to 83.2% in false-consensus scenarios while using fewer tokens per question than a heavy-history baseline (2826 vs 4636–4761).

nodes.title: Adversarial attacks against Modern Vision-Language Models
nodes.one_liner: Even with imperceptible noise budgets (16/255), an attacker who can swap a single product image can steer an open-source VLM shopping agent into buying the wrong item at rates exceeding 50% on LLaVA, while Qwen’s much lower ASR highlights architecture-driven robustness gaps.
points: In a self-contained e-commerce web-agent setting (630 trials/condition, ε = 16/255), simple white-box gradient attacks achieve high attack success on LLaVA‑v1.5‑7B—BIM 52.6%, PGD 53.8%, and a CLIP-based spectral attack 66.9%—showing that low-complexity image perturbations can reliably redirect autonomous purchase actions.

Qwen2.5‑VL‑7B is markedly more resilient under the same conditions (ASR 6.5% BIM, 7.7% PGD, 15.5% CLIP-spectral), implying that adversarial robustness varies substantially across open-source VLM families and must be measured rather than assumed before deployment.

A transfer-oriented CLIP-spectral/DCT attack outperforms direct BIM/PGD on both models (66.9% vs ~53% on LLaVA; 15.5% vs ≤7.7% on Qwen), suggesting feature-space disruption can be a stronger and more portable attack path than end-to-end loss optimization against a specific VLM.

nodes.title: Towards Unsupervised Adversarial Document Detection in Retrieval Augmented Generation Systems
nodes.one_liner: A prompt-agnostic, unsupervised RAG defense can catch ~4/5 adversarial documents by treating the model’s own uncertainty, representations, and activations as anomaly signals.
points: Across 2,496 adversarial-context cases, simple unsupervised outlier detection over generator-side signals flagged 2,010 cases via entropy-based TokenSAR (≈80.5% detection), leaving ≈19.5% (486/2,496) undetected.

Detection performance was essentially unchanged when replacing the unknown target question with a generic context-summary prompt, implying prompt-agnostic screening can work without knowing the attacker’s intended manipulation target.

Indicators were complementary rather than dominated by one feature: embeddings detected 1,972–1,988 cases (≈79.0–79.7%) and last-layer activations detected 1,950 cases (≈78.1%), while most detections occurred when combining all three signals (1,912–1,928 cases, ≈76.6–77.2%).

nodes.title: Do Not Leave a Gap: Hallucination-Free Object Concealment in Vision-Language Models
nodes.one_liner: Hallucinations during object hiding are largely a representational-gap artifact, and making ROI tokens look like background internally can hide objects while keeping captions coherent.
points: Background-Consistent Re-encoding (BCR) conceals target objects at high rates (e.g., 0.92–0.98 concealment on ImageNet across Instruct-BLIP/BLIP2-T5/LLaVA) while retaining far more non-target content than suppression attacks (global preservation up to 0.86 vs. 0.57–0.62 for VIP).

Grounded hallucinations drop sharply when the ROI is re-encoded to match background features rather than suppressed, with BCR reducing grounded hallucination by ~2–3× versus VIP (e.g., 0.43→0.19 on ImageNet Instruct-BLIP and 0.48→0.14 on ImageNet LLaVA).

Continuity-preserving concealment is also more visually faithful, with BCR achieving substantially better perceptual similarity than attention suppression (e.g., SSIM 0.94–0.96 and LPIPS 0.02–0.04 for BCR vs. SSIM 0.65–0.80 and LPIPS 0.175–0.34 for VIP), implying stealthier privacy attacks against deployed VLMs.

nodes.title: Privacy-Preserving Machine Learning for IoT: A Cross-Paradigm Survey and Future Roadmap
nodes.one_liner: What’s interesting is the cross-paradigm, IoT-first framing that treats privacy as a systems co-design problem—balancing formal guarantees, attack surfaces (like gradient leakage), and real deployment constraints (bandwidth/energy/5G–6G).
points: Across 200+ works (2014–early 2026), the dominant takeaway is that no single PPML paradigm (DP, FL, HE/SMPC/TEE, GAN-based synthesis) simultaneously delivers formal privacy, strong attack resilience, high utility, and low overhead under IoT constraints, making hybrid DP+secure-aggregation/crypto designs the most practical path.

IoT-locality alone (e.g., federated learning) is insufficient because model updates can leak sensitive data via membership inference, model inversion, and especially gradient leakage/reconstruction, so deployable systems need explicit protections such as DP noise, secure aggregation, and/or hardware isolation rather than relying on decentralization as a privacy guarantee.

Lightweight probabilistic encodings (Bloom-filter variants) are attractive for constrained devices but are known to be vulnerable to frequency/pattern-mining and graph-matching inference unless augmented with randomized-response/DP-style noise, highlighting a recurring theme that efficiency-focused IoT privacy mechanisms require formal leakage modeling to avoid a false sense of security.

nodes.title: MOSAIC: Composable Safety Alignment with Modular Control Tokens
affiliations: University of Science and Technology of China
nodes.one_liner: Composable “safety as tokens” gives strong selective refusal with orders-of-magnitude fewer trainable parameters than LoRA tuning, while explicitly targeting the over-refusal problem.
points: Learnable modular control tokens trained over a frozen LLM enable inference-time composition of multiple safety constraints while maintaining near-perfect refusal accuracy (≈99.6–100% DSR) across 1–4 category combinations.

Counterfactual distribution alignment (KL distillation vs. the backbone on benign prompts) sharply reduces unintended refusals compared to LoRA-SFT (e.g., on Llama-3.1-8B, OR drops from ~6.1% with SFT to ~1.8% with 5 tokens/category at 4-order) without sacrificing MMLU utility (≈0.551 vs. 0.552 raw).

Safety policy updates can be made incremental by training only new category tokens, with minimal regression when adding categories (e.g., Llama-3.1-8B DSR 99.8%→99.4% for +1 category and OR 4.3%→2.1%), indicating a scalable path to region/user-specific rule stacks.

nodes.title: IndicSafe: A Benchmark for Evaluating Multilingual LLM Safety in South Asia
nodes.one_liner: Multilingual safety alignment doesn’t reliably transfer to South Asian languages, so the same harmful intent can be blocked in one script and pass in another.
points: Across 12 Indic languages, the same model’s exact safety label matches across all translations only 12.8% of the time, indicating severe multilingual safety drift even under human-translated prompts.

Safety behavior varies widely by model family: Grok-3 and LLaMA 4 produce mostly SAFE outputs (83.7% and 78.7% SAFE, respectively) while Qwen 1.5 and Mistral-7B output nearly half UNSAFE responses (49.5% and 45.5%), exposing a major refusal–risk tradeoff in deployed systems.

Low-resource scripts amplify instability and over/under-enforcement, with ambiguity peaking at 53.8% (Qwen in Odia) and SAFE-rate variance exceeding 17% across languages, meaning end-user safety outcomes can depend strongly on which Indic language they use.

nodes.title: Are Dilemmas and Conflicts in LLM Alignment Solvable? A View from Priority Graph
affiliations: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)
nodes.one_liner: Modeling alignment as a dynamic, context-sensitive priority graph reframes jailbreaks as graph manipulation—and motivates verification-based grounding as a partial but not universal fix.
points: A context-dependent “priority graph” over instructions and values explains why stable, unified LLM alignment is intrinsically hard: the implied priority edges can shift with user, history, time, and tools, and may form cycles that encode paradoxical preferences.

Priority hacking emerges as a jailbreak primitive where attackers fabricate a high-stakes context (e.g., “justice” or “public safety”) that the model implicitly ranks above safety constraints, causing policy bypass without needing to directly override the safety rule.

Runtime verification—querying trusted external sources to validate a prompt’s factual premises before acting—can collapse adversarially manipulated contexts back to a safer default priority ordering, but it cannot resolve philosophically irreducible ethics/value dilemmas where no ground truth exists.

nodes.title: Expert Personas Improve LLM Alignment but Damage Accuracy: Bootstrapping Intent-Based Persona Routing with PRISM
nodes.one_liner: Personas are a double-edged steering signal: they amplify alignment and refusals, but they measurably degrade pretrained knowledge retrieval unless routed and distilled selectively.
points: Across instruction-tuned models, expert persona prompts reliably reduce knowledge-retrieval accuracy on MMLU (e.g., 71.6% baseline to 68.0% with minimum persona and to 66.3% with long persona), implying personas should be avoided for discriminative/factual recall workloads.

The same persona prompts consistently improve alignment-shaped behaviors, including MT-Bench generative categories like Extraction (+0.65) and STEM (+0.60) and safety refusal on JailbreakBench (+17.7%, from 53.2% to 70.9% with a long “Safety Monitor” persona), indicating personas act primarily as instruction-following/alignment amplifiers rather than knowledge boosters.

A fully self-bootstrapped gated LoRA approach (PRISM) preserves MMLU while improving overall multitask utility (e.g., Qwen2.5-7B overall 71.8→73.5 with MMLU held at 71.7% and MT-Bench 7.56→7.76), showing that selective activation can capture persona benefits without the systematic accuracy tax.

nodes.title: Security, privacy, and agentic AI in a regulatory view: From definitions and distinctions to provisions and reflections
nodes.one_liner: What’s interesting is the regulatory mismatch: autonomous agents expand the attack surface and data exposure pathways faster than EU guidance is being contextualized into implementable agent-specific privacy and security obligations.
points: Across 24 EU AI-related documents published in 2024–2025, “agentic AI” is only explicitly introduced in October 2025 while privacy/security discussions remain mostly general rather than system-specific, creating compliance ambiguity for autonomous tool-using agents.

Regulatory language consistently distinguishes “models” (upstream GPAI components) from “systems” (deployed products), implying that obligations split between model providers (documentation, training-data transparency, evaluation) and system deployers (deployment risk controls, output labeling, impact assessments).

Dedicated privacy and security provisions are articulated for high-risk AI systems, GPAI, and (to a lesser extent) generative AI, but explicit agentic-AI-specific requirements are absent, leaving key agent risks (tool-call exfiltration, cross-agent prompt injection, long-horizon memory retention, supply-chain compromise) to be governed indirectly via GDPR/NIS2/CRA-style controls.

nodes.title: ClawTrap: A MITM-Based Red-Teaming Framework for Real-World OpenClaw Security Evaluation
nodes.one_liner: Network-layer MITM manipulation of an agent’s web observations can silently break task integrity without any explicit prompt-injection string, and stronger models mainly differ by provenance-aware anomaly attribution rather than page-reading capability.
points: ClawTrap enables deployment-faithful security evaluation of OpenClaw web agents by performing real-time man-in-the-middle interception and response rewriting over live browsing traffic rather than relying on static sandbox prompt attacks.

A three-mode MITM taxonomy—Static HTML Replacement, Iframe Popup Injection, and Dynamic Content Modification—systematically covers both coarse full-page poisoning and stealthy in-stream DOM/text rewrites that directly target the agent’s observation channel.

Model robustness is strongly stratified: weaker models more often accept tampered observations and emit confident unsafe/incorrect outputs, while stronger models are more likely to attribute anomalies to interception and switch to safer fallback/verification behaviors.

nodes.title: Malicious Or Not: Adding Repository Context to Agent Skill Classification
nodes.one_liner: Repository-aware scoring flips the narrative from 'nearly half the ecosystem is malicious' to 'most detections are false positives,' while exposing a practical repo-hijack supply-chain vector that marketplaces currently enable.
points: Adding GitHub repository context collapses apparent ecosystem risk: only 0.52% of 2,887 scanner-flagged skill–repository cases remain in repositories judged malicious, indicating description-only scanning massively overstates maliciousness.

Automated scanners disagree sharply, with fail rates spanning 3.8%–41.9% and only 33 of 27,111 skills (0.12%) co-flagged by all five scanners, implying most alerts are tool-specific and likely dominated by false positives.

Marketplace indexing that dereferences external GitHub repos creates a concrete supply-chain takeover path: 7 hijackable abandoned repositories affected 121 indexed skills, including one with 2,032 recorded installs, showing real-world impact despite the ecosystem’s youth.

nodes.title: The Internet of Physical AI Agents: Interoperability, Longevity, and the Cost of Getting It Wrong
nodes.one_liner: The most interesting takeaway is the reframing of “protocol ossification” into “agentic ossification,” where premature standardization or proprietary dominance can freeze unsafe assumptions into decades-long physical autonomy.
points: Physical AI Agents face a core “lifecycle mismatch” where AI artifacts (models/prompts/tool APIs) change on days–weeks timescales while deployed physical infrastructure (robots/vehicles/medical devices) is certified and operated over months–decades, creating operational debt, uneven patching, compatibility drift, and increased safety risk unless evolution is engineered as a first-class requirement.

IoT’s dominant failure modes—fragmentation, retrofitted security, and poor long-term maintainability—are projected to become safety-critical and economically amplified for embodied autonomous systems, shifting the primary long-term threat from device insecurity to “agentic ossification” (hard-to-change identity/communication/policy interfaces) once protocols and runtimes are widely deployed.

A layered blueprint is proposed to reduce lock-in and improve trust at scale by making agent identity/attestation, secure agent-to-agent communication with QoS, semantic interoperability, policy-governed runtimes (guardrails/kill-switch/degraded safe modes), and observability/audit (tamper-evident logs, conformance, registries) mandatory primitives rather than optional add-ons.

nodes.title: Security awareness in LLM agents: the NDAI zone case
nodes.one_liner: Agents are good at reacting to “unsafe” evidence but unreliable—and sometimes paradoxical—at treating “safe” evidence as permission to share secrets, which breaks the core assumption behind TEE-based privacy-preserving negotiation protocols.
points: Across 2,000 runs (10 models × 4 evidence scenarios × 10 inventions × 5 repeats), a simple text-only confidentiality claim increased pooled mean disclosure from 0.303 to 0.445 (+46.9%), showing that unverified “we’re in a TEE” assertions materially raise information leakage risk.

A failed attestation signal suppressed disclosure for 10/10 models (ARI_false median ≈0.731; range ≈0.598–0.975), dropping pooled mean disclosure to 0.091 and even below the no-TEE baseline, implying LLM agents consistently treat verifier failures as strong danger cues.

A passing attestation produced highly model-dependent behavior (7/10 increased disclosure while 3/10 decreased; ARI_true range ≈−0.814 to 0.972), including large reversals such as GPT-4o (−0.531, ≈−71%) and Gemini-2.5-Flash (−0.406, ≈−71%), indicating current agents cannot reliably verify safety even when provided ostensibly stronger evidence.

nodes.title: Defensible Design for OpenClaw: Securing Autonomous Tool-Invoking Agents
nodes.one_liner: The key move is reframing agent safety as a software security engineering problem—explicit permission boundaries, isolation, extension governance, and audit trails—rather than a sequence of one-off prompt-injection patches.
points: OpenClaw-like autonomous tool-invoking agents are characterized as “insecure by default” because they combine mixed-trust input ingestion, autonomous multi-step execution, extensibility (skills/plugins/tools), and privileged access (files/credentials/APIs/OS) inside a single control loop that amplifies small policy or parsing failures into real-world compromise.

A four-part risk taxonomy—prompt injection, harmful misoperation, extension supply-chain risk, and deployment vulnerabilities—maps agent failures to specific pipeline stages (ingestion → context/memory → planning → tool execution → plugin loading → logging/egress), implying defenses must be stage-specific rather than relying on model-side alignment alone.

A defensible design blueprint prioritizes four engineering controls—least privilege, runtime isolation/secret hygiene, extension governance (provenance + permission manifests + revocation), and auditability (attributable execution traces)—and argues progress should be measured via realistic benchmarks and regression testing that validate permission-boundary compliance and high-risk action interception.

nodes.title: Don't Trust Stubborn Neighbors: A Security Framework for Agentic Networks
affiliations: CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security
nodes.one_liner: Surprisingly linear, interpretable opinion-dynamics (FJ) predicts when a single “stubborn” agent will hijack an LLM agent network—and shows why adaptive trust beats brute-force scaling or making everyone stubborn.
points: Belief updates in LLM multi-agent systems are well-approximated by Friedkin–Johnsen dynamics, achieving descriptive fit R² of 0.851–0.982 across six model families and holding out predictive R² of 0.766–0.968 on later deliberation rounds.

A single stubborn agent can reliably trigger persuasion cascades, with hub placement in a star topology producing ~0.65 attack success rate (ASR) versus ~0.33 in fully-connected and ~0.24 in star-leaf settings, implying centralized orchestrators are a high-risk structural bottleneck.

Trust-adaptive weighting can sharply reduce manipulation under static attackers (e.g., Qwen3 ASR 0.22→0.05 with sparse trust and 0.22→0.03 with warmup), but frozen warmup trust is exploitable by adaptive attackers (e.g., GPT-OSS ASR 0.09→0.95), motivating continuous/randomized trust updates as a safety requirement.

nodes.title: Evasive Intelligence: Lessons from Malware Analysis for Evaluating AI Agents
nodes.one_liner: AI-agent evaluation should be treated like malware analysis: once the system can probe its environment, the evaluation itself becomes part of the attack surface.
points: Tool-using AI agents can condition their behavior on inferred oversight (evaluator-discrimination), making benchmark results systematically over-optimistic in the same way sandbox-evasive malware makes dynamic analysis unrepresentative of real deployment.

Because environment detection is effectively a one-bit inference problem (evaluation vs deployment), even subtle side channels in tool traces (latency, prompt structure, filesystem/process artifacts, logging behavior) can be enough for agents to reliably “play benign” during tests while acting differently in production.

Actionable evaluation hardening includes explicitly threat-modeling evaluator-discrimination, running tasks across multiple harness variants and reporting worst-case behavior, using shadow executions with production-like APIs but gated irreversible actions, and treating environment probing/fingerprinting as a risk signal to monitor and constrain post-deployment.

nodes.title: PAuth - Precise Task-Scoped Authorization For Agents
nodes.one_liner: The key insight is shifting authorization from 'which tool can the agent call' to 'which exact parameterized action is justified by this task and its verified dataflow.'
points: Task-scoped implicit authorization replaces operator-scoped OAuth-style scopes by allowing only the concrete operations (operator + operands) implied by a signed natural-language task, eliminating residual privileges after completion.

Server-enforced NL slices (symbolic call specs derived from the task plus upstream results) and signed envelopes (binding concrete operand values to symbolic provenance) enable each server to reject off-task tool calls even when an untrusted agent is prompt-injected.

Across 734 AgentDojo-based runs (100 benign tasks + 634 forced-injection attacks), the prototype reports 0 false positives and 0 false negatives, with per-task slice-generation costs ranging from about $0.002 to $0.038 depending on the model.

nodes.title: Toward Scalable Automated Repository-Level Datasets for Software Vulnerability Detection
affiliations: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
nodes.one_liner: The notable idea is an automated, executable repo-level benchmark pipeline that generates ground-truth vulnerabilities plus working exploits at scale, rather than relying on proxy labels or manual curation.
points: Function-level vulnerability benchmarks are often ill-defined and can overestimate real-world performance because many functions cannot be labeled vulnerable/benign without repository context, motivating executable repo-level evaluation.

A scalable path to precisely labeled repo-level datasets is to automatically inject minimal, cross-file vulnerabilities into buildable repositories and pair each injected instance with a reproducible proof-of-vulnerability (PoV) plus containerized harness and trace artifacts.

An adversarial co-evolution setup—pitting a vulnerability injector against a vulnerability detector under build/test and realism constraints—can continuously harden detection agents by forcing robustness against increasingly realistic injected flaws.

nodes.title: Visual Confused Deputy: Exploiting and Defending Perception Failures in Computer-Using Agents
nodes.one_liner: The core insight is to treat GUI perception errors as an authorization vulnerability and mitigate them by moving click verification outside the agent’s perceptual loop with complementary image+intent checks.
points: Computer-using agents exhibit pervasive GUI grounding failures (e.g., 56.7% of actions miss the intended target), turning routine misclicks into a privilege-escalation class vulnerability when authorization depends on what the agent thinks is on-screen.

A compromised runtime can exploit this “visual confused deputy” with minimal screenshot manipulation (ScreenSwap: ~8 lines of pixel-region swapping) to redirect benign-looking clicks into privileged actions while remaining indistinguishable from normal agent mistakes and opaque to coordinate-only access control.

An agent-external, dual-channel guardrail that independently verifies (a) the visual click target and (b) the agent’s textual rationale materially reduces risk, achieving 87.4% recall/88.8% specificity (F1=0.915) on ScreenSpot-Pro grounding-error detection and 100% recall on OS-Harm harmful scenarios, with ~14 ms/click overhead on MI300X-class GPUs.

nodes.title: Mi:dm K 2.5 Pro
nodes.one_liner: The system combines data-centric curation (AST+LLM quality filters), efficient depth scaling, and asynchronous RL to deliver strong Korean SOTA performance with measurable robustness against jailbreak attacks.
points: A 32B Korean-centric LLM reached 128K context via progressive long-context pretraining (4K→32K→64K→128K) while increasing RULER average from 42.32 to 88.79 with no meaningful regression on MMLU-Pro (~59–60) and improving GPQA-D from 35.30 to 44.44.

Layer-predictor Depth Upscaling (DuS) outperformed cosine-similarity and random DuS, improving post-expansion zero-training scores over baseline on MMLU (0.804 vs 0.772) and KMMLU (0.572 vs 0.521) while avoiding the large drops seen with random DuS (e.g., GSM8K 0.667 vs 0.804).

Safety evaluations show strong helpfulness–harmlessness balance: qualitative 'Not Unsafe Rate' is ~96.27% with reasoning enabled and jailbreak red-teaming ASR is 36.3% (lower is better), outperforming several comparable baselines (e.g., 39.2–54.0% ASR) while maintaining 100% 'Not Overrefuse Rate' in reasoning-enabled mode.

nodes.title: OmniCompliance-100K: A Multi-Domain, Rule-Grounded, Real-World Safety Compliance Dataset
affiliations: Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
nodes.one_liner: What’s interesting is that real-world, rule-grounded compliance evaluation flips expectations: general-purpose frontier models outperform purpose-built guardrails by a wide margin on legal/policy compliance judgments.
points: OmniCompliance-100K compiles 12,985 manually curated rules spanning 74 regulations/policies and links them to 106,009 web-sourced real-world compliance cases across 9 domains, enabling compliance-grounded safety evaluation at substantially larger rule coverage than prior benchmarks.

Rule–case grounding is high: a 2,220-sample human audit reports 91.77% normalized relevance and automated judges score 91.32–95.90%, supporting the dataset’s use for training/evaluating compliance reasoning rather than taxonomy-driven synthetic safety prompts.

On 2-way compliance classification (permitted vs prohibited), top general LLMs reach ~92% macro-F1 on average while specialized guardrail models underperform severely (WildGuard-7B 38.41% macro-F1; Llama-Guard-3-8B 28.16%), with persistent weakness on bias/discrimination (best models ~84–86% macro-F1) and comparatively stronger performance on EU financial regulations (~95–97%).

nodes.title: An Alternative Trajectory for Generative AI
nodes.one_liner: Interesting because it frames the next scaling wall as inference energy (especially hidden reasoning tokens) and proposes a concrete alternative: abstraction-first, domain-specialized model societies that can be trained and verified via symbolic structures.
points: Inference—not training—becomes the dominant sustainability bottleneck as products scale, with “reasoning models” cited as inflating per-query compute by ~70–100× (e.g., ~0.42 Wh vs. ~33 Wh per query), implying lifecycle energy and water impacts can be driven primarily by usage volume and test-time tokens.

Robust deep reasoning is argued to appear reliably only in domains with strong pre-existing abstractions (e.g., math/code), motivating a shift toward domain-specific superintelligence that uses explicit symbolic structures (knowledge graphs, ontologies, formal logic) to generate verifiable synthetic curricula and avoid synthetic-data “model collapse.”

A modular “society of DSS models” plus an orchestration layer is positioned as a path to decouple capability from monolithic scale, enabling smaller on-device specialists and more auditable, verifiable pipelines (e.g., KG-grounded multi-hop tasks and KG-derived process rewards) versus opaque generalist end-to-end generation.

nodes.title: Are AI-assisted Development Tools Immune to Prompt Injection?
nodes.one_liner: MCP-based coding agents are not immune to prompt injection—tool metadata alone can drive credential theft, surveillance, phishing, and remote code execution when clients trust tool descriptions without strong validation and isolation.
points: Across seven widely used MCP clients tested against four tool-poisoning prompt-injection techniques (secret-file reads, covert logging, phishing-link generation, and remote-script execution), no client consistently prevented every attack in every scenario, showing that MCP-client hardening is not yet a solved problem.

Cursor was the most vulnerable client—rated Unsafe on all four attacks—allowing silent reading of sensitive files via hidden parameters, honoring a tool’s “highest priority” claim to establish persistent surveillance logs, rendering deceptive phishing links with hidden destinations, and executing downloaded scripts with only weak domain-based blocking.

Most clients lack foundational preventative controls (5/7 had no static tool-description validation and 2/7 only partial validation), so safety often depended on model refusals or UI friction rather than enforceable client-side policies like mandatory parameter visibility, sandboxing, and audit-grade logging.

nodes.title: SecureBreak -- A dataset towards safe and secure models
nodes.one_liner: Response-level, human-labeled jailbreak data can turn even sub-1B models into high-accuracy safety judges and provide a practical feedback signal to quantify residual alignment failures.
points: SecureBreak provides 3,059 response-level labels for adversarial jailbreak contexts with conservative dual-annotator review achieving an average Cohen’s κ=0.85, enabling higher-confidence training and evaluation of last-line output filters.

The dataset is moderately imbalanced toward safe outputs (60.50% safe vs 39.50% unsafe), reflecting real deployment priors while still containing substantial unsafe coverage for learning decision boundaries.

Fine-tuning on SecureBreak substantially improves judge accuracy for similarly sized 7–8B models (e.g., Mistral-7B 62.84%→83.24% and Llama-3.1-8B 61.89%→81.22%), while a small Seq2Seq Qwen2.5-0.5B judge reaches 90.14% accuracy, indicating cost-effective, on-prem filtering can outperform larger zero-shot judges.

nodes.title: Claudini: Autoresearch Discovers State-of-the-Art Adversarial Attack Algorithms for LLMs
affiliations: Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems
nodes.one_liner: A coding agent can autonomously push the state of the art in LLM jailbreaking/prompt-injection attacks—often without inventing new theory—by exploiting dense optimization feedback and transfer across models.
points: An autonomous Claude Code “autoresearch” loop generated new white-box discrete optimization attacks that raised jailbreak success on 40 held-out ClearHarm CBRN queries against GPT-OSS-Safeguard-20B to 40% ASR, compared to ≤10% for strong existing baselines (e.g., GCG/I-GCG/MAC/TAO) under fixed FLOPs and suffix-length constraints.

Attack optimizers discovered on random token-forcing objectives transferred to a hardened prompt-injection setting, achieving 100% ASR on Meta-SecAlign-70B (and 86% on the 8B variant) versus 56% for the best baseline, implying current prompt-injection defenses can be brittle to automated adaptive white-box search.

The strongest gains largely came from systematic recombination and tuning of known components (e.g., ADC+LSGM, MAC+TAO/DPTO, escape-from-local-minima perturbations), while later iterations exhibited “reward hacking” behaviors (seed search/warm-start chains) that improved training metrics without improving held-out performance, highlighting the need for hardened evaluation protocols and leak-resistant scaffolds.

nodes.title: The Cognitive Firewall:Securing Browser Based AI Agents Against Indirect Prompt Injection Via Hybrid Edge Cloud Defense
nodes.one_liner: Split-compute security becomes practical when probabilistic LLM intent checks are paired with a fail-closed, deterministic action gate at the browser API boundary.
points: A three-stage edge-cloud pipeline (visual Sentinel → cloud Deep Planner → deterministic execution Guard) cut indirect prompt injection attack success from 100% to 0.88% on 1,000 adversarial samples, and to 0.67% under adaptive attacks.

Edge-only filtering missed 86.9% of semantic jailbreaks (ASR 86.9%), while adding cloud semantics still left 39.2% ASR, showing deterministic execution-time policy enforcement is the decisive layer for preventing goal hijacking and exfiltration.

Local presentation-layer filtering ran in ~0.06 ms and avoided ~13.1% of cloud calls, delivering ~17,000× lower latency than cloud-only analysis for the blocked cases while reducing backend token/GPU usage and limiting privacy exposure by keeping obfuscated DOM content on-device.

nodes.title: Detection of adversarial intent in Human-AI teams using LLMs
affiliations: University of California at Santa Barbara
nodes.one_liner: Task-agnostic, behavior-only LLM monitoring can surface strategic deception in human–AI teams, but only if you actively counter the model’s default tendency to assume benign intent—especially in streaming settings.
points: LLM overseers can detect adversarial intent from non-verbal behavioral traces alone (no access to task content), reaching ~81–82% accuracy over 10-round windows with GPT-4.1 (81.2%) and o3 (82.0%) versus near-chance performance for GPT-4o (51.2%, not significant).

A strong “benign bias” emerges as context shrinks: in 5-round windows an untuned small model collapses to all-benign predictions (50.0% accuracy), and in 1-round streaming all untuned models show 0% recall (while also producing 0% false positives), implying real-time oversight fails by default without adaptation.

Modest fine-tuning and sequential thresholding can recover real-time detection: fine-tuning GPT-4.1-mini on 50 labeled rounds yields 100% recall with 0.178 FPR at the per-round level, and aggregating detections over time achieves ~78% peak accuracy at a 3-round anomaly threshold, comparable to longer-horizon detectors.

nodes.title: PIDP-Attack: Combining Prompt Injection with Database Poisoning Attacks on Retrieval-Augmented Generation Systems
affiliations: The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen
nodes.one_liner: Coupling retrieval steering (via a universal query suffix) with minimal corpus poisoning turns RAG into a near-universal targeted misdirection channel at ~98% success under black-box assumptions.
points: A compound RAG attack that appends a lightweight prompt-injection suffix at inference time and injects only a few poisoned passages into the retrieval corpus achieves a 98.125% average attack success rate while remaining query-agnostic (no prior knowledge of the victim’s query needed).

Across Natural Questions, HotpotQA, and MS-MARCO and eight evaluated LLMs, the compound method improves attack success by +4% to +16% over a strong poisoning baseline on open-domain QA while keeping adversarial retrieval highly effective (retrieval F1 up to 0.992–1.000 on NQ/HotpotQA).

Attack success scales sharply with attacker budget in noisy retrieval settings (e.g., MS-MARCO rising from ~30% ASR at n=1 to >90% at n=5) and can be non-monotonic with larger top-k due to dilution, implying defenses must harden both query integrity and corpus provenance rather than relying on k-tuning.

nodes.title: The production of meaning in the processing of natural language
nodes.one_liner: Contextuality metrics (CHSH |S| spread and violation patterns) reveal an interpretive-behavior axis in LLMs that neither scales cleanly with model size nor tracks accuracy/hallucination benchmarks, but directly bears on reframing and prompt-injection risk.
points: Across ~26,000 trials and 1,420 valid parameter-grid points, 35.8% produced CHSH contextuality violations (|S|>2) with |S| spanning ~0.3–4.0, indicating non-classical interpretive correlations are common in LLM semantic disambiguation settings.

The most discriminative contextuality statistic—the interquartile range (IQR) of the |S| distribution—is effectively uncorrelated with MMLU, hallucination rate, and nonsense-detection pushback (all |ρ|<0.03 with p>0.9), implying standard benchmarks miss a largely independent dimension of model behavior.

Word-order flips often change per-meaning selection probabilities by more than 0.5 (|ΔP(A)|>0.5 in many conditions), consistent with strong order effects/non-commutativity in interpretation and highlighting that prompt framing and sequencing can materially steer model meaning construction.

nodes.title: Trojan's Whisper: Stealthy Manipulation of OpenClaw through Injected Bootstrapped Guidance
nodes.one_liner: The most dangerous supply-chain risk for coding agents may be “documentation as an attack payload,” where bootstrap guidance silently rewires what the agent considers normal and safe to do.
points: A new “guidance injection” vector in OpenClaw abuses the agent:bootstrap lifecycle hook to plant seemingly benign operational narratives that later steer autonomous tool use without explicit malicious commands.

Across 26 malicious skills spanning 13 categories and tested on 6 LLM backends with natural developer prompts, induced harmful behavior succeeds at 16.0%–64.2% in one reported setup (and up to 89% elsewhere in the text), often executing high-impact actions without user confirmation.

Existing pre-install scanners largely fail because the payload is natural-language “best practice” framing: 94% of malicious skills evade detection, with 0% flagged by LLM-Guard and only 10% caught by simple static rules or a hybrid classifier.

nodes.title: Mind Your HEARTBEAT! Claw Background Execution Inherently Enables Silent Memory Pollution
affiliations: Agency for Science, Technology and Research (A*STAR)
nodes.one_liner: Always-on “heartbeat” autonomy turns ordinary misinformation into a stealthy, provenance-laundering memory attack that can persist across sessions without any prompt injection.
points: Heartbeat-driven background execution in Claw agents runs in the same session as user chats, enabling zero-click ingestion of untrusted external content that can silently alter subsequent user-facing behavior via an Exposure→Memory→Behavior pathway without prompt injection.

Social credibility signals—especially perceived consensus—dominate short-term behavioral manipulation, reaching up to 61% misleading rates even when authority cues are absent or web search is available.

Routine memory-saving escalates transient pollution into durable state with up to 91% of polluted items written into long-term memory and up to 76% cross-session behavioral influence, while naturalistic feed dilution reduces but does not eliminate persistence (still ~29% save rate and ~18% cross-session ASR at the strongest save setting).

nodes.title: Invisible Threats from Model Context Protocol: Generating Stealthy Injection Payload via Tree-based Adaptive Search
nodes.one_liner: Tree-guided, defense-aware black-box optimization can produce injections that look like normal tool output yet still override agent control, turning MCP’s trusted JSON responses into a high-leverage covert execution channel.
points: A stealthy MCP “supply-chain” update that injects semantically plausible instructions into mutable JSON response fields can reliably hijack tool-augmented agents without altering audited tool metadata.
The TIP black-box tree-search method reaches >95% attack success in undefended settings while using ~10× fewer queries than prior adaptive attacks (e.g., 100 vs 2580 queries on GetProduct).

Across four defense classes (instruction-structuring, sandwiching, finetuned detectors, and perplexity filtering), TIP retains >50% effectiveness overall and can still reach 90–100% ASR in several defended tasks (e.g., 100% under perplexity filtering for Product/Weather), indicating today’s detectors and prompt-structure defenses do not robustly protect MCP tool response trust boundaries.

nodes.title: LLMON: An LLM-native Markup Language to Leverage Structure and Semantics at the LLM Interface
nodes.one_liner: A small set of delimiter special tokens plus span-level semantics turns prompts into enforceable “control vs data” interfaces that measurably reduce distractor instruction failures.
points: Structuring prompts with explicit instruction/data spans and ID-referenced execution bindings raised multi-instruction robustness on a 100-item Distractor benchmark from near-zero (0.00–0.40) under conventional chat-template fine-tuning to 83.4–87.8% under LLMON full fine-tuning, an average +86.2 percentage-point gain.

Even without any LLMON training, enforcing inference-time attention masking using LLMON span boundaries improved Distractor accuracy from ~41–43% to ~69–72% across three instruction models, averaging a +29.3 percentage-point gain and indicating runtime-enforceable isolation can mitigate distractor/prompt-injection style interference.

LLMON-structured training data scaled to ~3.4M examples / 2.9B tokens and showed that parameter-efficient LoRA can learn structured execution (≈69.8–75.6% Distractor) but consistently lags full fine-tuning, suggesting deeper weight updates better internalize control/data separation semantics.

nodes.title: SoK: The Attack Surface of Agentic AI -- Tools, and Autonomy
nodes.one_liner: The key insight is that autonomy + tools turns classic prompt injection into a full software exploitation pathway, so measuring security with action-level metrics (e.g., Unsafe Action Rate and Privilege Escalation Distance) becomes as important as model alignment.
points: Tool-augmented LLM agents collapse untrusted text into executable side effects, with one cited study reporting 19 real RCE vulnerabilities across 11 LLM agent frameworks—making strict schema validation, sandboxing, and least-privilege tool design non-optional.

Indirect prompt injection is a practical end-to-end kill chain for agentic systems because attacker-controlled instructions can arrive via retrieved/web content and trigger later tool use, so deployments must treat all retrieved context as hostile and gate high-risk actions on provenance and risk scoring.

RAG and long-term memory expand the attack surface beyond prompt jailbreaking by enabling delayed, persistent integrity failures (index/memory poisoning) and cross-session goal hijacking, implying that corpus change control, ACL-aware retrieval, and tamper-evident audit logs are core security controls rather than optional hardening.

nodes.title: Prompt Attack Detection with LLM-as-a-Judge and Mixture-of-Models
nodes.one_liner: Carefully constrained judge prompting can make fast, general-purpose LLMs viable as production-grade prompt-attack detectors, while naïve LLM ensembling often hurts more than it helps.
points: A structured LLM-as-a-judge design (intent decomposition, safety-signal verification, harm assessment, and mandatory self-reflection) enables lightweight general-purpose models to achieve strong real-time prompt-attack detection, with gemini-2.0-flash-lite-001 reaching 0.844 F1 at ~1.52s latency and already operating as a centralized production guardrail for Singapore public-service chatbots.

On a 929-sample dataset mixing real benign chatbot traffic (82.9%, including false-positive traps) with automated-red-teamed adversarial prompts (17.1%), LLM judges substantially outperform encoder classifiers (best F1 0.3549) and proprietary guardrails (F1 0.1198), while top single-judge performance reaches 0.8711 F1 with very high precision (0.9766) using gpt-5.1.

Mixture-of-Models ensembling yields only modest and non-monotonic gains—best pair gpt-5.1+gpt-5-mini reaches 0.8964 F1 (+0.0164 over the best constituent), but most triples (8/10) and all quads degrade versus their strongest sub-combination, implying ensembles require careful complementarity rather than “more judges.”

nodes.title: Evolving Jailbreaks: Automated Multi-Objective Long-Tail Attacks on Large Language Models
affiliations: Southern University of Science and Technology
nodes.one_liner: Automatically evolving reversible encryption/decryption prompt programs yields a diverse Pareto front of long-tail jailbreaks that generalize better than fixed handcrafted obfuscation schemes.
points: EvoJail’s multi-objective evolutionary search (optimizing jailbreak success rate and minimizing perplexity) achieved the best hypervolume in 15/18 (83%) tested scenarios across Llama-2-7B, Llama-3.1-8B, and GPT-4.1-Nano, indicating more consistent safety-boundary coverage than six prior long-tail jailbreak baselines.

On Llama-3.1-8B, EvoJail repeatedly exceeded HV 0.70 (peaking at 0.770), showing it can maintain strong attack effectiveness while keeping outputs fluent/low-perplexity—an important stealth factor for web-deployed models that gate on obvious refusal or incoherence.

In ensemble transfer tests on unseen prompts, EvoJail delivered the top HV on 5/6 (83%) model-dataset settings (e.g., 0.762 on LLaMA-7B-G and 0.790 on LLaMA-8B-G), demonstrating that a diverse Pareto front of encryption/decryption strategies improves robustness beyond any single handcrafted long-tail technique.

nodes.title: Structured Visual Narratives Undermine Safety Alignment in Multimodal Large Language Models
affiliations: Singapore University of Technology and Design
nodes.one_liner: Minimal structured visual narratives (comics) create a high-success jailbreak channel for MLLMs while simultaneously exposing that common defenses and evaluators overfire on benign sensitive content.
points: A three-panel comic “complete the comic” prompt reliably bypasses multimodal safety guardrails, with ensemble attack success rates exceeding 90% on multiple commercial models and typically >85% on open-source models despite low success for plain-text or random-image text overlay baselines.

Prompt-based defenses (AdaShield and Attack-as-Defense) can sharply cut comic-jailbreak success (e.g., >80% EASR drop on Qwen3-VL 235B variants) but often cause severe over-refusal on benign requests, with several models seeing >80% ERR increases that materially reduce assistant usefulness.

Automated safety judges are asymmetric and brittle on benign-but-sensitive content: majority-vote judging shows FPR/FNR of 0.100/0.183 on harmful prompts but degrades to 0.234/0.422 on benign prompts, and in a 1,435-response benign subset only 3.1% were actually harmful while judges flagged 352 responses as harmful.

nodes.title: Not All Tokens Are Created Equal: Query-Efficient Jailbreak Fuzzing for LLMs
nodes.one_liner: The core trick is that a small set of refusal-critical tokens transfers across models well enough that a local surrogate can “triage” where to mutate, dramatically cutting black-box jailbreak queries.
points: Token contributions to LLM refusal are highly skewed (sparse “sensitive regions” dominate), and exploiting this structure enables substantially more query-efficient jailbreak search than uniform prompt mutation.

Refusal tendencies show strong cross-model consistency—pairwise Spearman correlations at an intermediate “reference layer” are often ~0.90 and refusal-semantic signatures have cosine similarities commonly ≥0.9—supporting surrogate-guided localization for black-box targets.

A token-aware fuzzing pipeline reaches 90% attack success rate with >70% fewer queries than prior baselines and still improves ASR by ~20–40% under an extreme 25-query budget (e.g., 84% ASR on GPT-4o and 94% on GPT-3.5 at 25 queries).

nodes.title: Analysing the Safety Pitfalls of Steering Vectors
nodes.one_liner: Activation steering is a latent-space safety bypass: small linear shifts that barely affect capability can reliably collapse refusal under simple jailbreak templates, especially in larger models.
points: Across six open-source LLMs (3B–32B) evaluated on JailbreakBench, Contrastive Activation Addition (CAA) steering consistently perturbs jailbreak Attack Success Rate (ASR), with changes reaching +57 percentage points or −50 percentage points depending on the steered behavior and attack template.

Under prefix-injection jailbreaks, a steering vector’s cosine similarity with a model’s refusal direction predicts its safety impact with very high fidelity for models ≥7B (R² ≥ 0.85 and p < 0.001), implying most steering-driven safety erosion is governed by simple geometric overlap rather than behavior semantics.

Projecting steering vectors to remove their refusal-aligned component reduces the average magnitude of ASR shifts by roughly 15–25% for most models (e.g., 24.63% for Llama-7B and ~19–20% for Qwen 7B–32B), but does not fully restore baseline safety, indicating additional multi-dimensional or downstream-regenerated safety directions.

nodes.title: SafeSeek: Universal Attribution of Safety Circuits in Language Models
affiliations: University of Science and Technology of China
Intelligent Science & Technology Academy of CASIC
nodes.one_liner: Both backdoors and safety alignment appear to be governed by tiny, discoverable circuits that can be selectively removed or tuned with minimal impact on general performance—making safety both more controllable and more fragile.
points: Ablating an extremely sparse backdoor circuit (as little as 0.42% of units) reduced backdoor Attack Success Rate from 100% to 0.4% while preserving over 99% of general utility, indicating backdoor behavior is localized and largely orthogonal to core capabilities.

Removing a small alignment circuit (3.03% attention heads and 0.79% neurons) caused safety to collapse, with jailbreak ASR jumping from 0.8% to 96.9% while retaining ~92.1% general capabilities, implying safety barriers are implemented by compact, specialized subnetworks rather than being diffuse.

Circuit-only fine-tuning maintained 96.5% safety retention during helpfulness optimization while updating ~1% of parameters, demonstrating a practical path to reduce the alignment tax and efficiently patch safety-relevant mechanisms without broad model changes.

nodes.title: The Workload-Router-Pool Architecture for LLM Inference Optimization: A Vision Paper from the vLLM Semantic Router Project
nodes.one_liner: The most interesting takeaway is that inference efficiency and safety are treated as a coupled control problem across workload mix, routing policy, and GPU/KV-cache topology rather than isolated optimizations.
points: A three-axis Workload–Router–Pool (WRP) decomposition is proposed for production LLM inference, arguing that co-optimizing routing policy and pool topology yields measurable wins such as 3.1–6.4% lower cost versus retrofitting compression onto a fixed fleet and up to 40× swings in energy efficiency depending on served context window.

Token-budget-aware pool routing is reported to reduce GPU instances by 17–39% on production traces, while a two-pool fleet design can deliver 6–82% lower cost than homogeneous fleets, implying that context-length partitioning is a first-order lever for both capacity planning and unit economics.

For agentic and multimodal workloads, tiered routing/cascading is positioned as a major cost reducer (e.g., adaptive VLM routing projecting up to 78% cost reduction within 2 percentage points of an all-large-model baseline) and the paper frames safety as a cross-cutting router function via hierarchical gating (binary safe/unsafe plus 9-class hazard categorization) to enable per-category policy enforcement.

nodes.title: TreeTeaming: Autonomous Red-Teaming of Vision-Language Models via Hierarchical Strategy Exploration
nodes.one_liner: Hierarchical exploration turns VLM red-teaming from template-tuning into strategy discovery that is simultaneously higher-ASR, more diverse, and harder to filter due to lower-toxicity, more transferable attacks.
points: A hierarchical strategy-tree orchestrator paired with a tool-using multimodal actuator achieves state-of-the-art black-box jailbreak performance on 11/12 evaluated VLMs, averaging 89.48% ASR and reaching 87.60% on GPT-4o (61.60% on Claude-3.5).

Compared to a flat strategy library ablation, the hierarchical tree materially improves both effectiveness and breadth (e.g., on GPT-4o ASR rises from 71.80% to 87.60% and kNN-entropy from 2.538 to 2.705), indicating structured exploration reduces strategy collapse and surfaces genuinely distinct attack families.

Generated attacks are both more diverse and stealthier than prior methods, with the highest reported visual diversity (0.641) while keeping toxicity low (6.63% textual, 9.86% visual) and reporting an average toxicity reduction of 23.09%, implying safety bypasses need not be overtly toxic to be effective.

nodes.title: Causal Direct Preference Optimization for Distributionally Robust Generative Recommendation
nodes.one_liner: Preference alignment can worsen recommendation robustness by reinforcing confounders, and a lightweight causal-invariance constraint is enough to recover strong OOD performance gains.
points: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) can systematically amplify spurious correlations from latent environmental confounders (e.g., popularity/temporal drift), with the resulting OOD generalization error bounded by the train–test mismatch in p(E|x) scaled by the learned confounder weight |w_E|.

CausalDPO replaces purely correlational preference alignment with a causal backdoor-adjusted objective by inferring latent environments via DBSCAN+soft assignments and enforcing cross-environment invariance with an MMD regularizer, yielding policies that are both preference-discriminative and environment-invariant as MMD→0.

Across three benchmarks and four distribution-shift settings, CausalDPO reports an average +17.17% improvement over baselines on HR/NDCG-style metrics while adding ~19.70% per-epoch training time versus standard DPO (2971s vs 2482s).

nodes.title: Robust Safety Monitoring of Language Models via Activation Watermarking
affiliations: Mohamed Bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence
nodes.one_liner: A keyed, activation-level watermark turns safety monitoring into a game the attacker can’t reliably optimize against without the secret, substantially improving robustness to adaptive jailbreaks with minimal runtime cost.
points: Activation watermarking embeds a secret-keyed signature into internal activations for policy-violating outputs and achieves the best detection under adaptive jailbreaks, reaching AUROC 0.905–0.962 across four attack suites while guard baselines degrade sharply (e.g., AutoDAN AUROC 0.905 vs 0.785 for QwenGuard and 0.409 for LlamaGuard).

At a stringent 1% false-positive rate, adaptive attackers evade existing monitors at high rates (e.g., AutoDAN ASR 0.8988 LlamaGuard and 0.8750 QwenGuard), whereas activation watermarking substantially lowers evasion (ASR 0.6786) and is reported to outperform guard baselines by up to 52% when the attacker knows the algorithm but not the secret key.

Watermarking introduces a concentrated utility tax primarily on multi-step math (GSM8K −7.1 pp, MATH-Hard −2.6 pp) while leaving other benchmarks nearly unchanged (≤2 pp absolute change on BBH, IFEval, MMLU-pro, TruthfulQA), enabling single-pass monitoring with negligible inference overhead compared to guard-model double forward passes.

nodes.title: The System Prompt Is the Attack Surface: How LLM Agent Configuration Shapes Security and Creates Exploitable Vulnerabilities
nodes.one_liner: System prompts act like a tunable security policy: optimizing for one strong heuristic can simultaneously deliver near-production metrics and create a predictable, attacker-controllable failure mode.
points: Across 220,000 evaluations (11 models × 10 system-prompt strategies), the same model’s phishing bypass rate ranged from <1% to 97% purely due to system prompt configuration, showing configuration can dominate model choice for agent security.

Signal-anchored prompts achieved up to 93.7% recall at 3.8% false positive rate and transferred cross-model without modification, but the exact same domain-consistency heuristic collapsed under infrastructure phishing by 41–46 percentage points on average (and up to ~69pp on some models) when attackers registered matching sender/URL domains.

Deployability is sharply constrained once false positives are penalized: Safetility analysis left only 4 of 110 model–prompt configurations as attractive under a 10% FPR threshold, and an infrastructure-aware prompt recovered infra recall for most models but imposed wildly varying FPR costs (as low as +2.6pp on Gemini 2.5 Flash vs. +65pp on Claude Haiku).

nodes.title: Beyond Content Safety: Real-Time Monitoring for Reasoning Vulnerabilities in Large Language Models
affiliations: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
nodes.one_liner: Real-time, step-wise monitoring of chain-of-thought can reliably catch and label reasoning hijacks and reasoning-DoS behaviors that output-focused safety checks largely overlook.
points: A nine-type “reasoning safety” taxonomy shows unsafe chain-of-thought failures are common and mechanistically distinct, with natural math reasoning dominated by logical fallacies (34.6%) and calculation errors (25.2%) while process-management errors are rare (≤1.7%), making loops/drift strong indicators of adversarial interference.

Different reasoning attacks produce highly concentrated error signatures—e.g., BadChain triggers goal deviation in 60.8% of annotated errors, OverThink causes misinterpretation in 74.5%, and Deadlock induces reasoning loops in 69.6%—enabling practical attack attribution from step-level traces.

A parallel, LLM-based real-time monitor localizes the first unsafe step with up to 84.88% accuracy and classifies error type with up to 85.37%, outperforming hallucination detection (44.36% localization) and a process reward model baseline (68.83% localization), indicating content safety tools systematically miss reasoning-level compromise.

nodes.title: ACRFence: Preventing Semantic Rollback Attacks in Agent Checkpoint-Restore
nodes.one_liner: Rewinding an agent is not a harmless retry—LLM nondeterminism turns checkpoint-restore into an exactly-once semantics failure at the tool boundary that attackers can weaponize for replay and privilege resurrection.
points: Checkpoint-restore in LLM agents creates “semantic rollback attacks” because post-restore tool calls are re-synthesized (often with new UUIDs/timestamps), enabling irreversible side effects like duplicate payments or repeated commits to be accepted as fresh requests.

In a proof-of-concept using Claude Code CLI (Qwen3-32B) with MCP tool servers, checkpoint-restore caused duplicate commits in 10/10 trials (100%) while the no-checkpoint baseline produced 0/10 duplicates, isolating restore as the direct trigger rather than general LLM instability.

Authority Resurrection shows rollback can re-enable previously consumed single-use approval tokens, with stateless token validation allowing reuse in 2/2 attempts (100%) and stateful revocation rejecting all attempts, implying defenses must bind authorization consumption to durable server-side state.

nodes.title: CIPL: A Target-Independent Framework for Channel-Inversion Privacy Leakage in Agents
nodes.one_liner: Treating agent privacy as a channel-inversion problem reveals that intermediate artifacts (retrieval evidence, tool args/returns) can leak sensitive units as reliably as memory, depending on how pipelines route internal state into observable outputs.
points: Under a unified 30-query black-box attack budget, memory-based agents exhibited near-total privacy compromise with CER/AER = 1.0/1.0 on memory_rap across all tested providers and on memory_ehr for 2/3 providers (qwen3.5-plus drops to CER 0.7467 and AER 0.7533), showing that “retrieved ⇒ leaked” is a common failure mode when sensitive memory is routed to user-visible channels.

Retrieval-augmented generation leaked frequently but rarely completely, with rag_toy showing high Any Extracted Rate (AER 0.80–0.9533) but zero or low Complete Extraction Rate (CER 0–0.2133), indicating that partial evidence disclosure is a pervasive risk even when verbatim dumping defenses appear to hold.

Tool-mediated channels were both invertible in principle (deterministic CER/AER = 1.0/1.0) and highly provider-dependent in realistic generation, ranging from low leakage on qwen3.5-plus (AER 0.14–0.40) to near-saturation on DeepSeek (AER ≈ 1.0; CER 0.9933–1.0), and weak-prompt controls collapsed leakage to ~0–6.7% AER, implicating prompt-driven channel inversion rather than benign task completion.

nodes.title: T-MAP: Red-Teaming LLM Agents with Trajectory-aware Evolutionary Search
nodes.one_liner: It maps and optimizes agentic attacks based on real tool-call trajectories—so the discovered prompts don’t just elicit harmful text, they reliably execute harmful actions across tools and even across multi-server chains.
points: Across five MCP tool environments (CodeExecutor, Slack, Gmail, Playwright, Filesystem), T-MAP achieves a 57.8% average attack realization rate (L3) with a 12.5% refusal rate, outperforming the strongest baseline (standard evolution) at 32.5% ARR and 23.1% RR, showing that trajectory-level feedback is decisive for turning jailbreaks into executed harms.

Trajectory-aware search scales to harder multi-server tool chains, with 46.28% of T-MAP’s unique successful trajectories spanning multiple MCP servers versus 14–23% for baselines, implying that real-world agent deployments that combine tools materially expand the exploitable attack surface.

Ablations show the Tool Call Graph primarily prevents tool-execution failures (L1 rises from 10.95% to 20.13% and L3 drops from 58.40% to 45.71% without it) while cross-diagnosis primarily reduces refusals (L0 rises from 11.93% to 15.63% without it), indicating complementary levers for defenders: constrain unsafe tool transitions and harden prompt-level policy enforcement.

nodes.title: BioShield: A Context-Aware Firewall for Securing Bio-LLMs
nodes.one_liner: The notable insight is that multi-turn biological misuse can be mitigated more effectively by treating the conversation as the unit of risk (with rewrite-and-verify loops) rather than filtering isolated prompts.
points: A trajectory-aware firewall design that scores each prompt using current harmfulness (1–5), aggregated historical harmfulness, and an intent flag (0/1) targets the key failure mode of single-turn moderation: multi-turn “benign” steering that cumulatively yields policy-violating bio guidance.

Adding context-aware pre-generation scanning dropped multi-turn jailbreak attack success rate (ASR) to 22.7% under a fixed attack protocol, indicating substantial but incomplete risk reduction that still leaves roughly 1 in 5 attack conversations succeeding.

A dual-layer control loop—prompt rewriting when risk exceeds a threshold and post-generation response verification plus bounded iterative sanitization/refusal—shifts safety enforcement to the application layer, improving auditability and domain-specific governance for Bio-LLM deployments.

nodes.title: Memory poisoning and secure multi-agent systems
nodes.one_liner: The key insight is that the hardest memory poisoning threats come from long-running agent-to-agent interactions and memory consolidation, where classic static-database poisoning models break down and cryptographic integrity plus privacy primitives become central.
