Ablations show the key capability is representing trust formation across interaction, not inspecting final parameters alone: parameter-only modeling yields 0.0 GUARDEDJOINT (RNR 89.0%) and removing trajectory-state scoring drops performance (e.g., 62.6→42.1 on Llama-3.1-8B), implying defenses must condition execution on interaction history rather than static tool descriptors or one-step checks.

nodes.title: Compile-time Security Analysis and Optimization of Sensitive String Producers
nodes.one_liner: Compiler-driven contextual escaping that is specified as library semantics, surfaced as compile-time diagnostics, and optimized down to near-concatenation performance is a practical route to making injection-safe code the default for both humans and AI code generators.
points: Additive tagged quoted-string syntax plus an accumulator/collector split enables contextual auto-escaping across multiple nested content languages (HTML/CSS/JS/URL) while keeping secure and insecure idioms lexically close, reducing the chance developers (or code generators) fall back to unsafe concatenation.

A pure, left-to-right automata specification (transition tables with optional substitutions and diagnostics) lets compilers lift runtime escaping logic into a flow-sensitive compile-time analysis that emits source-positioned errors/warnings, including conservative fail-stop behavior when control-flow merges cannot be safely resolved.

After compile-time context propagation, accumulator erasure rewrites each interpolation into a statically determined escaper chain and injects needed delimiters (e.g., quotes via epsilon-transition substitutions), yielding runtime costs that approach naïve concatenation with the primary remaining overhead being the irreducible escaping transforms.

nodes.title: GRID: Graph Representation of Intelligence Data for Security Text Knowledge Graph Construction
nodes.one_liner: Turning open-ended CTI document-to-knowledge-graph extraction into cheap, reusable edge-auditable training tasks yields higher recall and similar top-line F1 at a fraction of the token and reward-computation cost.
points: A 4B Qwen3-based CTI graph extractor trained with an offline task-bank reward plus ontology-guided inference achieved 84.62% average precision, 64.91% average recall, and 68.53% average F1 across 249 CTI articles from five datasets, delivering the highest recall among compared systems while staying essentially tied for best F1.

Replacing online LLM-as-judge full-graph rewards with scripted multi-select questions plus triple-level regex matching improved average F1 from 58.06% to 68.53% and recall from 53.85% to 64.91%, indicating reward shaping at the edge level is a major lever for reducing hallucinations and missed relations in long-form security text extraction.

The offline task-bank supervision was reported to cost about $60 to build once (vs. about $942 for online judge-based RL reward in the compared setup) and enabled a two-prompt inference pipeline using roughly 40% of the inference tokens of a similarly strong multi-step baseline, tightening the practicality gap for deploying small-model CTI knowledge-graph extraction.

nodes.title: Explainable Machine Learning for Phishing Detection on Heterogeneous Datasets with MCP-Enabled Deployment
nodes.one_liner: Combining heterogeneous phishing sources with XAI and an MCP-style deployment layer highlights both the upside of transformers (near-perfect URL classification) and the evaluation pitfall that easily separable synthetic data can make models look 'solved' when they are not.
points: Across heterogeneous phishing URL datasets, transformer models dominate performance with DistilBERT reaching 99.78% accuracy and ~1.00 ROC-AUC, far exceeding classical ML (best 92.44% with Logistic Regression) and slightly outperforming top ensembles (95.01% with CatBoost).

Realistic tool-generated phishing (EvilGinx/Zphisher) is materially harder for simple linear models (≈92% accuracy on EvilGinx) while boosted-tree ensembles remain more stable (CatBoost ≈97.96% accuracy and 0.9976 AUC on EvilGinx), implying non-linear feature interactions are important under distribution shift.

Explainability reveals dataset-dependent decision drivers (e.g., SSL state, prefix/suffix, web traffic, subdomain/IP indicators) and shows near-zero per-feature SHAP magnitude on the GenAI dataset alongside ~100% accuracy, signaling that synthetic GenAI URLs may be overly separable and risk overstating real-world robustness.

nodes.title: Measuring Real-World Prompt Injection Attacks in LLM-based Resume Screening
affiliations: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
nodes.one_liner: Prompt injection in the wild mostly looks like invisible keyword/credential stuffing in PDFs—not dramatic 'ignore previous instructions' jailbreaks—so document-aware, cross-modal defenses matter more than text-only filters.
points: Hidden prompt injection appears in ~1% of real resumes at scale (2,030/196,682), demonstrating that indirect prompt injection is a measurable, non-anecdotal risk in high-stakes LLM workflows.
Data-only injections dominate in practice (90.5%–95.7% of malicious resumes), implying defenses that key on explicit instruction patterns will miss the majority of real attacks.

Detection rates spiked to ~1.2% in 2024 before declining (to ~0.67% by 2025-H2 in the long-horizon dataset), yet absolute malicious counts keep rising with submission volume, increasing operational moderation burden even when prevalence drops.

nodes.title: Cordyceps: Covert Control Attacks on LLMs via Data Poisoning
nodes.one_liner: Data poisoning can teach LLMs a steganography-like semantic protocol that turns innocuous-looking facts into a high-success control and exfiltration channel that current trigger- and substring-centric defenses largely miss.
points: A 10% poisoned fine-tuning fraction reliably implants a semantic encode/decode covert channel (SHuSh) that achieves ~0.71–0.74 UCC prompt-injection success (ASV_h) across five LLMs, outperforming a strong heuristic prompt-injection baseline by up to ~80% relative (e.g., 0.712 vs 0.392 on Llama3-8B).

Covert control remains highly effective after representative mitigations, sustaining up to 93% attack success after backdoor defenses and up to 98% after prompt-injection defenses, while data-level and online detectors largely fail (e.g., ONION TPR 0.063 at 5% FPR and PromptGuard flags UCC at ~0.2–0.5%, near covertext rates).

Bidirectional covert data exfiltration succeeds under a keyword filter with 0.78–0.93 covert accuracy (and stays 0.75–0.92 even at a 1% poison rate with enough absolute poisoned examples), yet preserves general model utility within roughly ±1.5% across MuSR, GSM8K, MMLU, IFEval, and GPQA.

nodes.title: Prompt Injection Detection is Regime-Dependent: A Deployment-Aware Evaluation with Interpretable Structural Signals
nodes.one_liner: Prompt-injection detectors that look strong on benchmarks can fail under realistic low-false-positive budgets, and adding interpretable instruction-boundary structure helps mainly in specific regimes rather than everywhere.
points: Detection effectiveness varies sharply by deployment regime: DeBERTa leads on broad OOD harmful-vs-benign with Macro-F1 0.7528±0.0417, TF‑IDF unexpectedly tops the hard-negative injection regime with Macro-F1 0.8082±0.0563, and no single detector dominates across all settings.

Ranking metrics can materially overstate deployability because operating-point choice is decisive: on the primary OOD regime RoBERTa+IBVS attains higher low-FPR sensitivity than DeBERTa (TPR@1% FPR 0.4557±0.0257 vs 0.3368±0.0040) despite slightly lower Macro-F1 (0.7497±0.0297 vs 0.7528±0.0417).

Interpretable structural signals (IBVS) provide selective, consistent gains—especially improving low-FPR behavior via late fusion (e.g., primary OOD TPR@1% rises from 0.4358±0.0438 with RoBERTa to 0.4557±0.0257 with RoBERTa+IBVS)—but they are complementary rather than a universal replacement for strong encoders.

nodes.title: LLM-as-a-Reviewer: Benchmarking Their Ability, Divergence, and Prompt Injection Resistance as Paper Reviewers
affiliations: Missouri University of Science and Technology
nodes.one_liner: LLMs can look helpful as reviewer assistants, but their scoring calibration and hidden-prompt robustness vary so much by model family that unguarded deployment can measurably distort acceptance decisions.
points: Across 898 NeurIPS/ICLR papers and 12 long-context LLMs, most models inflated scores versus humans by roughly +1 to +2.4 points (and confidence by ~+0.3 to +1.2), while newer GPT-5 variants were notably more conservative (often negative score gaps) despite still showing confidence inflation.

LLM critique focus systematically diverged from humans, under-flagging Clarity as a weakness by about 14–20 percentage points while over-flagging Reproducibility (e.g., code/data availability) by up to ~+13 percentage points, implying automated reviews can shift what gets “rewarded” or “penalized” in peer review.

Invisible font-mapping prompt injection remained highly effective, promoting ~65–76% of originally sub-acceptance papers to ≥8 for several model families (e.g., Gemini and GPT-4o-mini) while GPT-5 models showed much lower promotion (≈0–8%), indicating strong model-dependent integrity risk and the need for document sanitization and instruction-isolation defenses.

nodes.title: Localization then Neutralization: Gradient-guided Token Suppression against Visual Prompt Injection Attack
affiliations: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences (UCAS)
Institute of Computing Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS)
State Key Laboratory of AI Safety, CAS
nodes.one_liner: A lightweight, theory-backed defense shows that visual prompt injection often hinges on a tiny subset of image tokens—and masking just those tokens can nearly eliminate attacks without sacrificing normal capability.
points: Gradient-guided Token Masking (GTM) suppresses perturbation-based visual prompt injection by identifying a sparse set of high-influence image tokens via a single forward–backward pass and masking ~5% of tokens to drive attack success rates down to 0–0.91% across multiple prompt-injection and multimodal jailbreak settings while keeping MM-Vet utility essentially unchanged.

Probability-gradient attribution on the first generated token can fail under token-alignment (when the adversarial and clean first token match), where log-probability gradients become non-discriminative, but a hidden-state gradient-norm score preserves token-importance ranking up to small error and improves aligned-case defense (e.g., reducing ASR from 30.91% with logit-based attribution to 24.55%).

Compared with heavier defenses, GTM delivers near-DiffPure-level robustness without requiring auxiliary models or retraining and with minimal overhead (3.50s vs 3.28s baseline inference time and 16.96 GiB GPU memory), whereas diffusion purification can severely degrade OCR/text-rich image performance despite achieving 0% ASR.

nodes.title: Can It Reach the Generator? Investigating the Survival of Prompt-Injection Attacks in Realistic RAG Settings
nodes.one_liner: Most “high-success” GEO prompt injections don’t actually reach the generator once retrieval and reranking are modeled, and the few that do are surprisingly easy to learn-and-detect with a small finetuned guard.
points: Evaluating seven generative-engine-optimization prompt-injection methods in a realistic retriever→LLM-reranker→LLM-generator pipeline shows upstream stages are decisive, with retrieval alone filtering out ~20% of attacked documents on average (Sr@10 ≈79.9% at rank-10) before they can influence generation.

End-to-end effectiveness is far lower and highly method-dependent than frozen-context testing suggests: gradient-based attacks collapse to <2% generator success (Sg@3), instruction-override drops to ~14.7%, while only LLM-driven prompt-optimization attacks remain reliably effective (CORE-reason ≈53.5%, CORE-review ≈46.7%, TAP ≈40.2% Sg@3 at rank-10).

Defense-wise, off-the-shelf guards fail to generalize across attack families, but a lightweight Prompt-Guard finetuned on a small dataset reaches near-perfect detection (97.3% F1) and catches every evaluated attack, implying survivable GEO injections exhibit learnable surface patterns that can be operationally blocked.

nodes.title: Mitigating Adaptive Attacks against Reasoning Models with Activation Consistency Training
nodes.one_liner: Supervising internal activations at the assistant-turn boundary can outperform output-level safety fine-tuning on reasoning models, stay robust to adaptive attackers, and yields an interpretable refusal “steering vector.”
points: Activation Consistency Training (ACT) generally reduces both prompt-injection and jailbreak attack success to near-zero under static and RL-trained adaptive attacks on most tested reasoning models while requiring only self-supervised (clean, wrapped) prompt pairs rather than supervising long chain-of-thought traces.

Under adaptive prompt-injection, undefended models are broken ~90–100% of the time, while ACT lowers best-of-8 ASR to 37–63% on several targets (e.g., GPT-OSS-20B: 100%→37%; Qwen3-8B: 96%→59%), though some models remain difficult (Gemma-4-E4B-it stays ~100% ASR).

Mechanistically, ACT appears to encode refusal as an approximately linear activation shift at the assistant-turn boundary, enabling recovery of a single steering direction that can induce refusal in the base model and undo refusal in the defended model, and ACT still refuses even when given a compliant chain-of-thought prefill that causes output-level training (BCT) to often comply.

nodes.title: How Agentic AI Coding Assistants Become the Attacker's Shell
affiliations: Huazhong University of Science and Technology
nodes.one_liner: Agentic coding editors collapse trusted instructions and untrusted artifacts into one token stream, so a few hidden lines in “helpful” files can reliably turn the assistant into a privileged command-execution proxy.
points: At-scale evaluation with 314 prompt-injection payloads (70 MITRE ATT&CK techniques) on Cursor v1.2.2 and GitHub Copilot v1.102 across five real-world codebases found 41%–84% attack success, indicating a systemic architectural weakness rather than a tool- or language-specific bug.

Successful injections translate untrusted text into real system actions—credential discovery (AWS/SSH), exfiltration, account creation, auth-config modification, and cron-based persistence—meaning agentic coding assistants can enable an end-to-end compromise chain, not just harmful text output.

Ecosystem exposure is already measurable in the supply chain: a scan of 3,984 community “agent skills” reported 13.4% with critical security issues and 91% of confirmed malicious skills combining prompt injection with traditional malware, making unvetted artifacts a high-risk default input channel.

nodes.title: Token-Level Generalization in LoRA Adapter Backdoors: Attack Characterization and Behavioral Detection
nodes.one_liner: Backdooring LoRA adapters can saturate at single-digit poison rates, but the most reliable defense is behavioral scanning that does not assume a “structured trigger” class—and weight-only signatures need per-base-model calibration.
points: A rank-16 LoRA adapter on Qwen 2.5 1.5B can be clean-accuracy-preserving backdoored with just 25 poisoned examples (4.2% of training), reaching 100% trigger success while maintaining ~95% clean accuracy, and the transition from chance to near-certainty occurs sharply around ~15–25 poisoned examples.

Trigger generalization is primarily token-neighborhood-driven rather than structure-driven: a backdoor trained on “per RFC 8472…” transfers to arbitrary RFC citations at ~96% mean success at saturation but only ~17% to structurally similar ISO/OWASP/CWE/NIST citations, creating a defender asymmetry because there is no generic probe for “structured citations.”

Two detection routes are effective in complementary ways: behavioral probing achieves AUC=1.000 when probes overlap the trigger neighborhood and still ~0.92 AUC with 83–87% recall at 0 false positives without overlap, while a weight-only LoRA-norm statistic can perfectly separate a calibrated cohort at 1.5B (AUC=1.000) but fails to transfer to Qwen 7B (AUC≈0.65), implying behavioral scanning is the most portable supply-chain defense.

nodes.title: Minimal Prompt Perturbations Lead to Code Vulnerabilities: Prompt Fragility and Hidden-State Signals in Coding LLMs
affiliations: Cyber-Defence Campus, armasuisse Science and Technology
nodes.one_liner: Benign-looking prompt noise can silently remove critical security guards, and the model’s own hidden states reveal which vulnerability classes are catchable pre-generation versus only during decoding.
points: Single-character prompt edits (e.g., typos) can flip LLM-generated code from secure to vulnerable across 3 coding models and 5 languages, expanding the threat model from adversarial prompt injection to routine prompt variation.

Hidden-state probing of the last prompt token predicts security outcomes only moderately well overall (≈0.70 mean held-out AUC), but is significantly better for input-handling flaws (mean AUC 0.753) than for secure-defaults flaws (mean AUC 0.674; Mann–Whitney p=0.009), implying different intervention points are needed.

Mutation impact concentrates around the middle of prompts (often the specification/docstring), and in many cases only a small subset of specific character/token substitutions at a position trigger security changes, indicating brittle prompt-to-code decision boundaries rather than uniformly noisy behavior.

nodes.title: MIRAGE: Context-Aware Prompt Injection against Mobile GUI Agents via User-Generated Content
nodes.one_liner: Mobile GUI agents that act from pixels can be hijacked by benign-looking user-generated text, and realism is a poor signal for filtering because high-plausibility injections can still redirect actions at double-digit success rates.
points: Across a 1,111-sample benchmark spanning 10 mobile apps and 11 attacker intents, all five evaluated VLM-based GUI agents were vulnerable to screenshot-only prompt injection via user-generated content, with 23.0%–30.2% attack success (peak 30.2% on GPT-4o-mini).

Attack success was driven far more by attacker intent than by model identity or visual polish, with intent success rates ranging from 6% to 88% (e.g., induce_wait at 62%–88%) and realism showing essentially no correlation with success (Spearman ρ = −0.03).

Injected screenshots were rated more realistic than the strongest prior baseline while retaining meaningful efficacy (overall human realism 3.02 vs 2.52 out of 5, p < 1e-4), implying that simple visual-quality filtering is not a reliable runtime defense and stronger mitigations must constrain action grounding or treat user-generated regions as untrusted.

nodes.title: Device Context Protocol: A Compact, Safety-First Architecture for LLM-Driven Control of Constrained Devices
nodes.one_liner: Protocol-level units, ranges, dry-run, and capability scoping—enforced by a single trust-boundary Bridge—deliver near-OpenAPI-grade call rejection on microcontroller footprints where HTTP/OpenAPI is infeasible.
points: DCP achieves MCU-scale deployment with a measured 27.6KB flash and 0.6KB RAM on ESP32 while using sub-50-byte typical frames (6-byte header + CBOR payload + optional 16-byte HMAC), enabling LLM tool control on devices that cannot fit MCP-class JSON-RPC stacks.

Across 675 real LLM-emitted tool calls from five models spanning four vendors under six adversarial prompt categories (including AgentDojo-style prompt injection), DCP rejected 100% of capability-escalation attempts and 78% of prompt-injection attempts at the host-side Bridge versus 0–1% rejection for Raw MCP and IoT-MCP.

An A/B latency benchmark on identical ESP32-S3 hardware shows DCP’s full validation and framing pipeline matches newline-delimited JSON in median round-trip time (15.60ms vs 15.59ms, within 5µs), indicating protocol-layer safety checks can be effectively “free” at typical serial-link scheduling floors.

nodes.title: Disentangling Adversarial Prompts: A Semantic-Graph Defense for Robust LLM Security
affiliations: Huazhong University of Science and Technology
nodes.one_liner: Combining mutual-information prompt disentanglement with Laplacian spectral features yields a low-latency, pre-generation defense that meaningfully cuts harmful outputs while staying robust to paraphrased and multilingual jailbreaks.
points: A pre-input defense that decomposes prompts with mutual-information minimization and flags malicious semantic-graph structure reaches 92.3% average adversarial detection accuracy across JailBreakBench, ToxicPrompts, and AdvPromptGen while keeping false positives at 3.7%.

Prompt sanitization guided by disentangled adversarial components reduces harmful output generation by 87.4% on average (86.8–88.2% across test sets), indicating that proactive input filtering can outperform reactive post-output moderation in preventing unsafe generations.

The end-to-end pipeline adds 12.3 ms per prompt (vs. 45.6 ms for post-output moderation) and ablations show the VAE module is the largest robustness contributor (ADA drops from 92.3% to 82.7% without it), suggesting real-time deployability without major safety-performance tradeoffs.

nodes.title: Poisoning the Watchtower: Prompt Injection Attacks Against LLM-Augmented Security Operations Through Adversarial Log Content
nodes.one_liner: SOC copilots that ingest raw logs inherit an attacker-controlled instruction channel where authority- and structure-mimicking injections can override triage and summaries even under “best-effort” prompt hardening.
points: Attacker-controlled log fields (e.g., URI, user-agent, payload, DNS query, usernames) can reliably carry prompt-injection instructions that manipulate SOC LLM outputs, with persona hijacks suppressing 68% of malicious events under naive classification prompting while direct overrides and base64-style obfuscation produce ~0% suppression.

Summarization is the highest-risk SOC task: context-manipulation payloads achieve 96% injection success with naive prompting and still succeed 38% of the time even when outputs are constrained, often by causing omission or downplaying of clear attack evidence rather than emitting an explicit “benign” label.

Common mitigations reduce but do not eliminate risk—mean injection success drops from 26.6% (naive) to 11.8% (strongest defense), and a literature-calibrated deterministic simulator poorly matches real model behavior (correlations r=0.22–0.47; MAE 0.22–0.30), implying defenses and testing must be validated on live models rather than mocks.

nodes.title: IterInject: Indirect Prompt Injection Against LLM Agents via Feedback-Guided Iterative Optimization
nodes.one_liner: The most striking result is that structured feedback plus history-conditioned optimization makes indirect prompt injection reliably adaptive, and the causal attention-layer interventions point to a practical, testable defense knob.
points: A feedback-guided iterative indirect prompt injection framework increases attack success over static and prior adaptive baselines, reaching 47.8% ASR on AgentDojo for DeepSeek-V4-Flash (vs 32.9% static) and lifting InjectAgent Total ASR from near-zero to 33–90% across four victim models.

On a production-grade coding agent (Claude Code with layered defenses), optimized payloads fully succeeded on 5/9 targets and improved the remainder to at least partial compromise in 3/4 cases, showing that iterative refinement can turn “ignored/detected” failures into measurable exploitation progress under realistic guardrails.

Mechanistic evidence links indirect prompt injection success to an attention “amplification zone” in mid-to-late layers (31–47) with a threshold-like tipping behavior, where attention knockout flips 32% of successes and attention steering induces up to 47% of failures to comply, implying inference-time attention suppression over untrusted tokens as a concrete defense lever.

nodes.title: Aligning Provenance with Authorization: A Dual-Graph Defense for LLM Agents
nodes.one_liner: Interesting because it treats injection-corrupted provenance as evidence and detects attacks by structurally comparing what the agent did against an injection-immune authorization baseline down to the parameter source.
points: Dual-graph alignment (clean authorization graph vs. injected execution provenance) cuts indirect prompt-injection attack success on AgentDojo from 40% to 1% on GPT-4o while keeping 76% task utility, indicating parameter-level provenance constraints can deliver strong security without crippling agent functionality.

On open-ended AgentDyn tasks, attack success drops from 39% to 2% on GPT-4o with 51% utility, outperforming stricter baselines that reach 0% ASR only by collapsing utility to near-zero, suggesting least-privilege replanning is key to usable safety in dynamic tool-use settings.

Ablations show layered enforcement matters: removing hard-blocking raises ASR from 1% to 12%, removing tool-name deviation checks raises ASR to 21% (largest regression), and removing parameter-source checks raises ASR to 18%, quantifying that both out-of-envelope tool calls and in-envelope parameter pollution are major real-world failure modes.

nodes.title: LACUNA: Safe Agents as Recursive Program Holes
nodes.one_liner: Turning agent actions into compiler-checked typed holes makes “the compiler” an enforcement point for agent safety—catching broken or unauthorized code before it can execute, while still supporting recursive, runtime-authored control flow.
points: A typed-hole agent primitive that compiles model-generated code at the call site rejects 8.6% of BrowseComp-Plus generations pre-execution (0.7 retries/query) while preserving atomicity—rejected snippets leave state untouched—so structural tool/mismatch failures never reach the environment.

On τ2-bench (392 tasks), the approach solves 76.0% with a capable model—on par with a native tool-calling baseline—yet still blocks ill-typed or out-of-scope actions, showing that static checking can be added without degrading end-task performance when the model can code reliably.

Capability tracking plus safe-mode restrictions largely contain prompt-injection damage by constraining effects and dataflow to the lexical scope, but real-world robustness remains imperfect (e.g., 4/560 successful attacks in one domain), implying least-privilege scoping is necessary but not sufficient for complete injection resistance.

nodes.title: Towards Demystifying and Repairing LLM-in-the-Loop Vulnerabilities
nodes.one_liner: LLM-specific vulnerabilities are both rarer than commonly assumed and substantially harder to patch correctly, especially when the exploit path depends on prompt semantics rather than deterministic code paths.
points: From 2,888 candidate issues across 230 popular LLM-related components, only 205 (≈7%) strictly require an LLM to participate as source/actuator/target, implying widespread mislabeling in existing vulnerability feeds and the need for LLM-aware triage criteria.

LLM-in-the-loop issues skew severe—83.5% were reported in 2024–2025 and 62.4% are High/Critical (39.0% High, 23.4% Critical)—with injection-heavy CWEs (e.g., CWE-94 code injection) dominating, signaling that natural-language-to-executable boundaries are the primary risk concentrator in LLM-integrated systems.

On 101 fully reproducible cases, agent-based repair drops from 71.8% Pass@1 on conventional benchmarks to 53.47% overall and to 28.57% on prompt-injection cases, with >70% of failures due to incomplete fixes or functionality damage, showing current “fix” strategies often trade security for broken behavior (or vice versa).

nodes.title: Steering Beyond the Support: Adversarial Training on Unsupervised Jailbroken Activation Simulation
nodes.one_liner: Unsupervised jailbreak-activation simulation + adversarial training learns a local safety vector field that generalizes beyond a fixed jailbreak dataset.
points: A bi-level adversarial training loop that synthesizes jailbroken activations via unsupervised latent-direction discovery drives attack success rates to mostly <5% across 3 aligned 7–8B models and 6 jailbreak families while avoiding reliance on labeled jailbreak prompts.

Compared to supervised safety steering, which shows large OOD fragility (e.g., unseen-family StrongReject scores staying high around ~29–32% in the provided AlphaSteer example), continual simulation expands the defended activation support and yields consistently lower HarmBench StrongReject (e.g., Safety score 4.01 on LLaMA-3-8B and 5.26 on Mistral-v2-7B).

The approach improves safety without simply “turning up refusal everywhere,” reducing over-refusal versus prior steering/representation baselines (e.g., OR-Bench FPR 16.6% on LLaMA-3-8B vs 24.4% for AlphaSteer) while largely preserving capabilities (e.g., LLaMA-3-8B ARC-C/TruthfulQA/GSM8K at 61.1/51.2/77.0 with utility 46.5).

nodes.title: Ellipsoid Control: A White-list Jailbreak Defense via Benign Latent Modeling
nodes.one_liner: A test-time, white-list jailbreak defense that steers any suspicious prompt toward refusal while explicitly preserving the benign latent manifold—dramatically improving robustness to embedding-space attacks without the typical over-refusal spike.
points: Ellipsoid Control replaces black-list harmful-sample fitting with per-input projected gradient descent constrained by a benign-data-fitted anisotropic ellipsoid, cutting SoftPrompt embedding-space jailbreak ASR from 80.7%→12.3% (Llama-3-8B), 98.3%→25.3% (Mistral-7B-v2), and 90.3%→20.0% (Qwen2.5-7B).

Across diverse jailbreak suites, Ellipsoid Control yields consistently low attack success rates while preserving utility, e.g., on Qwen2.5-7B it reduces DR/GCG/AutoDAN/PAIR ASR to 4.0%/6.0%/0.0%/13.3% while keeping MMLU and MT-Bench essentially unchanged at 79.4% and 8.7, respectively.

Modeling benign latent geometry globally (ellipsoid) rather than point-wise avoids exaggerated refusal: ORBench over-refusal drops from Circuit Breaker’s 48.4%→19.3% (Llama-3-8B) and 82.7%→14.5% (Mistral-7B-v2), and increasing benign fit data improves the safety–over-refusal Pareto tradeoff as effective-rank ratio rises and saturates around 100k–1M samples.

nodes.title: Evolving Skill-Structured Attack Memory Enhances LLM Jailbreaking
affiliations: The University of New South Wales
nodes.one_liner: Persistent, evidence-weighted “attack skills” with promotion/retirement and Thompson-sampling selection turn jailbreak prompting into a compounding, cost-reducing learning loop rather than per-goal trial-and-error.
points: MemoAttack’s skill-structured, lifecycle-managed attack memory reaches a 98.00% jailbreak attack success rate (147/150) on AdvBench across three black-box targets, beating the strongest baseline by 16.67 percentage points.

The framework improves query efficiency, cutting average successful-run requests by 45.9% (7.39 vs 13.66) while maintaining higher budgeted-ASR at every measured query budget, indicating faster exploitation of reusable attack tactics under sparse feedback.

Ablations show the largest performance drops come from removing memory machinery (-16.67 ASR points) or posterior-guided selection (-13.89), implying that statistically-tracked skill reuse (not just tree search) is the dominant driver of scalable jailbreak discovery.

nodes.title: Opir: Efficient Multi-Task Safety Classification for Toxicity, Jailbreaks, Hate Speech, and Harmful Content
nodes.one_liner: An encoder-only, multi-task guardrail closes the accuracy gap with top decoder guardrails while cutting 1024-token moderation latency from ~100–300 ms to ~9–26 ms and scaling to a 996-label safety taxonomy.
points: A DeBERTaV3-large encoder guardrail (Opir-multitask-large) reaches 0.8045 average macro-F1 across 12 binary safety datasets—within 0.0016 of the top system (0.8061)—showing encoder classifiers can match 7B–8B decoder guardrails on aggregate safety quality.

A 996-label, three-level safety taxonomy (16 top-level, 126 mid-level, 854 leaf labels) plus explicit safe/benign categories enables fine-grained moderation beyond binary routing, and the multi-task model leads 11/17 category-accuracy splits with a 0.5432 average.

Latency at 1024 tokens is dominated by architecture: the 32M-parameter edge encoder runs at 9.25 ms p50 (499.49 samples/s) versus 97.63 ms p50 for an 8B decoder guardrail and 243–309 ms p50 for other decoder baselines, enabling >10× lower guardrail overhead for real-time LLM pipelines.

nodes.title: Jailbreak to Protect: Buffering and Reinforcing via Temporary Jailbreaking for Safe Fine-Tuning in Large Language Models
nodes.one_liner: Intentionally (but temporarily) jailbreaking an LLM can make harmful fine-tuning updates effectively unlearnable, then safety can be re-injected with an orthogonal LoRA merge that preserves user-task performance.
points: Temporary jailbreaking acts as a gradient “buffer” in fine-tuning: safety-aligned models show negative Safety Gradient Scores (safety-degrading updates) even on harmless data, while jailbroken models drive these scores near zero on both harmful and harmless data, indicating saturation of safety-degrading gradients without losing benign learning capacity.

The Buffer-and-Reinforce framework achieves a near-flat safety profile under heavy dataset poisoning, holding Harmful Score at ~8–9% across harmful-data ratios p=0.0→1.0 (e.g., 8.1% at p=0.1 and 8.8% at p=1.0) while improving utility to 76–77% fine-tuning accuracy, versus SFT degrading to 75–81% Harmful Score as p increases.

Per-user deployment cost is essentially unchanged from standard SFT (3.93 min user fine-tune time and 39.11 GB peak memory), because the extra work is amortized into one-time adapter preparation (30.04 min) plus a small post-step (0.25 min), delivering materially better safety/utility than defenses that add heavy per-user overhead (e.g., AsFT at ~203 min user fine-tuning).

nodes.title: Open-Weight LLM Fine-Tuning Defenses are Susceptible to Simple Attacks
nodes.one_liner: Open-weight LLM safeguards built to withstand expensive fine-tuning can be bypassed by simple, no-training edits and prompt-structure tricks, and even an explicit anti-abliteration tuning objective only partially closes the gap.
points: Two cheap, gradient-free jailbreaks (activation/weight-space abliteration and static-response prefilling) raise harmful attack success rates against fine-tuning safeguards (TAR, SEAM) from typically <10% baseline to 16%–96% across BeaverTails, AdvBench, and HarmBench, showing that defenses focused on adversarial fine-tuning leave a large inference-time attack surface.

Abliteration is consistently the dominant bypass: on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct it drives ASR to 85%–97% for Base and 73%–93% for SEAM, while TAR remains more resistant but still reaches up to 62% ASR, implying meta-learning-style resistance helps but does not prevent extraction of pretrained harmful knowledge once refusal mechanisms are suppressed.

Abliteration-Resistant Tuning (ART) partially mitigates these attacks when layered onto existing safeguards, cutting abliteration ASR by ~10–20 points overall (e.g., on Gemma3-1B-IT Base 93%→49%, SEAM 80%→64%, TAR 76%→55%) but still leaves substantial residual risk, indicating that robustness requires evaluations beyond fine-tuning attacks and likely mechanisms that reduce underlying harmful capability rather than only refusal.

nodes.title: Reflect-Guard: Enhancing LLM Safeguards against Adversarial Prompts via Logical Self-Reflection
nodes.one_liner: Teaching a safety classifier to produce structured self-reflections shifts it from pattern matching toward intent reasoning, dramatically boosting adversarial recall with minimal fine-tuning cost.
points: A reflection-augmented Llama-Guard-3-8B trained with just 1,000 examples and ~0.5% (≈42M) updated parameters improved WildGuardTest F1 from 0.770 to 0.842 (+7.2 pp) by raising recall from 0.663 to 0.863 (+20.0 pp), implying small PEFT updates can materially harden moderation against evasive prompts.

On adversarially framed inputs, recall jumped from 0.513 to 0.921 (+40.8 pp), showing that generating an explicit intermediate analysis helps detect disguised intent that surface-pattern moderation often misses.

On JailbreakBench, overall attack success rate fell from 10.3% to 1.8% (82.5% relative reduction) and PAIR detection rose from 75.6% to 95.1%, indicating disproportionate gains against semantic/role-play jailbreaks rather than purely token-suffix attacks.

nodes.title: Beyond Attack Success Rate: Temporal Logit Observability for LLM Safety Failures
nodes.one_liner: ASR says whether a jailbreak happened, but TLO shows when and how it happened—often early enough to stop generation and cut successful jailbreaks by more than half without benign false alarms.
points: Temporal Logit Observability (TLO) converts per-token compliance–refusal logit margins into a training-free, calibrated 2D “relative position” plane that separates jailbreak conditions even when Attack Success Rate (ASR) is nearly identical (median RP-plane distance 0.572 for pairs within 5 ASR points).

Early decoding dynamics are strongly predictive of eventual safety failure—within the first five generated tokens, trajectory features reach ROC-AUC 0.771–0.784 and show large separation (Cohen’s d > 1.0), indicating many jailbreak outcomes are detectable long before the final response label used by ASR.

A simple TLO-derived early-stop rule (halt if refusal dominance does not appear by token 5) reduces aggregate ASR from 39.6% to 13.1% (−26.5 points) with 0.0% false positives on plain benign queries, demonstrating practical decode-time monitoring from logits alone.

nodes.title: Furina: Fragmented Uncertainty-Driven Refusal Instability Attack
affiliations: State Key Laboratory for Novel Software Technology
nodes.one_liner: Jailbreak success correlates less with any specific prompt trick and more with pushing models into a high-uncertainty regime where internal refusal signals weaken, making both refusals and detectors unreliable.
points: Safety refusals exhibit a measurable instability band where small rewrites shift compliance probability away from near-binary behavior (e.g., Qwen3-8B on AdvBench ASR rises 0.02→0.77 across rewrite strength), implying refusal is stochastic near the boundary rather than a crisp threshold.

Across text and multimodal models, successful jailbreak conditions share a consistent diagnostic signature—higher output uncertainty and attack success alongside lower internal safety activations (e.g., LLaVA-v1.5-7B image-text ASR 0.04→0.97 while HiddenDetect HDmax 0.034→0.001), which explains why detector-style defenses can miss the most dangerous near-boundary prompts.

The Furina fragmented, scene-anchored probing approach achieves high transferability without model-specific tuning, reaching 92.5–94.0% ASR on HarmBench across multiple LLM APIs and 91.9–93.8% ASR on MM-SafetyBench on several MLLMs, showing that distributing intent across benign-looking fragments plus synthesis can reliably bypass single-turn and many multi-turn baselines.

nodes.title: RouteScan: A Non-Intrusive Approach to Auditing MoE LLMs Safety via Expert Routing Telemetry
nodes.one_liner: Safety auditing can be moved from content inspection to hardware-level MoE routing footprints, reducing prompt exposure while still catching cross-domain jailbreaks.
points: RouteScan detects harmful and jailbreak prompts in MoE LLM services using only prefilling-phase GPU thread telemetry (active threads per expert) rather than prompts/outputs, reaching AUROC >0.93 on unseen harmful domains and up to 0.96+ on novel jailbreak wrappers across two MoE models and two NVIDIA GPU types.

The approach exploits MoE routing as an execution-side fingerprint: sparse expert activation creates stable, input-dependent per-expert workload patterns that remain discriminative under syntactic-preserving benign rewrites and generalize despite cross-hardware distribution shift via adaptive feature support selection and score calibration.

Privacy stress tests indicate aggregated prefilling telemetry is hard to invert into exact user text (Token F1=0.160, ROUGE-L=0.119, 0/54 exact sensitive-fact hits), though limited coarse attribute leakage can persist for some categories (e.g., oncology-related LOSO AUROC=0.746 with low high-confidence precision ≈0.516 at p≥0.9).

nodes.title: Cross-Entropy Games and Frost Training
nodes.one_liner: Reward gradients that power jailbreak search (GCG) can also be repurposed to make RL-style LLM training converge faster and to better best-of-k outcomes without collapsing diversity.
points: Frost-GRPO uses one-hot reward gradients (a GCG-style embedding-space Taylor signal) to propose single-token mutations that are then exact-scored and only accepted under a strict-improvement check, yielding higher-scoring training samples while keeping the policy-gradient update itself unchanged.

On the infilling Cross-Entropy Game with Qwen3-14B as both player and judge, Frost-GRPO consistently improves best-of-k validation reward and reaches higher maxima faster than compute-matched GRPO (e.g., Frost K=4,D=4 matches/beats GRPO K=8 at equal 8 judge forward passes per step) while maintaining higher token entropy and reward variance instead of collapsing to low-diversity modes.

In candidate discovery, Taylor-Gated selection (τ=1e-4) dominates Random, TopProb, and ungated Taylor on best-of-K across mutation budgets D∈{1…128} and group sizes K∈{1…32}, delivering roughly +1 nat at D=1 and ~+2 nats at D=128 over the next-best rule and showing that probability-gated reward gradients are a stronger search signal than either policy priors or ungated gradients.

nodes.title: Token Inflation: How Dishonest Providers Can Overcharge for Large Language Model Usage
nodes.one_liner: Per-token billing audits fail structurally when the only evidence comes from the party being billed, enabling massive undetectable overcharging without needing exotic capabilities.
points: Across three representative token-auditing approaches, a provider can evade detection and inflate billed usage dramatically—up to 1,469% average hidden-reasoning inflation in the most permissive setting—turning a $100 honest bill into roughly $1,569 for the same query.

Audits that rely on semantic-consistency checks over provider-supplied artifacts are highly gameable: simple block reuse or lightly perturbed duplicates can pass similarity verifiers while adding ~550%+ extra billable reasoning on average, because commitments bind the submitted trace but not its provenance.

Even when the full reasoning text is visible, tokenization non-uniqueness plus strategic reporting can keep aggregate statistical tests below thresholds while still enabling 50.85% total over-reporting, implying transparency of text alone does not guarantee verifiable token counts.

nodes.title: When Think-with-Image Meets Safety: What Determines Multimodal Jailbreak Robustness?
nodes.one_liner: Tool invocation itself (not the pixels it returns) appears to push LVLMs into a more refusal-prone internal state, creating a pipeline-level knob for multimodal jailbreak robustness.
points: Explicit image-tool interaction in think-with-image pipelines reduces multimodal jailbreak attack success rates by ~30% relative on average across evaluated vision-language model families, outperforming direct answering, simulated image generation/editing, and simple visual-state variants.

The robustness gain is largely independent of returned-image semantics—ASR stays low even when the tool’s returned image is overridden with unsafe-looking or white/noise content (e.g., ~23.8% standard vs ~24.8–26.2% overrides on Qwen3-VL-235B), but disappears under text-only prior-turn controls (ASR returns near direct levels).

Hidden-state analyses indicate image-tool invocation induces a stable, safety-aligned residual shift (“image-tool safety vector”) that makes safety more linearly readable (peak AUC 0.942 vs 0.850 on Qwen3-VL-8B) and behaviorally causal under activation interventions (e.g., ASR 36.6%→23.8% when adding a safety direction in direct mode; 24.8%→31.7% when subtracting it in tool mode).

nodes.title: BAIT: Boundary-Guided Disclosure Escalation via Self-Conditioned Reasoning
nodes.one_liner: Discussing what content should be restricted can itself become a reliable pathway to elicit the restricted content, especially when the model is asked to iteratively refine its own boundary definitions.
points: A fixed three-turn jailbreak (boundary elicitation → refinement → elaboration) exploits self-conditioned consistency so effectively that it achieves the strongest overall attack success rates with lower variance than prompt-transformation baselines across four safety benchmarks (AdvBench, JailbreakBench, AIR-Bench, SORRY-Bench) and eight evaluated LLMs.

Safety- and prevention-oriented framing (“what should be protected”) outperforms an otherwise identical direct knowledge request variant, implying that aligning the conversation with the model’s stated safety goals can reduce refusals while still extracting operationally harmful details.

The refinement step is a key escalation mechanism—removing it (BAIT-2) consistently reduces both overall success and final-step harmful disclosure, with the largest drops reported on strongly aligned proprietary models (notably Claude, then DeepSeek, Gemini, and GPT), indicating that intermediate self-correction prompts can be an attack primitive.

nodes.title: Audio Jailbreaks in Large Audio-Language Models: Taxonomy, Attack-Defense Analysis, and Cost-Aware Evaluation
nodes.one_liner: Acoustic Best-of-N turns “how it’s said” into a high-leverage jailbreak knob, and the most robust defense tested (defensive prompting) achieves safety largely by accepting a near-3× increase in benign refusals.
points: Across 10 open-source large audio-language models on 100 harmful requests, Acoustic Best-of-N (N=20) achieved the highest average jailbreak success (ASR=45.8%) versus Narrative Framing (37.6%) and a no-attack harmful-audio baseline (7.1%), showing that audio-realization search can dominate semantic prompt engineering in worst-case safety failures.

A Whisper-based input guard (VoiceShield) reduced overall attack ASR from 24.5% to 16.5% (32.7% relative) and nearly eliminated explicit Literal Attacks (17.6%→0.4%) but barely affected Acoustic BoN (45.8%→44.1%), indicating that content-based screening is brittle when attackers search over accent/emotion/language/style variations.

A defensive system prompt cut aggregate attack ASR to 6.4% (73.9% relative reduction) and strongly suppressed audio-space attacks (Acoustic BoN 45.8%→9.8%, Signal BoN 22.3%→2.2%) but increased benign refusal rate from 17.1% to 46.1%, quantifying a sharp robustness–usability trade-off that success-rate-only benchmarks would miss.

nodes.title: PAST2HARM: A Simple Adaptive Past Tense Attack for Jailbreaking Multimodal AI
affiliations: Indian Institute of Information Technology, Kalyani
nodes.one_liner: A trivial linguistic shift (past tense) plus a small amount of adaptive iteration reliably pierces multimodal safety filters and even transfers between vendors, making jailbreaks cheap to discover and reuse.
points: A past-tense reframing plus adaptive multi-turn escalation bypassed multimodal refusal pipelines in a black-box, gradient-free setting, reaching 83% ASR on Gemini Nano Banana Pro, 67% on GPT-Image-2, and 100% on SD-XL (with K=6) and up to 88%/69%/56% at K=8 across SDXL/Gemini/GPT-Image-2.

Adaptive temporal deepening delivered a consistent ~28.5 percentage-point ASR lift over non-adaptive baselines (mean 62.3% vs 33.8%), implying current safety training under-covers tense/temporal variants and fails to generalize refusal beyond present-tense imperatives.

Attack prompts were strongly transferable across models with cross-model ASR typically >50% (≈52.7–56.0%), while harm severity followed a rise–plateau–decline curve peaking around mid-conversation (median peak near depth 6), suggesting a short “maximum-risk” window for multi-turn defenses to target.

nodes.title: Curriculum Learning for Safety Alignment
nodes.one_liner: A model-dependent safety curriculum makes DPO both more robust (OOD + jailbreaks) and more data-efficient by turning shallow, first-token refusals into sustained suppression throughout the generation.
points: Staged-Competence (curriculum + competence sampling + staged reference updates) cuts average OOD harmful response rates by 16% and jailbreak attack success rates by 20% across three 8–9B model families while keeping general capability within ~2–3 points and near-zero over-refusal.

Cleaning widely used safety preference datasets is essential: 82.2% of PKU-SafeRLHF “chosen” responses were unsafe and 87.2% of HH-RLHF “rejected” responses were actually safe, leaving only 11.8% of pairs (10,931/92,840) usable after safety filtering for DPO-style training.

The curriculum produces substantially stronger safety separation and depth—~3× larger safe-vs-unsafe reward margin and ~3× greater cumulative per-token suppression of unsafe continuations—explaining improved robustness to prefill-style attacks that bypass initial refusal tokens.

nodes.title: KSAFE-MM: A Multimodal Safety Benchmark via Localized Contextualization for Korean Cultural Risks
nodes.one_liner: Localized cultural cues plus multimodal jailbreak prompting reveal safety failures that translation-only benchmarks systematically miss, while also surfacing over-refusal as a dominant “safe-but-useless” failure mode.
points: KSAFE-MM provides 14,135 Korean multimodal safety samples spanning 11 risk categories, including both localized versions of global risks (KSAFE-MM-G) and Korea-specific culturally grounded attacks paired with jailbreak prompts (KSAFE-MM-C).

Cultural and adversarial prompt framing materially increases harmful compliance: culturally grounded prompts raise ASR versus naive translation (e.g., 29.38% → 38.20%), and jailbreaks can spike ASR from 13.4% (template) to 74.2% (ProgramExecution) on Gemma-27B.

Safety performance shows a strong utility trade-off where the lowest-ASR models on culturally grounded risks (e.g., HyperCLOV A X-Think 10.4% ASR; GPT-5 nano 14.5% ASR) also exhibit high refusal rates (51.0% and 41.9%), indicating safety often comes from broad refusals rather than precise harm discrimination.

nodes.title: Position: Retire the "Positive Backdoor" Label -- Secret Alignment Requires Strict and Systematic Evaluation
nodes.one_liner: “Positive backdoor” is a misleading label: hidden trigger-conditioned behaviors behave like a brittle alignment control and can fail hard on CIA unless proven under standardized, adversarial evaluation.
points: Reframing trigger-activated hidden behaviors as “Secret Alignment” highlights that protective uses of backdoor-style mechanisms are not secure by default and must be evaluated against confidentiality, integrity, and availability (CIA) failure modes before deployment.

Across three representative applications, access control via SudoLM showed large brittleness under realistic tests (e.g., jailbreak bypass rates of 45.73% for domain gating and 85.56% for fine-grained gating), indicating trigger-based access gating is easily subverted by prompt attacks.

Instructional Fingerprinting achieved 100% fingerprint success when triggered, but robustness was poor with accidental/adversarial collisions (misactivation 0.7% under unconditional generation and >50% for prompts moderately similar to the trigger template), implying watermark-style secret behaviors can leak or be probed even in black-box settings.

nodes.title: Security in the Fine-Tuning Lifecycle of Large Language Models: Threats, Defenses,Evaluation, and Future Directions
affiliations: China Mobile (Zhejiang) Innovation Research Institute Co., Ltd.
Quantum Cloud Computing and Distributed Systems (qCLOUDS) Lab
nodes.one_liner: A lifecycle view plus unified experiments reveals that fine-tuning security failures often come from phase-mismatched defenses and from attacks that don’t look “malicious” in the data at all.
points: Unified benchmarks on 1B–4B open LLMs show fine-tuning security is highly model- and alignment-state-dependent, with weight-editing attacks that worked on older models becoming weak or zero-ASR on many modern configurations and defenses sometimes increasing attack success or harming clean accuracy.

Cross-lingual backdoor transfer can collapse entirely at smaller scales (0% transfer across all tested non-poisoned languages on 1B–4B models), implying that multilingual capability—not just poisoning quality—drives transfer risk and that results from larger multilingual models may not generalize downward.

Benign-only fine-tuning can still jailbreak instruction-tuned models (e.g., refusal rate dropping from 74%→6% on Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct and 100%→22% on Qwen3-4B-Instruct), demonstrating that content-based screening is insufficient and that training-dynamics/overfitting controls are necessary in FTaaS settings.

nodes.title: Hijacking Agent Memory: Stealthy Trojan Attacks Through Conversational Interaction
nodes.one_liner: A dialogue-only attacker can stealthily plant a persistent, triggerable backdoor that survives memory summarization and paraphrasing by exploiting entity preservation and embedding-space geometry.
points: MemPoison reliably implants triggerable backdoors into selective long-term memory pipelines via dialogue-only access, reaching up to 0.95 attack success rate (ASR) and up to 0.98–1.00 retrieval success at rank-1 (RSR@1) across multiple agent domains and memory mechanisms while keeping benign accuracy near baseline (~0.87–0.96).

Selective extraction and rewriting break prior memory-poisoning baselines (often driving RSR@1/ASR to ~0), but binding trigger and payload into a single semantically interdependent statement is necessary (removing the bridge yields RSR@1=0 and ASR=0) and entity-masqueraded triggers survive paraphrasing because named entities are preserved far more often (0.85 verbatim retention vs 0.60 for non-entity nouns).

Common defenses are insufficient or costly: perplexity filtering only suppresses the attack at strict thresholds while harming utility (ACC drops to ~0.71 at PPL≤75) and paraphrasing defenses that neutralize concatenation-based attacks still leave MemPoison at ASR ~0.77–0.89, indicating a fundamental vulnerability in embedding-similarity retrieval and memory admission control.

nodes.title: Conceptual Steganography
nodes.one_liner: Paraphrasing scrambles words but tends to preserve reasoning behaviors, leaving a covert channel that can carry messages through the structure of thought itself unless the sanitizer explicitly targets those behaviors.
points: Encoding bits via high-level reasoning behaviors inside chain-of-thought steps (conceptual steganography) survives content-preserving paraphrase defenses better than matched keyword/lexical schemes across 4 open-weight model families and 2 reasoning benchmarks (e.g., message accuracy after paraphrase improves from 17.8%→53.6% on GPT-OSS-120B/MATH-500 and 76.3%→89.4% on MiMo/GPQA).

The robustness advantage comes primarily from lower within-step bit corruption rather than step boundary drift, with concept channels showing materially lower post-paraphrase bit error than keyword baselines (e.g., on GPT-OSS-120B/MATH-500 16.9% vs 38.9% bit error) while step error remains small in most settings (often ≤0.5%).

Disabling chain-of-thought is an impractical mitigation because accuracy drops sharply (20–33 absolute points on GPQA and 28–43 on MATH-500), whereas a strategy-aware paraphraser that targets the carrier behavior can raise bit error by ~10× and cut message accuracy by 58–72 points (e.g., GPQA: 89–99%→~20–41%), indicating defenses must operate on reasoning content, not surface form.

nodes.title: SilentRetrieval: Hijacking Retrieval-Augmented Generation via Semantically-Preserving Adversarial Data Poisoning
nodes.one_liner: Stealthy single-document poisoning can hijack both retrieval and generation at scale, and simple fluency filtering is too weak unless paired with heavier reranking and generation-time isolation defenses.
points: A two-stage RAG corpus-poisoning pipeline (fluency-constrained Coordinated Beam Search + context-adaptive trigger fusion) achieves 84.6%/81.3% HR@10 and 57.5%/54.8% ASR-LLM on NQ (361K passages) and MS MARCO (8.8M), while keeping perplexity near benign levels (32.4 vs 28.4).

Effectiveness transfers beyond the optimized stack, reaching 48.6–57.5% ASR-LLM across four different target LLMs and ~64.7% average HR@10 when attacking unseen retrievers (including ColBERT and commercial embeddings) via surrogate-optimized poisoned documents.

Layered mitigations cut generation manipulation substantially but at real cost: combining hybrid retrieval, cross-encoder reranking, and passage-isolation voting reduces ASR-LLM to 25.6% (6× latency) and 21.3% in the strongest setting, while an adaptive attacker recovers +6.2% HR@10 against a matched reranker at ~3× longer optimization time.

nodes.title: Grounded Cache Routing for Retrieval-Augmented Generation: When Is It Safe to Reuse an Answer?
nodes.one_liner: Semantic answer caching is fast but dangerously error-prone under paraphrases, drift, and multi-turn state, and a lightweight evidence-support check can make reuse auditable and near-safe via an operator-facing USR metric.
points: A four-gate evidence-validated cache router (query similarity, evidence overlap, source-version match, and evidence support) reduced unsafe-served rate (USR) from 15–35% to 0.0% across all HotpotQA regimes and from 51.5% to 1.5% in mtRAG document drift (≈34× fewer wrong cached answers per query than naive semantic caching).

A cheap lexical evidence-support gate is the primary safety mechanism, with its removal increasing mean USR by +0.125 on HotpotQA and +0.118 on mtRAG, while query-similarity and evidence-overlap gates contributed near-zero marginal USR change but add low-cost defense-in-depth.

Safety comes mainly from aggressively suppressing answer-cache hits under drift and multi-turn referent shift, keeping p50 end-to-end latency within 1.04–1.07× of a no-cache RAG baseline (vs. 1.73–1.95× speedups for naive caching that carry 17–26% USR), making USR a practical operator metric for choosing a safe point on the reuse–risk frontier.

nodes.title: Turning Bias into Bugs: Bandit-Guided Style Manipulation Attacks on LLM Judges
nodes.one_liner: It shows that minor, meaning-preserving style tweaks—learned adaptively with bandits—can reliably “cheat” LLM judges at scale and slip past common detectors and debiasing.
points: A black-box contextual-bandit attack (BITE) can inflate LLM-as-a-judge scores while preserving semantics, achieving >65% attack success and typically +1–2 points on a 9-point scale across multiple judges and tasks.

BITE outperforms prompt-injection and jailbreak baselines under a strict 25-query budget, with average score gains up to +2.01 (vs. ≤+1.86 for PAIR and ≤+1.49 for AutoDAN) across five judges, indicating stylistic bias is a higher-leverage attack surface than overt instruction hijacking.

Attacks are stealthy and model-specific: semantic similarity is usually >0.9, OpenAI’s prompt-injection detector flags 0% of BITE samples (vs. 100% for injection baselines), and transferability is low (often ~10–30% Transfer ASR), implying each judge has a distinct “vulnerability fingerprint” that evades simple debiasing like style-control calibration.

nodes.title: Honeyval: A Comprehensive Evaluation Framework for LLM-powered HTTP Honeypots
nodes.one_liner: A standardized, agent-driven harness makes LLM honeypots measurable on realism, stealth, and economics—and shows prompt “deception” knobs can backfire by boosting detectability.
points: LLM-powered HTTP honeypots keep attackers engaged for ~2.7× more requests than rule-based mocks (82.6 vs 30.6 on average), increasing defender-side opportunity for tactic/goal collection without exposing real system resources.

Agent-based honeypot detection stays relatively low for LLM honeypots (typically <40% detected) while rule-based honeypots are flagged at very high rates, and detection does not strongly track model size/capability (e.g., an open 9B model remains competitive on stealth).

Even with a token-inefficient implementation, LLM honeypots usually maintain a per-interaction cost advantage over agentic attackers (≈3.6× on average), but latency remains a practical fingerprinting risk (median ~3.3–13.7s) and prompt-based “counter-offense” increases detection (TPR 0.24→0.45–0.49) while trading off fidelity (Pass@1 0.84→0.62 under prompt-injection behavior).

nodes.title: Intelligent Detection and Mitigation of Carpet-Bombing DDoS Attacks in SDN Using Retrieval-Augmented Generation and Large Language Models
nodes.one_liner: RAG turns SDN DDoS defense into a near-zero-training classification problem where a structured JSON schema plus retrieval context makes LLM decisions both accurate and operationally deployable.
points: A retrieval-augmented LLM pipeline using interface-level SDN statistics (10 features polled every 10s) detected carpet-bombing DDoS with 99.94% accuracy, 99.92% precision, 99.93% recall, and 99.93% F1 (AUC=1.00) while avoiding supervised training or fine-tuning.

Structured JSON feature prompts were marginally more stable than natural-language descriptions (JSON: 7 FP/6 FN vs NLR: 13 FP/2 FN on the same test split), indicating that stricter schema reduces semantic ambiguity and improves consistency across inference runs.

In real-time SDN experiments across attack intensities from ~20k to ~100k packets/sec, controller-integrated detection plus source-interface drop rules rapidly reduced Packet-In surges and CPU pressure, with an auto-recovery policy that re-enabled blocked interfaces after 40 seconds to preserve operational availability.

nodes.title: HARP: Measuring Harm Amplification in Multi-Agent LLM Systems
nodes.one_liner: Measuring whether an attack succeeds is insufficient in multi-agent systems because small, localized perturbations can be amplified several-fold by routing, shared context, and decision gates—so evaluation should quantify propagation (HA) over full execution traces.
points: Across 12 finance-oriented scenarios (100 queries each; 50 attack-triggering), shared-context corruption had the highest no-defense attack success rate (70.7% ASR), while a single compromised specialist produced the clearest propagation signature with global harm averaging ~3.9× local harm (HA≈3.91).

Prompt-only “React-sandwich” defenses preserved benign utility at 100% but left attack success largely unchanged versus no defense in most regimes (e.g., VT-1 ASR 56.7% vs 58.0%; VT-3 ASR 68.0% vs 70.7%), implying that lightweight prompt hardening does not address orchestration-driven harm spread.

A trace-consistency verifier (IntegrityGuard) reduced ASR sharply across vulnerability regimes (e.g., VT-2 collusion 12.0% and VT-3 shared-context 4.7%) and lowered global harm, but incurred sizable false positives/utility loss in several settings (benign utility down to 60–67% in VT-1–VT-3) and higher token-cost overhead (e.g., VT-1 NTC ~896).

nodes.title: Harmless Yet Harmful: Neutral Prompting Attacks for Stealthy Hallucination Steering in Agent Skills
affiliations: National Yang Ming Chiao Tung University
nodes.one_liner: Harmless-looking “quality/creativity” instructions can covertly turn coding-agent package suggestions into a scalable software supply-chain hazard by increasing both hallucinations and installable commands while slipping past content-based prompt scanners.
points: Neutral Prompting Attack (NPA) uses semantically benign “be imaginative/exhaustive” Skill text to amplify dependency hallucinations, boosting Hallucination ASR on Qwen2.5-Coder-32B from 3.53%→57.76% (LLM_AT) and 4.54%→78.99% (LLM_LY) while also raising executable Pip Install ASR to 50.63% and 63.33% respectively.

NPA is transferable across models: an NPA Skill optimized on Qwen2.5-Coder-32B increased hallucination on GPT-5 from 1.28%→29.29% and Pip Install ASR from 8.17%→32.15%, indicating a general prompt-level behavioral steering risk rather than a single-model artifact.

NPA shifts outputs in a structured way (not just “more noise”) by concentrating on repeatable hallucinated package names (unique-name share drops from 73.7% under baseline to 54.4% under NPA), and it largely evades current Skill scanners (static tools missed it; only Snyk Agent Scan flagged it, and an optimization variant (NPA-Snyk) regained strong ASR while bypassing detection).

nodes.title: How Reliable Are AI Attackers Against a Fixed Vulnerable Target? A 400-Run Empirical Study of LLM Penetration Testing Consistency
nodes.one_liner: The striking result is that repeated, fully autonomous LLM attackers show zero durable refusals under an authorized-pentest wrapper, while their success, failure modes, and chaining behavior diverge dramatically—and even cloud API overload events can masquerade as “safety.”
points: Across 400 autonomous pentest runs (100/model) under an “authorized assessment” scaffold with one-shot re-prompting, 0% of runs exhibited a surviving model-level content refusal, implying refusal-based safety gates can fail completely under professional framing.

End-to-end compromise reliability varied sharply on the same fixed 3-service target: Gemini 2.5 Flash-Lite succeeded in 85% of runs, GPT-4o-mini 56% (with 98/100 unique strategies), Claude Sonnet 4 61% despite 39% run truncation from upstream HTTP 529 overloads (8.0% of 1,135 API calls), and qwen2.5-coder:14b 25% due mainly to premature completion (52%).

Cross-service credential reuse emerged only when more context effectively persisted—qwen reused FTP-leaked creds for SSH in 57% of runs and GPT-4o-mini in 49%, while Claude and Gemini showed 0% on a 5-exchange history window—suggesting context retention policies can materially suppress multi-step attack chaining even when base exploit rates are high.

nodes.title: MemMorph: Tool Hijacking in LLM Agents via Memory Poisoning
nodes.one_liner: Long-term memory turns into a stealthy, persistent control plane for tool-augmented agents: a tiny, well-crafted poison budget can reliably reroute actions even under rewriting and auditing.
points: Injecting only three benign-looking long-term memory records can hijack an agent’s tool choice with up to 85.9% attack success across 3 benchmarks and 10 backbone LLMs, showing tool routing can be persistently subverted without touching tool metadata.

The attack achieves very high retrieval exposure (average 92.3% attack-hit rate across benchmarks), and the gap between retrieval and success indicates that persuasive, multi-style “evidence” (facts + incident + policy) is a key driver of unsafe tool selection rather than retrieval alone.

Common storage-time defenses tuned to ≤5% false positives (perplexity filter, distilled classifier, and an LLM memory auditor) reduce but do not stop the attack, leaving 54.9–69.8% attack success under defense and implying current agent memory lacks integrity controls robust to semantically plausible poisoning.

nodes.title: GraphSteal: Structural Knowledge Stealing from Graph RAG via Traversal Reconstruction
nodes.one_liner: Graph-grounded retrieval doesn’t just leak facts—it leaks enough relational evidence to algorithmically reconstruct the underlying private topology via BFS/DFS-style query chaining.
points: Adaptive black-box traversal prompts can turn Graph RAG into a “structural oracle,” enabling reconstruction of over 90% of hidden knowledge graphs (NRR up to 0.969, MCS up to 0.969, normalized GED as low as 0.055) on representative systems.

Targeted extraction remains highly reliable across retrieval- and agent-based Graph RAG, with F1 consistently above 0.86 on MIMIC-IV and peaking at 0.917, showing that sensitive entity attributes and incident edges can be recovered without internal access.

Common mitigations are weak: protective system prompts can be overridden and output token caps reduce leakage but do not prevent it (and harm utility), while even summary-based Microsoft GraphRAG still leaks ~70% of entities (NRR 0.712 on MIMIC, 0.665 on FreeBase).

nodes.title: Five Queries Are Enough: Query-Efficient and Surrogate-Free Membership Inference Attacks on RAG via Entailment
nodes.one_liner: Entailment-based verification turns a handful of normal-looking questions into a high-confidence membership test for RAG corpora, exposing a serious privacy gap between “grounded answers” and “grounded privacy.”
points: A black-box membership inference attack on RAG can reach up to 0.991 AUC using only 5 non-templated queries by scoring natural-language entailment between answers and a candidate document, outperforming prior black-box baselines by up to 0.42 AUC under matched budgets.

The attack remains strong under common RAG mitigations (query paraphrasing, instruction prompts, reranking, and DP-style output perturbation), retaining ~0.90+ AUC in defended settings (e.g., 0.913 AUC on NFCorpus under DP) while a leading stealth baseline collapses near chance (e.g., 0.518 AUC under DP).

Input-query detectors struggle to stop stealthy MIAs without breaking usability: an LLM-based detector flags only ~6% of these benign-looking attack queries, while a similarity-based detector can reach high recall but at prohibitive benign false-positive rates up to 0.881.

nodes.title: GEO-Bench: Benchmarking Ranking Manipulation in Generative Engine Optimization
nodes.one_liner: Standardized evaluation reveals that fluent, keyword-clean content rewriting can outperform or match classic gradient attacks and slip past common detectors, making GEO manipulation a practical black-box risk.
points: Across five datasets on a fixed Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct ranker, black-box methods were often strongest (the top average-NRG methods were TAP, Authoritative, and Content Improvement), showing that access level (black-box vs. gradient/white-box) is not a reliable predictor of manipulation strength.

Adversarial methods exhibit a strong effectiveness–stealth tradeoff: TAP achieves decisive promotion (Promote@0.1 up to 0.92) but is highly keyword-detectable (KVR ≥ 0.83 across datasets), while gradient STS keeps keywords low (KVR 0.03–0.20) but severely degrades fluency (PPL-R up to 12.72).

Seemingly benign rewriting can be the hardest-to-detect threat: on C-SEO Bench, Authoritative rewriting reached the best NRG (0.83, tied) with 0.00 keyword violations and a low perplexity ratio (PPL-R 0.74), simultaneously evading both keyword- and perplexity-based detectors while strongly promoting rank.

nodes.title: Cybersecurity AI (CAI) Dataset
nodes.one_liner: A massive, longitudinal dataset of real cybersecurity LLM agent trajectories shows automation scaling fast while operators routinely trade confidentiality for speed—creating a concentrated systemic risk at model/API providers.
points: CAI Dataset logs 230,935 real-world cybersecurity agent sessions over 14 months with 26,027,742 user prompts (18.07 TB) spanning 123 countries, 16,768 source IPs, 4,187 LLM identifiers, and 23,147 target domains, making it one of the largest described corpora of LLM-driven cyber trajectories and enabling training/evaluation at operator-behavior scale rather than benchmark scale.

Prompt-level role mix skews strongly offensive (36.4%) and attacker-intent (20.1%) versus defensive (4.4%), implying most in-the-wild LLM cyber usage is oriented toward exploitation workflows and that safety/abuse controls must be evaluated against multi-step agent trajectories rather than single-turn prompts.

Operational confidentiality leakage is measurable and non-trivial: 0.46% of sessions contain pasted credentials and 0.76% contain pasted production identifiers (e.g., internal hostnames/buckets), indicating that centralized model-API logging creates a high-value, single-failure data concentration point and strengthening the case for on-prem/private-hosted cyber LLM deployments with strong redaction.

nodes.title: Refusal Before Decoding: Detecting and Exploiting Refusal Signals in Intermediate LLM Activations
nodes.one_liner: Refusal is an early, linearly decodable—and operationally exploitable—internal signal that can accelerate jailbreak prompt search without fully running the model.
points: Linear probes predict refusal from intermediate residual-stream activations with ~0.992–0.995 test accuracy across Llama-3.2-3B, Qwen3Guard-Gen-4B, and Qwen-3.6-27B, showing refusal is represented well before final-layer decoding.

Replacing output-based fitness with probe-scored partial forward passes yields jailbreak attack success rates close to AutoDAN while cutting per-iteration search time by up to 72% (e.g., Qwen-27B 17.44s → 4.79s at Block 10 LR with 0.89 → 0.82 ASR).

Probe-guided optimization can improve cross-model transfer in several settings (e.g., Qwen-27B→Qwen-4B ASR 0.27 with AutoDAN vs 0.50 with a Block 10 LR probe), but probe guidance is least informative on smaller models where ASR remains high even when optimizing in the “wrong” direction (≤0.05 ASR change), indicating search saturation dominates there.

nodes.title: SAMD: A Tool for Identifying False Data Injection Scenarios in AI/ML-enabled Medical Devices
nodes.one_liner: Interesting because it turns messy, multi-vendor medical-device integration risk into fast, structured, vulnerability-grounded false-data-injection scenarios that are concrete enough to drive design-time mitigations and deployment advisories.
points: SAMD automatically surfaces inference-time false data injection attack paths in AI/ML-enabled medical devices by combining STPA-Sec control-structure modeling with NER, CVE mining, and LLM-guided scenario generation to connect peripheral-device vulnerabilities to hazardous ML outputs.

Across five FDA-cleared devices, the tool extracted technologies from documentation with 100% precision, filtered CVEs relevant to data tampering with 63.2% precision (36/57 flagged were truly relevant), and generated end-to-end attack scenarios with 95.3% average accuracy while completing analyses in as little as 8.74s and at most 191.64s.

The vulnerability triage surfaced actionable risk concentration in newly disclosed issues (35/36 relevant CVEs from 2025), including 14 unpatched and 3 rated severe/critical, implying that continuous, automated post-deployment reassessment is necessary because upstream peripherals can rapidly invalidate “secure-at-launch” assumptions.

nodes.title: KBF: Knowledge Boundary as Fingerprint for Language Model and Black-Box API Auditing
nodes.one_liner: Knowledge-boundary numerical recall— including repeatable wrong numbers—acts like a stable, low-cost fingerprint that can expose silent model substitution and even partial mixed-routing in black-box LLM APIs.
points: A black-box numerical-recall fingerprinting protocol detects all 155 economically relevant model substitutions across 16 production endpoints at p<0.05 while producing 0/16 false-positive rejections on same-model controls, indicating practical, precision-first auditing of relay/reseller LLM APIs is feasible without logits or provider cooperation.

The method remains robust under deployment variation (role prompts, temperature changes, and RAG-style wrappers), achieving 0/30 false positives and 60/60 substitution detections where baseline black-box approaches show high false-positive rates (e.g., 29/30 for a distributional test) or miss many substitutions (e.g., 14/60 missed), implying boundary-recall signals are substantially more configuration-invariant than output-distribution or behavioral probes.

It detects mixed-routing attacks at low substitution fractions in favorable cases (95% TPR with only 5–10% rerouted traffic for Opus-based pairs) but needs up to 50% rerouting for the hardest close-capability pairs, and a six-platform shadow audit flags 7/27 endpoints (26%) as inconsistent with references with 6/7 flags on premium Claude endpoints, suggesting economically motivated substitution concentrates on high-margin proprietary models and can vary by pricing tier.

nodes.title: APT-Agent: Automated Penetration Testing using Large Language Models
nodes.one_liner: Grounding LLM outputs to a real tool knowledge base plus minimal JSON state is enough to turn brittle prompt-chains into a mostly reliable end-to-end pentest agent.
points: An autonomous LLM agent orchestrating recon→exploit→exfiltration reached 84.29% end-to-end success (59/70) across seven Metasploitable2 services, outperforming Script Kiddie (48.57%) and PentestGPT (18.57%) under matched GPT-4o conditions.

Hallucinated Metasploit module paths were frequent (30.2% of 53 module invocations), and a hybrid suffix+fuzzy rectifier recovered 62.5% of hallucinations in live runs (and 51.95% on a 78-item held-out set), directly converting otherwise-failing exploit attempts into executable actions.

Structured, stage-aware command/outcome memory improved long-horizon reliability by cutting success from 84.29% to 65.71% when removed and reducing redundant exfiltration steps by up to 17× (UnrealIRCd: 52→3 iterations), indicating that compact state tracking is a primary driver of stable multi-step cyber agents.

nodes.title: HunterAgent: Neuro-Symbolic Attack Trace Reconstruction under Anti-Forensics
affiliations: National Computer Network Emergency Response Technical Team/Coordination Center of China (CNCERT/CC)
Chang An Communication Technology Co., Ltd.
Harbin Institute of Technology (Weihai) Qingdao Research Institute
Shandong Key Laboratory of Industrial Network Security
nodes.one_liner: Interesting because it operationalizes 'don’t hallucinate' for LLM-driven forensics by converting trace reconstruction into budgeted search that provably stops when evidence is too thin.
points: A cost-bounded neuro-symbolic search with an LLM-as-hypothesis-generator plus deterministic verifier achieves 86.1% mean F1 (91.3% precision) across four benchmarks under strict Leave-One-Family-Out validation, outperforming the strongest agentic baseline by +26.7 F1 and the strongest non-agentic baseline (KAIROS) by +17.1 F1.

Constraining generation to a typed ontology and requiring identifier-level grounding on surviving orthogonal telemetry cuts path-level hallucinations from 61.5% to 6.4%, turning fluent-but-invalid investigative narratives into audit-grade evidence stratified into verified edges vs. explicitly labeled inferred leads.

Under severe evidence loss (70% targeted log wiping), precision stays ≥84% while 95.7% of cases halt with an explicit insufficient-evidence outcome instead of fabricating chains, implying a practical safety mechanism where recall degrades gracefully but forensic integrity remains intact.

nodes.title: TTPrint: Evidence-Grounded TTP Extraction via Diverge-then-Converge Verification
nodes.one_liner: Separating high-recall hypothesis generation from evidence-and-definition verification turns LLM-based TTP extraction from a brittle single-pass guess into an auditable, tunable pipeline that sharply reduces unsupported technique labels.
