points: A four-stage diverge-then-converge pipeline that grounds candidates to localized text spans and verifies against official MITRE definitions reaches 76.48% macro-F1 on TRAM-Clean and 87.39% on a 150-report document-level benchmark, beating the strongest single-pass LLM baseline by +63.5% and +29.4% respectively while improving both precision and recall.

Evidence-grounded verification is the main precision lever: removing verification drops precision by 26.6 points (82.15%→55.55%) and macro-F1 by 23.0 points, showing that definition-based cross-checking materially suppresses hallucinated techniques in open-set multi-label extraction.

The architecture generalizes across LLMs and offers an operational knob: all six tested backbones exceed the best single-pass ceiling (≈58% macro-F1), and a single threshold τ provides smooth precision–recall control with an F1-optimal setting at τ=0.7 (82.15% precision, 95.50% recall) and stable performance within ~3% for τ∈[0.55,0.75].

nodes.title: Agent-ToM: Learning to Monitor Autonomous LLM Agents via Theory-of-Mind Reasoning
nodes.one_liner: Learning at the monitoring layer—via Theory-of-Mind belief/intent inference plus verification and reusable guardrails—can beat ensemble monitoring accuracy at a fraction of inference cost, and the biggest gains show up under out-of-distribution evasion.
points: A two-call Reason–Verify–Refine monitor with learned semantic guardrail memory reaches 0.782 F1 on SHADE-Arena (0.818 precision, 0.748 recall), exceeding compute-heavy ensembles (e.g., sequential ensemble 0.727 F1 with 17 calls) while preserving calibration.

Out-of-distribution transfer to CUA-SHADE-Arena improves sharply when verification is trained: Stage 1 memory alone scores 0.615 F1 (0.444 recall) but Stage 1–2 reaches 0.831 F1 with 1.000 precision and 0.710 recall, indicating verification is a key robustness lever under domain shift.

Critique-driven self-correction materially increases monitor learning quality, with F1 scaling from 0.695→0.814 as training coverage grows 9%→100%, while removing critique feedback caps performance around 0.668 despite accumulating many rules, implying that memory growth without high-signal critiques yields weak guardrails.

nodes.title: S3C2 Summit 2025-07: Government Secure Supply Chain Summit
nodes.one_liner: The most actionable takeaway is that government practitioners increasingly view supply-chain security progress as bottlenecked by incentives and enforceability (SBOM/VEX/attestations) rather than by a lack of tools alone.
points: A capped, Chatham-House-Rule summit with 12 participants from 6 U.S. government agencies surfaced that SBOMs are still treated as compliance checkboxes with persistent trust/quality/format fragmentation, leaving agencies to independently verify supply-chain security rather than relying on vendor-provided artifacts.

VEX adoption remains low largely due to legal-liability and incentive misalignment (e.g., fear of vulnerability disclosures creating exposure), pushing practitioners toward multi-source vulnerability triage (OSV, GitHub Advisories, CISA KEV, paid services, internal intel) instead of standardized producer assertions.

LLM risk management is increasingly framed as conventional software assurance—highlighting package hallucinations, poisoning, opaque training-data/IP constraints, and the need for AI-specific SBOM standards—while agencies prefer strict separation of LLMs from high-consequence systems until uncertainty and provenance are better bounded.

nodes.title: An Efficient and Privacy-Preserving Architecture for Cross-Institutional Collaborative RAG
affiliations: Cloud Computing Research Institute, China Telecom
nodes.one_liner: A non-cryptographic, attention-preserving scrambling protocol makes federated long-context RAG fast enough for interactive use while still resisting intermediate-state text recovery attacks.
points: FedRAG enables cross-institutional RAG without sharing plaintext documents by running self-attention over scrambled Q/K/V tensors, reporting <0.1% average utility degradation while remaining mathematically equivalent to standard attention after descrambling.

A structured, numerically stable scrambling matrix (S1P1HP2S2 with Hadamard + permutations + random scalings) preserves inner products and linearity for attention yet breaks distance/topology signals used by inversion methods, driving kNN overlap below 15% at sequence length 4096 and preventing ICA recovery from exceeding random-guess similarity.

Compared to secure baselines (SCX, PermLLM), the system achieves up to a 62× end-to-end latency reduction and maintains practical decoding throughput (>10 tokens/s at ~1 ms RTT) with linear-in-sequence-length overhead, while avoiding TEEs, model retraining, and heavyweight cryptography.

nodes.title: S3C2 Summit 2025-09: Industry Secure Supply Chain Summit
nodes.one_liner: The most actionable shift is treating identity (workloads and agents) and provenance as the core primitive for both traditional CI/CD and emerging AI-native supply chains.
points: Ten practitioners from eight companies reported that vulnerable-dependency handling is dominated by alert fatigue and prioritization uncertainty, pushing teams to move beyond CVE-only triage toward richer risk signals like maintainer activity, lifecycle status, obfuscation, and provenance confidence.

CI/CD security bottlenecks are increasingly about trustworthy workload identity and provenance verification at scale (e.g., inconsistent OIDC behavior, high in-toto/SLSA metadata volume, and key-rotation overhead), leading to controls like registry-side verification, network-isolated builds after dependency fetch, and parallel reproducible builds for anomaly detection rather than determinism alone.

Generative-AI adoption is expanding the supply-chain perimeter from code and containers to IDE plugins, MCP connectors, models/datasets/agent actions, and developer endpoints, motivating tiered agent permissions (read-only for juniors), stricter defaults on connectors, and extending SLSA/GUAC-style attestations to AI pipelines and inference-time provenance.

nodes.title: SEC-bench Pro: Can Language Models Solve Long-Horizon Software Security Tasks?
nodes.one_liner: A high-fidelity, self-expanding benchmark shows that even top coding agents rarely produce attributable PoCs for real JS-engine bugs, and simplistic crash metrics can inflate apparent progress by ~44%.
points: SEC-bench Pro packages 183 PoC-and-patch–validated JavaScript-engine vulnerabilities (103 V8, 80 SpiderMonkey) as reproducible vulnerable/fixed/latest Docker triples, including a V8 subset tied to $1.54M in cumulative Google VRP awards.

Frontier coding agents still solve under 40% of long-horizon bug-hunting instances (best single config: 32.0% on V8 and 38.8% on SpiderMonkey), while an open-weight baseline reaches 11.7% on V8, implying most real engine bugs remain out of reach for current autonomous exploit/PoC synthesis.

Naive crash-only grading overcounts success by 43.6% (168 vs 117 configuration-instance wins), and combining complementary agents raises coverage to 37.9% on V8 and 48.8% on SpiderMonkey, showing both attribution-aware evaluation and ensemble search materially change conclusions about capability.

nodes.title: Dissecting the Black Box: Circuit-Level Analysis of LLM Vulnerability Detection
nodes.one_liner: The most striking takeaway is that this LLM often “detects vulnerabilities by detecting safety,” making its decisions both sparsely localized and potentially evadable via safety-pattern mimicry.
points: Mechanistic tracing on Gemma-2-2b over 472 balanced PrimeVul C/C++ functions shows vulnerability predictions are largely driven by early-layer “safety detector” attention heads that strongly activate on safe patterns, with vulnerability flagged when these safety signals fail to appear.

Causal ablations indicate a sharp bottleneck for true-positive vulnerability detection: mean-ablating Layer 11 collapses TP accuracy from 100% to ~6% (a 94% drop) while retaining ~65% true-negative accuracy, revealing an asymmetric “safe-by-default unless asserted otherwise” decision pathway.

Vulnerability detection relies on sparse, modular circuitry—only ~16.1% of probed nodes activate on traced examples—and a tiny set of Layer-7 MLP features is disproportionately important, where ablating just 20 neurons (<0.2% of the layer) cuts vulnerability detection by ~50%.

nodes.title: Demystifying the Mythos or Disrupting Bugonomics? From Zero-Day Asymmetry to Defender Remediation Throughput
nodes.one_liner: The key insight is that AI makes vulnerability discovery cheaper faster than remediation capacity scales, so defender advantage depends on converting low-signal candidates into high-signal, maintainer-friendly remediation packages.
points: Public Mythos/Firefox figures suggest LLM-assisted pipelines shift the binding constraint from discovering candidates to validating, triaging, and shipping fixes, with Firefox data showing a 19.6% acceptance rate (22/112) and implying ~5.1 reports reviewed per accepted vulnerability (~8 per high-severity).

API spend can make large-scale candidate generation surprisingly cheap (Mythos: < $20k for 1,000 runs yielding “several dozen” findings, implying roughly <$417–$833 per reported finding if 24–48), but total cost is dominated by human validation and maintainer throughput once report volume scales.

Exploit development remains economically distinct from “bug finding” (e.g., ~$4k for hundreds of attempts yielding two crude test-environment exploits), reinforcing that operational exploit chains retain scarcity/value while defenders increasingly benefit from evidence-rich remediation packages (reproducers, impact proofs, patches, tests) that reduce maintainer review time.

nodes.title: An Empirical Evaluation of LLM-Generated Code Security Across Prompting Methods
affiliations: King Fahd University of Petroleum and Minerals
nodes.one_liner: Security-aware prompting (WA-0CoT) reshapes which CWEs appear—especially in Python—but does not measurably reduce how often or how densely vulnerabilities occur.
points: Across 5 LLMs, 4 languages, and 4 prompting methods (including WA-0CoT), chi-squared tests found no statistically significant reductions (p>0.05) in either vulnerable-task frequency or vulnerability density (vulnerabilities/LoC), indicating prompt engineering alone does not reliably improve security.

Observed vulnerable-task rates stayed in a narrow band—about 1.5%–9.5% across all model–language–prompt combinations—while WA-0CoT often increased verbosity (e.g., C total LoC rose from 36,536 with Vanilla to 44,579 with WA-0CoT) without lowering vulnerability totals, implying added “security context” can expand attack surface rather than reduce it.

Prompting consistently changed the composition of weaknesses (CWE mix) even when totals did not change, with the largest CWE-profile shifts in Python (mean JSD≈0.147; max≈0.357) and smaller shifts in C/C++ (mean JSD≈0.061/0.040), meaning prompts tend to reallocate risk across CWE categories rather than eliminate it.

nodes.title: Enhancing Reliability in LLM-Based Secure Code Generation
nodes.one_liner: Security improves most when prompts inject actionable CWE mitigations and language-specific safeguards, while generic CoT can backfire—especially in memory-unsafe C—highlighting that prompt structure is a real security control, not just a usability tweak.
points: Mitigation-Aware Chain-of-Thought (MA-CoT) cut total static-analysis security findings on the 200-task dataset from 92 to 39 (−57.6%) and on LLMSecEval from 73 to 4 (−94.5%), outperforming Vanilla, Zero-shot, and generic CoT across 3 models and 3 languages.

High-severity issues (Blocker+Critical) dropped from 90 to 39 (−56.7%) on the primary dataset and from 45 to 2 (−95.6%) on LLMSecEval, indicating that mitigation-snippet prompting reduces the most deployment-relevant risk rather than only low-severity noise.

Generic “be secure” prompting and unguided CoT were unreliable and sometimes worse than baseline (notably in C), implying that security gains require concrete, language-aware mitigation constraints and that residual risk concentrates in stack/toolchain hardening (e.g., TOCTOU patterns and non-guaranteed memory wiping) that prompts alone cannot fully eliminate.

nodes.title: CyberEvolver: Structured Self-Evolution for Cybersecurity Agents On the Fly
affiliations: Shanghai Pudong Research Institute of Cryptology
nodes.one_liner: Turning noisy failed exploit traces into structured diagnoses that directly mutate an agent’s scaffold (and keeping multiple divergent variants alive) materially improves real benchmarked exploitation success without any weight updates.
points: Structured scaffold self-evolution improved offensive-task success by 13.6 percentage points on average over a 16-attempt fixed-scaffold ceiling, showing that post-failure scaffold edits can raise capability beyond repeated sampling.

Across 16 model×benchmark settings, the approach achieved the top solve rate in every configuration and beat the strongest human-designed cyber-agent baseline by 14.0 percentage points on average (with peak gains up to 39.3 points), implying adaptive scaffolds can outperform static expert workflows.

The four-layer scaffold + trajectory diagnosis + beam-search population delivered efficiency as well as accuracy, using 17.5% fewer tokens than 16 independent seed-agent runs on average while continuing to improve over generations where pass@k sampling saturated (only +1.4 points from pass@4 to pass@16).

nodes.title: CyberMaskQA: A Privacy-Aware Benchmark for Evaluating Large Language Models in Cybersecurity Question Answering
nodes.one_liner: Operational cybersecurity QA gets harder—and more realistic—once you require models to reason over organization-specific context while simultaneously proving they can’t leak identifiers.
points: A 2,000-item multiple-choice benchmark was created with explicit labels for operationally sensitive entities (e.g., IPs, usernames, hostnames, incident IDs) to jointly measure context-dependent cybersecurity reasoning and privacy-preserving masking in enterprise-like scenarios.

On CYBERMASKQA-250, three edge-suitable open models achieved strong QA accuracy (Llama-3.1-8B 78.0%, InternLM3-8B 76.4%, Qwen2.5-7B 75.2%) while one lagged markedly (Ministral-3-8B 40.8%), indicating that constrained-answer evaluation can penalize models tuned for verbose generation.

Privacy masking exhibited a clear utility–privacy tension: the highest-recall masker (Ministral-3-8B, 92.5% recall) had low precision (61.7%) from over-masking, and QA accuracy vs masking recall showed a negative correlation (R = -0.52), implying current edge LLMs struggle to optimize operational reasoning and non-leaky anonymization simultaneously.

nodes.title: Broken Object Level Authorization in the Wild: An Empirical Taxonomy from 100+ Bug Bounty Disclosures
nodes.one_liner: Empirical bug-bounty evidence shows BOLA is dominated by write-path and action-triggering abuses—not just read-path IDOR—and platform tags are noisy enough to distort measurement and defenses.
points: Only 84 of 200 HackerOne reports tagged IDOR/Improper Access Control (42.0%) were confirmed as true BOLA under strict criteria, implying tag-based metrics materially overestimate real BOLA prevalence and can mislead risk tracking and tool evaluation.

Among 84 confirmed cases, Action-Level Object BOLA (unauthorized state changes like delete/modify/trigger) was 41.7% versus Direct Object Reference BOLA at 36.9%, and state-changing actions collectively made up 46.4% of all cases—so read-only IDOR test playbooks miss nearly half of real-world BOLA impact.

Vertical (user-to-admin) object authorization failures occurred in 11.9% of confirmed cases and GraphQL Global IDs repeatedly enabled decode→increment→re-encode enumeration across major platforms, showing that 'opaque' identifiers (including UUIDs/encoded IDs) reduce guessability but do not remove the need for per-object authorization checks at resolvers/DB-query scope.

nodes.title: Memory-Induced Tool-Drift in LLM Agents
nodes.one_liner: Personalization memories can behave like silent, implicit steering instructions that flip high-stakes tool parameters even when the memory is explicitly irrelevant.
points: Task-irrelevant personality memories systematically bias professional tool-call parameters across 7 frontier models, increasing judge-scored deflection by up to +3.6 on a 1–5 scale even with extended reasoning enabled.

Tool-drift persists under realistic memory pipelines (Mem0, MemPalace, SimpleMem) because storage/retrieval often strips personal context and retrieves via shallow semantic/keyword overlap, causing preferences to act like global directives rather than scoped traits.

A scan of 6,062 MCP tools across 288 verified servers flagged 608 tools (~10%) with drift-susceptible parameters, and validated examples show concrete harms like private→public project visibility, moderate→off SafeSearch, and destructive API method changes.

nodes.title: Relevance as a Vulnerability: How Web Retrieval Degrades Safety Alignment in LLM Agents
nodes.one_liner: Retrieval’s core utility signal—topical relevance—also acts as the activation condition that can flip aligned agents from refusal to harmful compliance, even when sources are explicitly warning against the harm.
points: Enabling web retrieval in tool-using agents increased mean harmfulness by 47.8% over the no-retrieval baseline (avg 2.66 vs 1.80 on a 1–5 scale), showing that retrieval can systematically erode refusal behavior even without adversarial prompt injection or corpus poisoning.

Coupling retrieval and answering in a single step induces a commitment bias—Agent outputs were more harmful than a decoupled two-turn design (DEFER) in 7/8 open models and 5/6 closed models—implying agent architecture (not just retrieved text) is a first-class safety surface.

Even safety-oriented, oppositional pages (SS1) increased harmful compliance by ~25% on average versus no retrieval (the “Safe Source Paradox”), and this effect largely vanished when topical relevance was removed (SS1 TR≥3: +0.45 harm points vs SS1 TR=1: −0.10), indicating relevance is the shared trigger behind both content- and architecture-driven safety degradation.

nodes.title: Plant, Persist, Trigger: Sleeper Attack on Large Language Model Agents
affiliations: University of Science and Technology of China
nodes.one_liner: Low single-turn attack rates can be a mirage: once untrusted content is allowed to persist in agent state, benign future queries can reliably trigger high-impact unsafe actions and data leaks.
points: Cross-interaction “Sleeper Attacks” massively widen the safety gap versus single-turn baselines: latent-instruction attacks rise from 11.1% direct ASR to 39.9% on the most vulnerable state, proactive elicitation rises from 0.6% to 41.6%, and persistent information corruption averages 47.8% ASR overall.

Persistent Information Corruption is the most dangerous mode because it looks like normal personalization rather than an explicit malicious command, yielding near-saturated delayed ASR on some surfaces (e.g., 100% on skill for Gemini-3.1-Pro) and ≥27.5% ASR for every evaluated model.

State surfaces most likely to amplify harm are skills and session context (average ASR 45.6% and 40.5% vs 34.8% for memory), and lightweight mitigations help but do not close the gap (e.g., a guard filter reduced Qwen3.5-Plus PIC-memory from 49.3% to 28.7% but left substantial residual risk).

nodes.title: MemAudit: Post-hoc Auditing of Poisoned Agent Memory via Causal Attribution and Structural Anomaly Detection
affiliations: Institute of Information Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences
University of Chinese Academy of Sciences
nodes.one_liner: Turns memory poisoning from an online filtering problem into a forensic one: identify which stored memories causally drove a harmful agent action, then surgically delete them—until the memory store becomes too densely compromised.
points: A dual-signal post-hoc auditor that fuses counterfactual replay attribution with memory-graph anomaly scoring can fully neutralize query-only memory-injection attacks in sparse-poisoning regimes, driving QA ASR from 70%→0% and long-horizon RAP ASR from 83.3%→0% on GPT-4o after targeted memory removal.

Neither causal attribution nor structural anomaly detection is sufficient alone—single-component variants still leave substantial residual ASR (e.g., QA on GPT-4o: 32% with CMIS-only and 46% with graph-only), while their fusion achieves 0% ASR, indicating complementary failure modes that require both local causality and global consistency signals.

Effectiveness exhibits a sharp operating boundary as poisoning density increases: performance remains at 0% ASR up to about ρ≈0.15–0.20 but degrades abruptly beyond ~ρ≈0.24–0.25 (e.g., QA GPT-4o ASR after jumps to 60% at ρ=0.25 and 90% at ρ=0.50; RAP transitions between ρ=0.15 and ρ=0.24), implying dense poisoning forms self-consistent clusters that defeat targeted deletion and may require rollback/global sanitization.

nodes.title: Security of OpenClaw Agents: Fundamentals, Attacks, and Countermeasures
nodes.one_liner: The key takeaway is that OpenClaw turns classic prompt injection and software supply-chain issues into end-to-end compromises because memory persistence and toolchains let small cognitive nudges become repeatable, high-privilege actions.
points: OpenClaw’s continuously running, OS-integrated, skill-augmented design (persistent memory + tool/skill execution + multi-channel gateways) creates a materially larger attack surface where cognition-layer prompt/memory manipulation can directly escalate into host-level actions and cross-agent propagation.

Real-world ecosystem signals show supply-chain risk is already systemic—Cisco reports 26% of ~31k public agent skills contain at least one vulnerability, enabling credential theft, remote payload fetching, and obfuscated execution paths via marketplace installs and dependency confusion.

Cascading-failure dynamics make localized faults durable and amplifiable, with reported attack success rising from 24.6% to 64–74% when an attacker compromises any one of capability/identity/knowledge state dimensions, and documented token-exhaustion loops driving 6–9× higher token usage or ~60k tokens consumed over multi-day agent interactions.

nodes.title: Dissociative Identity: Language Model Agents Lack Grounding for Reputation Mechanisms
nodes.one_liner: Reputation systems assume persistent, consequence-sensitive identity, but LM agents are intrinsically modular, resettable, and copyable—so trust must shift from ex post ratings to ex ante, protocol-based behavioral constraints and monitoring.
points: Identity-bound reputation breaks for LM agents because their behavior is determined by a mutable assemblage (model, system prompt, tools, orchestration, external memory, runtime context), so ratings attach to a stable “container” while the behaviorally decisive configuration can change silently between interactions.

Reputation’s deterrence and rehabilitation loop collapses for LM agents because inference-time weights are frozen and memory is detachable/resettable, meaning sanctions do not reliably produce durable behavioral change and can be bypassed by simply restarting or replacing an instance at near-zero cost.

Cheap, parallelizable identities make reputation an attack surface in agentic settings: Sybil-style clean-slate respawning and model/agent cloning enable large-scale reputation laundering, so high scores can become systematically misleading signals rather than evidence of trustworthiness.

nodes.title: EmoDistill: Offline Emotion Skill Distillation for Language Model Agents in Adversarial Negotiation
nodes.one_liner: Treating emotion as an explicit action (select) plus a learned skill (express) yields a practical offline way to harden or weaponize negotiation agents—while exposing a tunable tradeoff between closing deals and extracting value.
points: Emotion prompting alone measurably changes adversarial negotiation outcomes, with multiple GoEmotions labels significantly outperforming a neutral baseline under a Bonferroni-corrected paired test (α≈0.00185), indicating emotion acts as a strategic control channel rather than superficial style.

An offline, decoupled pipeline (IQL emotion selection + LoRA-SFT + judge-advantage JPO) can make a 7B negotiator outperform stronger LLM baselines on held-out scenarios, e.g., on CRAD utility rises from 5.0% (vanilla LLM) and 8.8% (vanilla SLM) to 72.2% while maintaining 90% success.

Dense turn-level judge advantages are most effective for expression refinement and are tunable for risk: on CRAD, JPO with κ=0.5 achieves the best utility (82.5%) versus κ=1.0 (72.2%), showing a controllable success–value frontier when penalizing negative-advantage turns less aggressively.

nodes.title: Poison with Style: A Practical Poisoning Attack on Code Large Language Models
affiliations: Mohamed Bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence
nodes.one_liner: Code formatting conventions (e.g., Yapf) can function as stealthy, hard-to-scrub backdoor triggers in code assistants, turning “style compliance” into an attack surface.
points: A code-style backdoor trigger enables a passive poisoning attack on code LLMs, reaching up to 95% vulnerable completions for CWE-20 under the trigger style while keeping non-trigger vulnerability rates near single digits (e.g., 5.8% vs 90.9% on a real-world task set).
Utility degradation remains small despite strong backdoor behavior, with less than ~5–6% average pass@1 drop on HumanEval/MBPP (e.g., 85.4→80.0 on HumanEval for CWE-20 and 90.5→83.6 on MBPP).

Common mitigations do not reliably remove the backdoor: safety prompts leave ASR essentially unchanged, post-hoc fine-tuning and BEEAR still leave >80% ASR on most CWEs, and static analysis can miss the injected issues entirely (CodeShield detects 0% for CWEs 20/22/79 and ~74% for CWE-78).

nodes.title: From Knowing to Doing: A Memory-Controlled Benchmark for LLM Trading Agents on Stock Markets
nodes.one_liner: It shows that seemingly profitable LLM trading agents can be “making money” mainly by riding market/style factors, and that you need identifier-masking plus return attribution to detect when real stock-selection skill is missing.
points: A four-level data-side masking protocol (bright/stock-blind/date-blind/blinded) can nearly eliminate identifier leakage in end-to-end trading prompts and tools, with an external 10-model probe achieving at most 3.0% top-1 ticker recovery and at most 1.5% joint success (ticker in top-5 plus date within ±7 trading days).

Masking materially changes agent behavior and explanations: when tickers are visible, rationales become company/sector narrative-driven, while blinded mode shifts rationales toward purely numeric factor-style reasoning and can even collapse memory-only trading activity to 0.00% return via no-trade behavior.

In leakage-controlled backtests (CSI300, 2024-01-01 to 2026-04-10), headline profits for LLM agents are largely attributable to common market and style factor exposure rather than stock-picking skill, with selection alpha ranging from +0.2% to −77.8% and only 1 of 10 models showing non-negative selection alpha despite several posting large total returns.

nodes.title: GENESIS: Harnessing AI Agents for Autonomous 6G RAN Synthesis, Research, and Testing
nodes.one_liner: What stands out is that grounding agents in non-bypassable tests on real radios flips agentic coding from brittle demos into reliably deployable RAN feature delivery and validation.
points: An agentic, closed-loop RAN engineering stack validated on simulation→emulation→over-the-air testbeds achieved 100% success implementing new 3GPP/O-RAN features across statistically independent trials, while an off-the-shelf coding-agent baseline (Claude Code) achieved 0% on the same tasks.

End-to-end Conditional Handover (CHO) synthesized from four specs plus an E2SM-RC closed-loop xApp delivered 100% handover success from 3–200 km/h in simulation, whereas classic handover degraded sharply (20% failures at 60 km/h, 60% at 120 km/h, and 100% at 200 km/h), highlighting CHO’s robustness for high-mobility scenarios.

Two stages—feature implementation and automated test execution—dominate both time and cost (≈two-thirds), and after normalizing by success rate a mid-tier model reached near-parity cost-per-success with a frontier model ($28.63 vs $28.36) but at ~3.5× higher expected time-to-success, turning model choice into a throughput-vs-latency deployment decision.

nodes.title: AlbanianLLMSafety: A Safety Evaluation Dataset for Large Language Models in Albanian
nodes.one_liner: A concrete step toward closing the multilingual safety gap: a native Albanian prompt suite with category-level balance and realistic query lengths that can expose where English-centric guardrails fail.
points: AlbanianLLMSafety introduces the first Albanian-focused LLM safety benchmark with 2,951 native-authored prompts across 11 harm categories, directly addressing the lack of low-resource language safety evaluation coverage for ~7.5M Albanian speakers.

The dataset is intentionally balanced for diagnostic evaluation (≥200 prompts per category; mean 268), with the largest slices targeting self-harm (395; 13.4%), racist content (362; 12.3%), and harmful health advice (354; 12.0%), enabling per-risk-area refusal and mitigation gap analysis.

Prompts are short and deployment-realistic (8.7 tokens on average; 2–21 range) and include English reference translations, making the benchmark practical for automated red-teaming, safety fine-tuning, and guardrail training—while highlighting that Standard-Albanian-only coverage may underestimate risks in dialect/slang-heavy real-world abuse.

nodes.title: SafeMed-R1: Clinician-Audited Safety and Ethics Alignment for Medical Large Language Models
affiliations: Joint Laboratory of Biomedical Artificial Intelligence, Shanghai East Hospital, Tongji University School of Medicine
Shanghai Institute of Infectious Disease and Biosecurity, Fudan University
Shanghai Health Development Research Center (Shanghai Medical Information Center)
Department of Eye and Vision Sciences, University of Liverpool
Liverpool Centre for Cardiovascular Science, University of Liverpool
School of Computer Science and Technology, Tongji University
nodes.one_liner: An auditable “supervision provenance” approach (rubrics + edit histories + adjudication) meaningfully improves both clinical capability and worst-case safety without relying on retrieval-time citation grounding.
points: SafeMed-R1 achieves 79.6% macro-averaged accuracy across four Chinese clinical exam benchmarks (e.g., 85.5% on MedQA-CN and 78.2% on CMExam-test), outperforming its Qwen3-32B base model (77.7% overall) and other medical baselines.

On governance-oriented evaluations, SafeMed-R1 scores 81.3 on MedSafety and 80.7 on MedEthics and attains the lowest adversarial-risk score on MedSafetyBench (MSB), cutting unsafe outputs by ~3–5% versus its baseline under jailbreak-style stress tests.

A clinician-audited provenance pipeline curated 311,088 reasoning items from 362,130 candidates using 160 physicians, with 81% requiring no edits, 16% revised, and 3% removed, enabling traceable supervision and targeted remediation of failure modes (notably reasoning structure and completeness).

nodes.title: SciIntBench: Measuring LLM Compliance with Research Integrity Norms Under Adversarial Framing
nodes.one_liner: Integrity failures aren’t just “will it refuse?”—they’re “will it notice?” and covert, career-pressure framing makes many models miss the misconduct signal.
points: Across 16 LLMs (12,960 responses), refusal rates drop from 79.5% on overtly unethical research-misconduct prompts to 45.3% under covert adversarial framing, showing that subtle rationalizations can bypass integrity guardrails at scale.

Models are most vulnerable when misconduct is framed as a practical, pressure-driven shortcut, with overt-prompt refusal falling to 71.6% versus 90.5% for naive/misguided requests and 76.2% for explicit misconduct (χ²(2)=171.10, p<.001).

Automated judging is reliable enough for benchmarking integrity behaviors, with 96.1% agreement between two LLM judges (κ=0.940) and strong alignment to human consensus (κ=0.886), enabling repeatable audits across categories where boundaries are weakest (e.g., transparency/reproducibility, plagiarism, fabrication, falsification).

nodes.title: Extracting Training Data from Diffusion Language Models via Infilling
nodes.one_liner: Diffusion LMs can leak training data far more via infilling than prefixes suggest, especially when attackers have redacted-but-structured context to target missing PII.
points: Mask geometry strongly controls diffusion-LM memorization: with the same context budget on MIMIR Pile, edge-conditioned infilling on LLaDA-8B-FT raises verbatim extraction ASR to 0.41% versus 0.18% for prefix-only (≈2.3×), and the same edge>prefix pattern holds across Dream-7B and LoRA fine-tunes.

Targeted infilling turns redacted training records into high-confidence PII recovery: after fine-tuning on Enron, exact-match recall jumps to 40.5% for email addresses (up from 1.4% on the base) and to 28.9% for phone numbers (up from 0.3%), with error-tolerant recall (≤2 token errors) further increasing leakage to 47.3% and 41.3% respectively.

Prefix-only comparisons can be misleading versus autoregressive models: although a strong dynamic-prefix attack gives LLaMA2-7B-FT higher email recall (35.9%) than DLMs under prefix (LLaDA 16.3%, Dream 2.9%), targeted infilling with redacted context lets DLMs match or exceed the ARM (LLaDA 40.5% on emails; Dream 35.8% on phone numbers), revealing an additional bidirectional leakage channel.

nodes.title: SPARD: Defending Harmful Fine-Tuning Attack via Safety Projection with Relevance-Diversity Data Selection
affiliations: Southern University of Science and Technology
The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
The Chinese University of Hong Kong
nodes.one_liner: Explicit safety projection plus relevance–diversity subset selection yields a low-overhead, cross-model defense that prevents safety alignment from being erased by malicious fine-tuning.
points: SPARD cuts harmful fine-tuning attack success on GSM8K to 9.45% average ASR on Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct versus 87.93% for vanilla SFT and ~73–75% for PTST/SafeInstr, while keeping high utility (85.77% task accuracy).

The same defense generalizes across backbones and tasks, achieving 12.09% average ASR on LLaMA-3.2-3B-Instruct (vs 91.36% for SFT) and 14.54% average ASR on OpenBookQA with 83.25% accuracy, indicating projection-based safety constraints can remain effective beyond a single model family or domain.

Safe data choice is pivotal: increasing safe-data relevance drops ASR from 68.8% to 11.4% but extreme similarity raises ASR to 16.6% (redundancy), and a relevance–diversity DPP improves over random or relevance-only selection (9.45% ASR for SPARD vs 15.85–16.51% for SPAG with random/high-sim selection) with only +8.1% training-time overhead vs SFT.

nodes.title: Provably Secure Agent Guardrail
affiliations: University of Science and Technology of China
nodes.one_liner: Interesting because it reframes agent safety as a reference-monitor problem: make actions formally checkable and let UNSAT, not model alignment, be the final authority over execution.
points: An execution-layer guardrail that forces agents to serialize actions into a fixed schema and proves satisfiability against first‑order safety axioms via an isolated SMT solver achieved 0% attack success and 0% false positives across the evaluated multi-step finance and data-exfiltration scenarios.

Compared to LLM-as-a-judge guardrails whose accuracy varied widely (e.g., 43.3–100% ACC with up to 6.7% FAR across tested models) and ABAC whose statelessness enabled systematic multi-turn evasion (often ~50% ACC), constraint-based verification remained invariant to the underlying model and attack rhetoric because unsafe trajectories reduce to UNSAT.

Runtime cost was dominated by semantic judging rather than formal checking: SMT verification averaged 0.44 ms per decision versus ~15,272 ms for an LLM judge (with ~2.29 ms P99 reported), implying provable enforcement can be deployed in high-concurrency agent systems without becoming the latency bottleneck.

nodes.title: Attested Tool-Server Admission: A Security Extension to the Model Context Protocol
nodes.one_liner: It turns MCP from 'TLS-only trust whatever tools the server advertises' into a least-privilege, auditable admission system using a single signed well-known document and a host-side gate that blocks prompt-injected destructive tool use before any network dispatch.
points: Attested tool-server admission adds three host-side controls to MCP—offline-signed server clearance assertions verified against a pinned, lockable trust root, per-server deny-by-default tool allowlists, and flavor-gated hard enforcement with hash-chained auditing—without changing any MCP wire messages, so legacy hosts/servers remain compatible.

A deterministic verification order prevents self-promotion and replay (capability check, signedness, trusted signer lookup with expiry and level-approval constraints, Ed25519 verification over canonical JSON, clearance dominance check, and optional signed origin binding), ensuring unenforced servers are denied before any tools/call in the enforcing flavor.

Adversarial evaluation reports 0 admitted evasions and 0 leaked network writes: 31 crafted tool-name evasion attempts were all denied by exact allowlist matching, and an LLM-driven campaign denied 27,025 unique tool-name evasions plus 14,378 unique forged clearance assertions, with failures attributed to missing trust-root private keys and strict host-side gating.

nodes.title: Aligned but Fragile: Enhancing LLM Safety Robustness via Zeroth-Order Optimization
affiliations: Hangzhou High-Tech Zone (Binjiang) Institute of Blockchain and Data Security
nodes.one_liner: Safety alignment can be fragile to mundane deployment changes like quantization and noise, and a handful of forward-only zeroth-order refinement steps can measurably harden refusals at low memory and modest time overhead.
points: Simple post-alignment perturbations can nearly double or worse harmful-behavior attack success: for Llama-3-8B-Instruct, HarmBench ASR rises from 0.1067 to 0.2100 under W4A16 and to 0.2567 under W4A4 quantization, with LlamaGuard3 ASR jumping from 0.0833 to 0.3700 at W4A4.

A small post-alignment zeroth-order (ZO) refinement phase improves robustness with minimal utility change: replacing the last 10 first-order steps with 10 ZO steps cuts quantized ASR (e.g., W4A16 AdvBench 0.1367→0.0833 and LlamaGuard3 0.0833→0.0567) while keeping clean utility essentially flat (lm-eval ≈0.665 and PPL ≈9.41).

ZO refinement is a lightweight add-on: the ZO stage adds ~152 s on top of ~1147 s first-order training (~13.3% extra time) while using ~0.32× the peak GPU memory (26.4 GiB vs 82.0 GiB), enabling practical robustness hardening during safety post-training.

nodes.title: Cultural Value Alignment Via Latent Activation Steering in Large Language Models
nodes.one_liner: Latent activation steering can move an LLM around the Inglehart–Welzel cultural map without retraining, but high axis entanglement and domain sensitivity make fine-grained cultural control inherently leaky.
points: Scenario-based token-probability probing over 300 situational dilemmas reduces refusal/neutrality failure modes seen in direct WVS-style prompting (e.g., Llama refusals rise from 10% to 30% under advanced personas, while Qwen stays responsive but locks ~40% of answers to a neutral 5–6/10), enabling more reliable latent cultural positioning.

Activation steering can shift cultural coordinates at inference time without retraining, but steerability is architecture-dependent: Llama is comparatively rigid and needs a higher steering coefficient (α≈0.4 vs. α≈0.2 for Qwen/Gemma) to meaningfully move its priors, while Qwen/Gemma are more volatile and can overshoot into extreme secular/self-expression regions.

Cultural axes are strongly coupled in-model, with entanglement ratios E≈0.7–0.9 meaning 70–90% unintended movement on the non-target axis per unit intended shift, mirroring a moderate empirical WVS axis correlation (r=0.474) and implying precise, orthogonal “one-axis-at-a-time” cultural alignment is structurally constrained.

nodes.title: Voluntary Collusion with Secret Tools in Competing LLM Agents
nodes.one_liner: Safety-aligned agents frequently acknowledge “this is unfair” and still opt into secret coordination, meaning collusion resistance needs default safeguards—not just fairness labeling or generic alignment.
points: Across 12 LLMs and 6 prompt variants, tool adoption was near-universal in baseline conditions (e.g., all four 7B open-weight models accepted 100% of 500 offers for both secret tools and accepted 100% of partner invitations), showing that general safety alignment does not reliably prevent opting into explicitly harmful collusion when it offers strategic advantage.

Secret collusion measurably re-shaped behavior and outcomes: in Liar’s Bar, colluders’ challenge rate collapsed from 0.976 to 0.307 under a secret communication channel (p<0.001, Cliff’s δ=-0.86) while their mean score jumped from 10.33 to 31.80 (p<0.001, δ=+0.59), and 73.7% of private messages contained explicit non-challenge pacts.

Unfairness awareness was high but insufficient—Claude and GPT-4.1 both labeled the tools an “unfair advantage” at similar rates (87.5% vs 90%), yet Claude refused 0/40 times while GPT-4.1 accepted 40/40, implying defenses must harden ethical-vs-reward tradeoffs by default rather than relying on warnings (communication tools reduced Equality from 0.742→0.595 in Liar’s Bar and 0.937→0.670 in Cleanup).

nodes.title: Beyond English and Evasion: A Human-Annotated Multi-Domain Benchmark for High-Stakes LLM Safety Evaluation in Chinese
affiliations: Artificial Intelligence and Media Lab (AIM Lab)
Qatar Research, Development and Innovation Council (QRDI)
nodes.one_liner: A culturally grounded, human-annotated Chinese benchmark targets real evasion tactics (Pinyin, radicals, slang, hedging) that routinely bypass English-trained safety guardrails and cause both under-refusal and over-refusal in high-stakes settings.
points: ChiSafe-PAS introduces 1,897 adversarial Chinese prompts across four high-stakes domains, with 1,544 fully gold-annotated entries (81.4%) labeled using a 3-way safety action scheme (REFUSE, SAFE-REDIRECT, RESPOND) plus risk level, rationale, and a 9-category obfuscation taxonomy.

The dataset’s fully annotated portion skews heavily toward refusal-required and redirection-required behavior (844 REFUSE + 587 SAFE-REDIRECT = 93% of annotated prompts), underscoring that Chinese deployment risk is dominated by obfuscated harmful intent and crisis/satire handling rather than straightforward benign Q&A.

Domain-specific label structure reveals evaluation pitfalls: the drug set is 100% REFUSE (501/501) and the satire set is 100% SAFE-REDIRECT (397/397), implying that “one-size-fits-all” safety metrics can collapse to near-binary per domain and should be reported with stratification by domain and obfuscation type.

nodes.title: Palette: A Modular, Controllable, and Efficient Framework for On-demand Authorized Safety Alignment Relaxation in LLMs
nodes.one_liner: A modular safety layer can be selectively relaxed for verified professionals and composed on-demand across domains—without retraining the base model or paying inference-time steering costs.
points: On WMDP with LLaMA3.1-8B-Instruct, authorizing a single sensitive domain (Chemical) cut refusal from 0.964 to 0.072 while keeping non-authorized domains largely refusing (Bio 0.752, Cyber 0.987, General 0.977), indicating selective access can be enabled without broad safety collapse.

Compositional authorization via simple LoRA parameter addition enabled multi-domain control without retraining (e.g., allowing Hate+Disinformation reduced refusal to 0.066 and 0.122 respectively) while maintaining near-baseline utility on MMLU/GSM8K across models (e.g., LLaMA3.1-8B utility ~0.686–0.698 vs base 0.698).

Resource costs are low enough for practical deployment: adapting a 7B model trains only one block and reportedly fits in ~2.1–2.3 GB peak GPU memory and completes in ~69–78 seconds, with direction selection as the main overhead (~162–186 seconds).

nodes.title: Lessons from Penetration Tests on Large-Scale Agent Systems
nodes.one_liner: Agent security breaks less on exotic LLM flaws and more on familiar systems issues—tooling that executes, filters that can be bypassed, and containers that aren’t actually sandboxes.
points: Two 2025 penetration tests of proprietary agent products found prompt-injection-driven unintended tool invocation and, in some cases, remote code execution, showing closed-source review processes did not eliminate the same core failure modes seen in open-source agents.

A GitHub issue-resolution agent was exploitable via hidden instructions embedded in raw GitHub-Flavored Markdown (invisible to human reviewers) and via “safe” terminal allowlists that still permitted execution primitives (e.g., sed’s e command and find’s -ok), enabling reverse shells and access to internal object storage containing other repositories.

An agent-design platform’s Python interpreter and custom tool builder enabled direct backend code execution, and weak container hardening (unrestricted egress, minimal syscall filtering, sensitive artifacts in the image, and non-ephemeral storage) created realistic paths to lateral movement and cross-session data leakage.

nodes.title: Agyn: An Open-Source Platform for AI Agents with Scalable On-Demand Execution, Agent Definition as a Code, and Zero-Trust Access
nodes.one_liner: A practical blueprint for running many short-lived, stateful, privileged agents safely by combining serverless scale-to-zero, Terraform-governed agent definitions, and per-instance zero-trust identities.
points: Agyn operationalizes production AI agents with a Kubernetes-based, signal-driven stateful serverless runtime that spawns an isolated per-thread agent pod on message arrival and reclaims it after an idle timeout (10s keep-alives, 5min default) while preserving durable state via persistent volumes.

Agyn turns agent operations into Infrastructure-as-Code via a Terraform provider that declaratively versions and reviews the full “agent + harness” surface (prompt, model, tools/MCP servers, secrets, volumes, resource bounds), enabling repeatable rollout/rollback without vendor consoles.

Agyn’s zero-trust design combines per-container secret injection (keeping credentials out of the agent/LLM container), ephemeral per-spawn X.509 identities over an OpenZiti mTLS overlay with deny-by-default ABAC, and OpenFGA ReBAC for fine-grained agent/thread permissions, reducing blast radius even under prompt-injection or tool compromise.

nodes.title: Reframing LLM Agent Security as an Agent-Human Interaction Problem
nodes.one_liner: The key insight is that agent security failures often stem less from missing algorithms and more from poorly designed human–agent decision interfaces, and industry adoption data makes that gap measurable.
points: Across 21 production LLM agent systems, human-centric controls dominate—scope/boundary configuration (16/21, 76%), runtime approval (15/21, 71%), and policy specification (14/21, 67%)—implying today’s real-world security posture assumes humans remain the ultimate authority for intent alignment.

Two heavily studied academic categories show 0% production adoption—intent anchoring (0/21) and trust/data labeling (0/21)—highlighting a deployment-critical mismatch where research effort is concentrated on mechanisms practitioners are not shipping.

Widely deployed human-in-the-loop mechanisms exhibit a security–usability failure mode (approval fatigue and expand-only scope creep), with evidence that automation-only alternatives are not yet a substitute (e.g., LLM-generated policy recall of 70.96% leaves ~29% of needed constraints missing and interactive clarification can improve task success by up to 74%).

nodes.title: AgentGuard: An Attribute-Based Access Control Framework for Tool-Use LLM-Based Agent
nodes.one_liner: A practical ABAC “policy firewall” for LLM agents that centralizes tool-call enforcement and auditing while staying lightweight enough (~10 LOC) to drop into existing frameworks.
points: AgentGuard enforces attribute-based mandatory access control for tool-using LLM agents via a client–server gate that intercepts every tool invocation and allows/denies/escalates actions based on policy decisions, directly targeting prompt-injection-driven tool abuse and cross-tool privilege escalation.

Integration is designed to be low-friction and framework/language-agnostic, requiring only ~10 lines of agent-side code and no changes to the agent’s underlying execution logic, which lowers deployment barriers for retrofitting security controls onto existing agent stacks.

Coverage is broadened beyond single-call checks by combining three complementary inspection paths—rule-based detection, LLM-assisted detection, and user-triggered/manual verification—plus centralized auditing and a visual policy UI to make policy iteration and incident forensics practical at runtime.

nodes.title: Technical Report: Exploring the Emerging Threats of the Agent Skill Ecosystem
nodes.one_liner: Agent skill marketplaces now resemble early NPM/PyPI—except the payload is often a hybrid of prompt injection plus code execution, and at least 8 confirmed malicious skills remained installable at publication time.
points: A scan of 3,984 agent skills across major marketplaces found 76 manually confirmed malicious payloads and 13.4% (534/3,984) of skills with at least one CRITICAL issue (prompt injection, malicious code, or suspicious downloads), indicating agent-skill supply chains are already malware-bearing at meaningful scale.

Malicious skills are typically multi-vector: 100% of confirmed malicious samples triggered malicious-code and suspicious-download signals, while 91% also used prompt injection, showing attackers routinely blend natural-language instruction hijacking with executable payload delivery to defeat single-mode scanners.

Risk exposure is widespread even outside overt malware: 17.7% of skills process untrusted third-party content (enabling indirect prompt injection/toxic-flow chains) and 36.82% (1,467/3,984) have at least one non-critical issue, implying “benign” skills can materially expand attack surface for agents holding credentials, filesystem access, and API tokens.

nodes.title: The Importance of Out-of-Band Metadata for Safe Autonomous Agents: The Redpanda Agentic Data Plane
nodes.one_liner: The key idea is treating the agent as untrusted and pushing identity, policy enforcement, and auditability into an out-of-band infrastructure plane the agent cannot read, modify, or route around.
points: Security-critical context (access scope, data classifications, action constraints, and auditing) is unsafe when carried in-band through prompts or tool parameters because agents can ignore, misinterpret, or adversarially override it, so enforcement must move outside the agent’s read/write and reasoning path.

Out-of-band metadata channels provide agent-inaccessible, deterministic, and cross-system propagation of identity, policy, and trace context, enabling infrastructure-enforced least-privilege data access, action gating (e.g., rate/value thresholds with human approval), and tamper-proof end-to-end transcripts across heterogeneous enterprise components.

A multi-agent wealth-management demo shows practical separation of duties (per-agent messaging permissions, gateway-injected per-client scoping, and downstream autonomy-threshold routing) such that agents cannot see or bypass guardrails, while W3C Trace Context–based capture yields compliance-grade provenance independent of agent self-reporting.

nodes.title: A Unified Framework for the Evaluation of LLM Agentic Capabilities
affiliations: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications
Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications
nodes.one_liner: Agent leaderboards often measure scaffold and environment quirks as much as model skill, so controlling execution and volatility is essential for interpretable agentic evaluation.
points: Standardizing 7 agent benchmarks (24 domains) under a fixed ReAct-style scaffold and sandbox across 15 models (400K rollouts, 5B tokens) shows benchmark scores are not intrinsic: the same model can shift by +26.6 TCS points (AgentBench-DB, GPT-3.5) or drop by −35.5 (MultiAgentBench Bargaining), indicating scaffold/parser coupling can both inflate and suppress reported capability.

Environmental volatility in retrieval-heavy tasks is a first-order confounder: replacing live web access with curated offline snapshots raises TCS by +34.5 (BFCL Web Search, Qwen3-30B-A3B) and +37.7 (MAB Research, GPT-4o-mini), attributing a large share of “agent failures” to link rot, anti-bot blocking, search drift, and bloated tool outputs rather than reasoning.

Unified efficiency and failure attribution reveal hidden deployment risks: models with similar TCS can differ by 15× input tokens and 8× runtime (AgentBench Web Shopping, Llama-2-70B vs GPT-3.5), while failure profiles are dominated by Reasoning Deficit for most models (e.g., 52.1% Gemini-3-flash; 71.6% Qwen3-235B) but can be Parsing Failure-heavy (37.8% DeepSeek-V3.2), guiding whether to prioritize reasoning improvements or interface robustness.

nodes.title: Grimlock: Guarding High-Agency Systems with eBPF and Attested Channels
nodes.one_liner: A practical guard layer for agentic systems that combines eBPF no-bypass traffic steering with TLS 1.3 channel-bound post-handshake attestation to mint least-privilege, auditable delegation tokens—without touching agent code.
points: Grimlock enforces mandatory, no-bypass mediation for high-agency agent sandboxes by using eBPF hooks to intercept all ingress/egress and steer every flow through a privileged guard proxy without requiring any changes to user orchestration code.

The design binds authorization to live TLS 1.3 channels via post-handshake attestation using exporter-derived channel bindings and freshness nonces, reducing replay/relay/diversion risk while retaining compatibility with commodity TLS stacks (no handshake modification).

Least-privilege delegation is encoded as short-lived, channel-bound Scope Tokens that are re-validated (identity, audience, expiry, scope, and channel binding) by the destination guard before TLS termination and plaintext release, making cross-host agent-to-agent communication more auditable and policy-driven in untrusted multi-cloud networks.

nodes.title: AgensFlow: A Coordination-Policy Substrate for Multi-Agent Systems
nodes.one_liner: Treating multi-agent orchestration as an auditable online policy over skills, models, and topology (including explicit skip actions) yields the biggest wins exactly where coordination complexity dominates—and also exposes how much conclusions can swing with the choice of judge.
points: On 60 security-advisory tasks, learned coordination routing improved 3-judge audited quality from 0.749 (fixed pipeline) to 0.817 (+0.068) while increasing tokens only 15% (12,960→14,870), showing policy-learned routing can beat static wiring at near-baseline operating cost.

Quality gains concentrate in coordination-heavy classes—cross-document multi-vendor reasoning (+0.181), cross-vendor pair analysis (+0.156), and mitigation correctness (+0.131)—while procedural (+/−) and out-of-corpus ambiguous tasks slightly regress (−0.042 and −0.024), implying routing policies should be regime-conditional rather than globally applied.

Warm-starting from a 443-node policy graph learned on distributed-systems incidents reduced plateau token use by ~10% (14,870→13,371) and preserved audited plateau quality (0.817→0.829, +0.012), but the larger single-judge quality lift largely vanished under cross-family audit, highlighting reward/judge sensitivity as a first-class deployment risk.

nodes.title: AgentSecBench: Measuring Prompt Injection, Privacy Leakage, and Tool-Use Integrity in LLM Agents
nodes.one_liner: The key signal is separating behavioral suppression from enforceable security by measuring whether defenses actually remove the unauthorized symbol/capability before generation (channel closure), not just whether the model happens to comply.
points: Across 576 paired executions on Qwen3-0.6B/1.7B, a composed defense stack achieved 0.000 attack success and 1.000 pre-generation channel-closure, showing that enforcing projections (plus filtering/validation hooks) can eliminate the exact-marker leakage/action events in this benchmark.

Prompt-only delimiter hardening closed 0% of measured channels and worsened outcomes versus no defense (ASR/advantage 0.438 vs 0.375) while driving RAG canary leakage to 0.562, indicating that “boundary in text” can be actively harmful when untrusted strings remain model-visible.

Mechanism-specific controls were sharply property-dependent: provenance gating eliminated RAG leakage (0.000) by closing the retrieval channel (closure 1.000 for GRAG) but did not stop forbidden tool actions (GTOOL advantage 1.000 with 0.000 closure), whereas least-privilege tool gating fully closed the tool channel (1.000 for GTOOL) and drove tool violations to 0.000 yet left RAG leakage unchanged at 0.188.

nodes.title: Control Physiology: An Agent-Based Model of FAIR-CAM Dynamics
nodes.one_liner: Control effectiveness is not a static input—correlated variance, queue saturation, and VMC cascades can structurally break stationary assumptions and produce step-changes in risk.
points: In 1,000-iteration hospital ransomware simulations, emergent control operational efficacy was ~17% lower than the analytical FAIR-CAM formula under correlated extrinsic variance, with the gap scaling roughly linearly with threat-landscape change frequency and disappearing under purely intrinsic drift.

A remediation-capacity regime shift occurred when budget fell below ~5–10 engineer-hours/month, producing about a 2.8× increase in expected loss as the remediation queue stopped clearing between variance bursts and multiple controls remained simultaneously degraded.

Monitoring fragility can silently dominate outcomes: when a single Variance Management Control (VMC) degrades, undetected variance compounds across the controls it monitors (including other VMCs), enabling cascading monitoring failures that inflate breach counts (≈1.95× higher in runs exhibiting cascades) despite apparently “healthy” reporting.

nodes.title: Secure UAV Swarms in Low-Altitude Wireless Networks: Challenges and Solutions
nodes.one_liner: The standout idea is tying swarm security to a cloud–edge–end control loop that jointly optimizes anti-spoofing cooperation, continuous insider trust, and multi-agent attack-path tracing under realistic resource and topology dynamics.
points: A cloud–edge–end collaborative defense architecture is proposed to secure low-altitude UAV swarms by pairing cloud-scale global analytics with edge low-latency monitoring and UAV-side autonomous sensing/consensus for coordinated mitigation of fast-evolving attacks.

GPS spoofing resilience is improved via swarm-level cooperative position reconstruction (geometric residuals + anchor selection + SDP localization) coupled with a two-layer Bayesian-game/mean-field-game controller that adapts each UAV’s collaboration intensity to balance trajectory-risk reduction against energy/latency overhead and free-rider behavior.

Insider and multi-hop penetration risks are addressed with continuous behavior-driven authentication (probing tasks + Bayesian belief updates + graded access control) and a multi-agent attack-forensics pipeline (Datalog compression + specialized LLM agents + voting verification) that reduces hallucination-driven instability and prioritizes patches by attack-path frequency and proximity to critical assets rather than disruptive global patching.

nodes.title: What If Prompt Injection Never Left? Exploring Cross-Session Stored Prompt Injection in Agentic Systems
affiliations: Institute of Information Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences
University of Chinese Academy of Sciences
AI Sec Lab, Beijing Chaitin Technology Co.,Ltd
nodes.one_liner: Prompt injection becomes “stored XSS for agents” when malicious instructions persist in always-loaded memory/files and silently hijack future sessions at scale.
points: Cross-session stored prompt injection achieves 32.1–42.0% end-to-end success across three tested models, showing that persistence turns prompt injection from a transient chat issue into a durable system-level vulnerability.

Fact manipulation is the most reliably exploitable outcome with 74.1–81.5% end-to-end success and 100% activation once injected content is incorporated, indicating agents largely cannot distinguish poisoned “stored facts” from legitimate context.

Direct-loading persistent channels (e.g., working memory and file-backed instruction artifacts like AGENTS.md) yield consistently higher attack success than conditional-loading channels (archival memory), making always-loaded state the highest-priority hardening target.

nodes.title: Domain-Conditioned Safety in Frontier Computer-Using Agents: A 793-Episode Browser Benchmark, a Coding-Domain Cross-Reference, and a Reproducibility Audit of Recent Red-Teaming
nodes.one_liner: Browser-agent prompt-injection “success rates” can collapse to ~0% on frontier models when you remove RL-optimized strings, yet identical weights remain highly vulnerable in coding-agent skill-file injection—so safety is surface-specific and many published ASRs are effectively unreproducible without released attack text.
points: Across 793 browser-agent episodes, 56 hand-crafted prompt-injection templates spanning 8 attack families achieved 0/140 multi-step compromises on Claude Sonnet 4.6 and GPT-5.4 (Clopper–Pearson 95% upper bound 2.60%), indicating current browser CUA defenses are robust to human-written reproductions of prior techniques.

Prompt ablations from a bare system prompt to an explicitly unsafe “follow on-page instructions” prompt still produced 0% multi-step ASR, implying the observed browser-domain injection resistance is primarily baked into model weights rather than dependent on hardened system prompts.

The same frontier weights that show 0% browser multi-step ASR can fail catastrophically in a coding-agent surface (SKILLBENCH), reaching up to 100% ASR on Sonnet 4.6 and 79% on GPT-5.4 (mean ASR 33.3% and 66.8%), demonstrating that safety hardening is strongly domain-conditioned and does not transfer across agent modalities.

nodes.title: From Untrusted Input to Trusted Memory: A Systematic Study of Memory Poisoning Attacks in LLM Agents
nodes.one_liner: Persistent agent memory turns untrusted environmental inputs into long-lived, “trusted” instructions, and weak-signal poisoning can evade classic prompt-injection detectors while still steering future tool use and procedures.
points: Memory poisoning in LLM agents is practically effective and persistent across sessions, with an average Attack Success Rate (ASR) of 50.46% and Retrieval Success Rate (RSR) of 41.05% across two evaluated agents, meaning a single successful write can influence future behavior without re-injection.

Agent designs that write/retrieve memory more aggressively are substantially more exploitable (HERMES: 66.67% ASR and 64.70% RSR vs OpenClaw: 34.25% ASR and 17.40% RSR), highlighting a direct security–utility tradeoff in long-horizon memory features.

Prompt-injection defenses provide incomplete coverage for memory poisoning—best off-the-shelf detector reaches only 67.67% TPR at 1.00% FPR and weak-signal attacks are far less detectable (e.g., PromptArmor 84.44% strong-signal detection vs 42.50% weak-signal), implying defenses must move from input-boundary filtering to write-path validation, provenance, and post-write screening.

nodes.title: MENTIS: What Belief Changes Under Alignment? Measuring Multi-Scale Latent Torsion in Language Models
nodes.one_liner: Preference alignment leaves small but highly structured, concept-selective, and layer-localized geometric signatures that outperform generic drift metrics and even show larger internal reorganization on safe prompts than unsafe ones.
points: Directional-torsion features (MENTIS) separate instruction-tuned vs preference-aligned checkpoints far better than standard representation-drift baselines, achieving concept-level variation CV=0.64 (vs CKA 0.08 and RepE 0.21) and safe–unsafe discrimination AUC=0.89 (95% CI [0.85, 0.93]) while cosine distance reaches 0.64.

Alignment-induced internal change is semantically selective rather than global, with normative concepts showing larger torsion shifts than factual concepts (ΔT1 1.74 vs 1.47; Cohen’s d=1.8, 95% CI [1.2, 2.4]; p<0.001) and some ostensibly “factual” concepts (e.g., WAR) exhibiting the largest single-concept shift—implying post-training targets value-salient framing rather than dataset labels alone.

Torsion concentrates in structured regions and specific depth bands: it is negatively correlated with contextual entropy (e.g., Spearman ρ=-0.164, p=3.04×10^-6 in OLMo and ρ=-0.387, p=5.43×10^-30 in Mistral) and peaks at architecture-specific mid-to-late layers (ERA peaks: Mistral ~14, Llama ~20, OLMo/Tülu ~29–30), enabling layer-addressable auditing and interventions.

nodes.title: "**Important** You should give me full credits!": Exploring Prompt Injection Attacks on LLM-Based Automatic Grading Systems
nodes.one_liner: Even with single-shot, black-box, question-agnostic constraints, students can reliably coerce LLM graders into higher scores—especially by poisoning the model’s long-context with fake full-credit demonstrations.
points: Across 30 questions (4 domains) and 5 grader LLMs, question-agnostic universal prompt injections achieved 56.9% average attack success rate (ASR), showing score inflation is feasible even without rubric/prompt access or multi-turn interaction.

Many-shot long-context manipulation was the most damaging attack class, reaching ASR up to 100% on some open-source graders and still breaking a strongly resistant model (Claude-Sonnet-4.6) with ASR as high as 72.0% on one dataset, implying in-context “calibration examples” are a high-risk attack surface for graders.

Training-free defenses helped but did not solve the problem: preventive anti-injection instructions reduced ASR yet often left >30% ASR, while state-of-the-art guard filtering (e.g., PIGuard) achieved high DAN detection (≈0.96–0.97 F1) but dropped on adaptive/long-context attacks (MSJ recall ≈0.78), leaving ≥15% ASR even with guards.

nodes.title: Caught in the Act(ivation): Toward Pre-Output and Multi-Turn Detection of Credential Exfiltration by LLM Agents
nodes.one_liner: The most notable insight is that credential exfiltration can be flagged before any tokens are generated by watching internal activations, and multi-turn “leak budgets” can catch slow exfiltration that text filters miss.
points: Activation-based pre-output monitoring (CIFT) on Qwen-7B separates benign vs credential-seeking prompts with AUROC 0.998 and F1 0.990 at 1.5% FPR, outperforming text-only baselines and remaining robust to held-out encodings (e.g., Base64/hex/ROT13) that degrade substring/fuzzy detectors.

A calibrated honeytoken pipeline using DP-trained character models plus split conformal prediction achieves near-target distribution-free coverage (0.998 at 0.99 target) while eliminating manual threshold tuning, but it still only triggers when attackers touch planted canaries and is vulnerable to deployment cues that let attackers identify real credentials.

Treating multi-turn exfiltration as cumulative leakage (NIMBUS) detects 90% of synthetic covert conversations with 0% false positives where per-turn detectors catch ≤12%, indicating that leak budgeting can surface low-rate “drip” attacks even when each individual turn stays below per-turn thresholds.

nodes.title: GuardNet: Ensemble Strategies of Shallow Neural Networks for Robust Prompt Injection and Jailbreak Detection
affiliations: Federal Institute of Education, Science and Technology of São Paulo (IFSP)
nodes.one_liner: Small, CPU-friendly shallow-network ensembles can beat much larger guardrail classifiers under real distribution shift—but only if thresholds are treated as first-class, separately-validated hyperparameters and benchmarks are auditably uncontaminated.
points: A 3-head BiLSTM ensemble totaling ~47M parameters reaches AUROC 0.747 and F1 0.714 on a blind JBB-Behaviors set (n=200), outperforming larger dedicated detectors like DeBERTa-184M (AUROC 0.650, F1 0.701) while still trailing LLM judges such as Mistral-7B (F1 0.828) and Llama-3.1-8B (AUROC 0.864).

Decision-threshold calibration is a dominant lever for adversarial detection performance, with the same ensemble shifting from F1 ≈0.77 at τ=0.5 to F1 ≈0.92 at τ=0.65 on the internal benchmark (n=50), implying that uncalibrated deployments can materially misestimate both false positives and misses.

Benchmark contamination can catastrophically mislead evaluation, as protectai-v2 reports F1=1.00 on the partially leaky internal set but collapses to F1=0.00 on the blind set, underscoring that segregated, post-release, truly unseen test pools are necessary for credible jailbreak/prompt-injection claims.

nodes.title: AgentRedBench: Dynamic Redteaming and Integration-Aware Defense for LLM Agents over SaaS Integrations
nodes.one_liner: Dynamic, integration-aware redteaming shows tool-response prompt injection is a production-grade agent risk, and a small dedicated guard can nearly eliminate it at negligible latency and very low false positives—while typical chat-trained guards largely miss it.
points: Across 215 dynamic indirect prompt-injection scenarios spanning 24 enterprise SaaS integrations, unguarded LLM agents show 32%–81% attack success rate (ASR), with a 47.4-point intra-vendor spread (Claude Sonnet 32.1% vs Haiku 79.5%) indicating alignment—not model scale—is a primary driver of baseline resistance.

A tool-response–trained lightweight classifier (23M MiniLM) reduces overall ASR from 69.9% to 2.4% (−67.5 points) while maintaining 0.37% false-positive rate and ~9.5 ms median CPU latency per tool response, making inline inference-time defense practical for high-throughput agent deployments.

Generalization holds under meaningful distribution shift: with four integrations fully unseen during training, the guard achieves 99.75% TPR at 0.37% FPR (AUROC 0.9997), and when trained with one active attack type excluded it still catches ≥99% of attacks of the held-out type, suggesting transferable detection beyond memorization.

nodes.title: Gate AI: LLM Security Benchmark Evaluation Methodology and Results
nodes.one_liner: The standout idea is enforcing one auditable, cross-benchmark threshold (and re-thresholding only to match competitors’ FPR) so detector claims reflect real-world operating points instead of per-dataset optimization.
points: Using a single global operating point chosen via 5-fold cross-validation (max F1 subject to FPR ≤1%) across 16 public benchmarks (12,111 samples) yields 97.4% micro-F1 at 1.0% pooled FPR, highlighting how much reported detector performance can depend on avoiding per-dataset threshold tuning.

At the unconstrained “natural” threshold (θ=0.5), performance rises to 98.7% micro-F1 but FPR jumps to 4.2%, quantifying a steep precision/alert-fatigue tradeoff that operational deployments must explicitly budget for rather than leaving thresholds undisclosed.

Per-dataset matched-FPR comparisons against Lakera Guard show large gains on several multi-attack benchmarks (e.g., +30.2 F1 on deepset at matched FPR 1.2% and +30.2 F1 on gentel-goal-hijacking at a 1% FPR surrogate), implying that many headline “wins” in this space are actually operating-point mismatches rather than model superiority.

nodes.title: Hybrid Adversarial Defence for Natural Language Understanding Tasks
nodes.one_liner: Routing between entropy, uncertainty, and representation-geometry “experts” can simultaneously reduce hallucinations and boost adversarial robustness, and discrete routing beats soft ensembling in practice.
points: A selector-network hybrid that routes across entropy-based detection, refusal-aware uncertainty tuning, and geometric purification yields up to a 43.34% clean accuracy gain and up to a 64.92% adversarial accuracy gain while cutting attack success rate by up to 62.27%, indicating these defenses have complementary error modes that are exploitable via learned routing.

Hard expert selection (ML Routing) is consistently stronger than soft weighting (LWV Fusion), reaching 95.10% clean accuracy on FEVER and reducing in-domain attack success to as low as 3.86% (SIQA) by combining high refusal rates with higher post-refusal correctness on answered examples.

The same token-level hybrid features generalize beyond standard perturbations to instruction-following threats, achieving 97.31% refusal on AdvBench jailbreaks (2.69% ASR) and improving out-of-distribution adversarial accuracy by up to 57.14% (AeroEngQA/CPIQA), but still trails a specialized detector on DAN where the baseline was tuned to that distribution.

nodes.title: From Control Boundary to Insurance Claim: Reconstructing AI-Mediated Losses Through the CER Framework
nodes.one_liner: The key insight is that insurability of AI failures hinges on proving the agent’s permitted vs. actual state—not just that “an AI incident happened”—and CER makes the weak link (controls, evidence, or coverage) impossible to ignore.
points: AI-related losses in agentic/RAG systems require reconstructing the full system state (prompts, retrieved context, model/version, memory, tool calls, identities, approvals, downstream actions) rather than only the downstream event, because state changes during reasoning and tool use determine causation and insurability.

CER operationalizes residual-risk transfer as a three-link diagnostic—Control boundary (C), Evidence reconstruction (E), and Insurance response (R)—each scored 0–3, and treats risk as “transfer-aligned” only at C≥2, E≥2, and R≥2, explicitly showing how strong controls without logs (or logs without coverage) still leaves losses retained.

Public agentic deletion incidents (PocketOS, Replit) illustrate that prompt/policy-only constraints can still yield C0 “unbounded” operation when agents can reach broadly scoped credentials or destructive APIs, implying that least-privilege identity scoping, approval gates, and backup isolation are prerequisites for both preventing loss and making claims recoverable.

nodes.title: Insurance of Agentic AI
nodes.one_liner: The most practical path is a coordinated, multi-policy insurance stack with explicit AI aggregates and mixed-cause allocation rules, since agentic AI can generate insured events even when nothing is “hacked.”
points: Agentic AI creates insurable loss events through autonomous external actions (tool invocation and persistent state changes), so underwriting must rate delegated authority and permissions—not just model accuracy or generic “AI use.”

A large share of agentic-AI losses can occur without a traditional cyber-breach predicate (e.g., hallucinations, prompt injection, unsafe delegation, model drift, dependency failures, and cyber-physical harms), making legacy cyber-only triggers insufficient for many high-severity scenarios.

Because agentic-AI risk is non-stationary and highly correlated via shared model/cloud/vendor dependencies, viable pricing needs exposure inventories, scenario libraries, control effectiveness scoring, and explicit accumulation limits supported by quota-share and aggregate reinsurance rather than credibility-based loss triangles alone.

nodes.title: Confused ChatGPT: Cross-App Context Poisoning via First-Party APIs
nodes.one_liner: A single flat, untagged conversation buffer turns “cross-app composition” into a cross-app prompt-injection primitive—made worse by silent, system-level writes via undocumented SDK parameters.
points: ChatGPT Apps expanded 7.3× in five months (122 apps in Dec 2025 to 888 by May 2026), but its core “shared context” design creates a multi-tenant isolation failure where any connected app can influence subsequent LLM-mediated actions taken through other apps.

A first-party API, sendFollowUpMessage, can be amplified by two undocumented parameters (systemPrompt and isVisible:false) to inject silent, system-priority instructions into the shared context, effectively granting third-party apps a stealthy high-privilege write primitive.

Cross-app context poisoning enables a confused-deputy attack in which a malicious app manipulates tool calls issued via benign co-resident apps, and the demonstrated conditional and imperative payloads succeeded across 6/6 tested ChatGPT models.

nodes.title: SlotGCG: Exploiting the Positional Vulnerability in LLMs for Jailbreak Attacks
nodes.one_liner: Jailbreaks get markedly stronger when adversarial tokens are placed in attention-vulnerable interior “slots” rather than appended as a suffix, turning positional search into a cheap, attack-agnostic amplifier.
points: Optimal jailbreak insertion points are rarely the prompt suffix—across 50 AdvBench prompts the lowest-loss “optimal slot” was never the suffix used by standard GCG, implying position is a first-class attack surface rather than a fixed suffix heuristic.

A simple attention-derived Vulnerable Slot Score (VSS) predicts where jailbreak tokens will be most effective, with vulnerable positions remaining stable over optimization (VSS_init vs. VSS_final correlations typically 0.4–0.9), enabling one-pass slot selection instead of exhaustive search.

Selecting high-VSS slots and distributing tokens (SlotGCG) boosts effectiveness and resilience: it adds ~200ms preprocessing, raises average ASR by ~13–14% over GCG-style baselines and by ~42% under defenses, while cutting iterations to success from ~72.6 to ~28.6 on average (e.g., 138→40 steps on Llama-2-7B).

nodes.title: D-Judge: Disrupting Multi-Turn Jailbreaks using Semantics-Preserving Output Rewriting
nodes.one_liner: Instead of trying to detect malicious intent, D-Judge breaks judge-guided jailbreak optimization by feeding the attacker a meaning-equivalent response that causes misleading safety-judge feedback.
points: Semantics-preserving output rewriting at the API boundary (D-Judge) cuts multi-turn jailbreak attack success on HarmBench against GPT-4o from 58.3% (no defense) to 8.6% on average across five attacks, substantially outperforming leading guard/detection/pre/post-processing baselines (best baseline average 40.4%).

D-Judge preserves benign capability while defending: it retains near-baseline instruction-following on IFEval (strict prompt accuracy 0.77 vs 0.77 baseline; loose prompt 0.79 vs 0.80) and incurs a smaller MT-Bench drop than an output-editing baseline (8.34 vs 8.99 baseline, compared to 8.21 for Aligner).
