Memorization depends on fine-tuning capacity: as LoRA rank increases, the canary entropy that maximizes memorization shifts upward (higher-T canaries become most detectable), and under weak DP-SGD settings synthetic canaries can still show leakage when other canary designs fail (e.g., baseline canaries staying below empirical µ≈1.35 while synthetic canaries exceed it).

nodes.title: Runtime Skill Audit: Targeted Runtime Probing for Agent Skill Security
nodes.one_liner: Behavior-grounded auditing flips skill security from “does it look suspicious?” to “what does it actually do when invoked with the right context,” making evasive, trigger-based payloads much easier to catch.
points: Targeted runtime probing in a sandboxed agent environment achieves 90.0% accuracy (88.0% TPR, 8.0% FPR) on a balanced 100-skill benchmark, outperforming the best static baseline by +13 percentage points in accuracy (77.0% → 90.0%).

Under adaptive “self-evolving” attacks, static scanners degrade to 0/20 detections after 1–2 mutation rounds, while runtime trace-based auditing stays robust at 19–20/20 detections across all rounds, indicating behavior-level signals are far harder to evade than artifact-level patterns.

Knowledge-guided risk profiling and context materialization materially improve dynamic auditing, boosting TPR from 82.0% to 88.0% while halving FPR from 16.0% to 8.0%, showing that better probe targeting reduces both missed attacks and false alarms.

nodes.title: Mind your key: An Empirical Study of LLM API Credential Leakage in iOS Apps
nodes.one_liner: LLM API key leakage in iOS apps is not just common—it persists after disclosure because many apps ship unauthenticated or incorrectly-authenticated LLM proxy backends.
points: Dynamic traffic interception on 444 LLM-integrated iOS apps found 64% (282 apps) exposing LLM API credentials in network traffic, with 52% of those (146 apps) offering fully exploitable access via plaintext keys or unauthenticated proxies—enabling direct billable abuse at scale.

Credential exposure most commonly came from JWT/bearer-token leakage (48%), followed by unauthenticated backend proxies (33%) and plaintext provider API keys (19%), showing that “use a backend proxy” often fails in practice when authentication and token validation are missing or misconfigured.

Three months after responsible disclosure, only 28% of vulnerable apps remediated while 72% remained exploitable, driven by hard-to-revoke unauthenticated proxy designs and broken JWT implementations (e.g., missing exp, accepting expired tokens, or extreme lifetimes up to ~100 years).

nodes.title: DIG: Oracle-Guided Directed Input Generation for One-Day Vulnerabilities
nodes.one_liner: Turning security patches into executable oracles makes PoC generation a constraint-solving problem that is both more reproducible and dramatically faster than distance-only directed fuzzing.
points: Across 138 real-world Magma CVEs, the system triggered 80 vulnerabilities (58.0%), beating the strongest baseline by 40% in absolute count (80 vs. 57) and uniquely triggering 9 CVEs that no other evaluated technique could reproduce.

Patch-derived oracle synthesis was correct for 122 of 135 evaluable CVEs (90.37%), indicating that patches can be systematically converted into actionable, necessary preconditions that materially improve downstream exploit/PoC search.

Relative to other tools’ mean time-to-trigger, it was faster on 92.9% of CVEs (78/84), delivered >100× speedups on 48.8% of cases, and reached a maximum observed speedup of 3,664×—showing that explicit constraint guidance can dominate brute-force mutation when triggers are “far” from seeds.

nodes.title: The Emergence of Autonomous Penetration Capabilities in Large Language Model-Powered AI Systems
nodes.one_liner: Even without target hints or web search, high-capability LLM agents can autonomously chain recon → exploit selection → reverse shell on real CVEs at nearly 70% success, and can exploit post-cutoff vulns purely by discovering the right tool module.
points: Across 300 realistic multi-service target servers (30 RCE CVEs) and a black-box agent given only the victim IP, 19 evaluated LLM-powered systems achieved end-to-end reverse-shell compromise rates of 12.0%–69.3% in Tier-1 and 10.7%–68.7% in Tier-2 environments, indicating autonomous penetration is already operationally non-trivial.

Autonomous penetration success strongly tracks general model capability (LiveBench correlation r=0.886 on Tier-1 and r=0.830 on Tier-2), implying that capability scaling directly increases offensive misuse risk unless compensating controls scale faster.

Environmental “noise” from adding three benign services reduced success by only ~7.3% on average, while most failures were due to tool constraints rather than reasoning (46% insufficient tool capability, 42% improper tool usage), suggesting that better toolchains and tighter tool-invocation reliability would disproportionately raise compromise rates.

nodes.title: Hallucination Cascade: Analyzing Error Propagation in Multi-Agent LLM Systems
nodes.one_liner: Multi-agent cascades can reliably damp hallucinations, but the same refinement that reduces unsupported claims also erodes factual preservation—so cascade depth and final-agent choice should be treated as safety-critical parameters.
points: Across 500 multi-agent cascades (1,250 evaluated outputs), deeper 3-agent chains attenuated hallucination from 0.422 to 0.272 (amplification factor 0.644) but also reduced factual accuracy from 0.789 to 0.769, showing hallucination suppression can come with factual-detail loss.

Each agent-to-agent refinement step reduced hallucination by ~0.072 on average while causing small but significant drops in factual accuracy (~0.008) and response quality (~0.017), implying multi-agent “correction” is not lossless and can over-prune or compress valid content.

Model choice and ordering dominate reliability–efficiency trade-offs: LLaMA-3-70B-Instruct had the lowest hallucination (0.272) but highest latency (~15.1s) while GPT-5.3 was fastest (~5.25s) yet most hallucinatory (0.418), and placing the more reliable model last in a heterogeneous chain achieved the best final hallucination (down to 0.263).

nodes.title: MIRAGE: A Polarity-Flipping Encoding Subspace in LLM Agents
nodes.one_liner: Covert exfiltration via “harmless” encodings leaves a strong, transferable internal signature that can be monitored in real time and even flags tool-delegated intent before the tool call happens.
points: A shared ≤5D residual-stream encoding subspace generalizes across nine covert-encoding families and eight open-weight models, with leave-one-encoding-out probe transfer AUC of 0.975–1.000 (and cross-architecture difficulty rankings correlating at Spearman ρ=0.957).

The same subspace flips polarity at the last input (planning) token to predict whether the agent will simulate the encoding inline versus outsource it to a tool call, separating the two strategies before any encoded text exists (controlled-set mean per-layer AUC = 1.000 across all decoder layers on Qwen-7B, with embedding-layer separability eliminated by matched-token controls).

A two-channel internal monitor that reads (1) generation-time subspace activation and (2) planning-time polarity reaches AUC = 0.918 on 126 agentic exfiltration scenarios versus output-only detection near chance (perplexity AUC = 0.518), while benign-encoding false positives vary dramatically by host-model geometry (0% on Qwen-7B, 18.8% on Mistral-7B, 100% on Phi-3.5).

nodes.title: MemVenom: Triggered Poisoning of Multimodal Memories in Web Agents
affiliations: Shenzhen International Graduate School, Tsinghua University
nodes.one_liner: External memory turns web agents into persistently backdoorable systems where a small multimodal memory subgraph plus a visual trigger can repeatedly hijack actions with minimal impact on benign utility.
points: A black-box multimodal memory-poisoning attack can achieve near-complete end-to-end control of web-agent behavior by combining trigger-conditioned recall with post-retrieval action induction, reaching up to 99.15% attack success on GPT-5.4 ReAct-style agents (ASR-ra) and 93.97% on SeeAct with Qwen3-VL-8B.

Trigger-optimized visual retrieval makes malicious memories reliably reappear on demand (often ~95–99% malicious recall, ASR-r) while remaining largely dormant without the trigger, enabling persistent and reusable compromise without modifying model weights or prompts.

Lightweight guardrails are insufficient against memory-level multimodal attacks: hardcoded safety rules only reduce end-to-end success from 93.97% to 77.65% on SeeAct and from 80.15% to 75.97% on ReAct, while LlamaGuard still leaves 75.89% (SeeAct) and 50.71% (ReAct) ASR-ra.

nodes.title: The Containment Gap: How Deployed Agentic AI Frameworks Fail Public-Facing Safety Requirements
nodes.one_liner: Deployed agent frameworks still lack basic containment boundaries, so one poisoned memory write can silently and persistently skew real-world decisions unless lightweight gates are added.
points: An architectural audit of LangChain, AutoGPT, and the OpenAI Agents SDK found 0/3 frameworks provide any containment principle enabled by default, making safety controls opt-in rather than secure-by-default for public deployments.

Memory integrity checks are absent in all three frameworks, and a single memory-poisoning write caused persistent targeted corruption across all tested seeds and multiple model backends, raising wrongful denials for a targeted region to 88.9% (corruption rate 1.000).

Two deterministic framework-layer guards—a memory-write integrity validator and a tool-call policy gate—reduced both memory-poisoning and tool-bypass success from 100% to 0% with sub-millisecond overhead (<0.2 ms per call), showing high-impact risk reduction is feasible without model changes.

nodes.title: EvoBrowseComp: Benchmarking Search Agents on Evolving Knowledge
nodes.one_liner: A continuously refreshable, contamination-resistant benchmark shows that today’s strongest search agents still fail on most multi-source, post-cutoff web questions unless they can retrieve and reason efficiently.
points: An auto-updatable benchmark of 800 complex questions (400 English/400 Chinese) is generated from live-web traversal with a three-agent pipeline that filters credibility and popularity to reduce memorization shortcuts and test-set contamination.

Even a top tool-augmented model (Claude-Opus-4.6) reaches only 44.8% accuracy (English) and 36.8% (Chinese), while removing tools collapses performance to 6.0% and 8.8%, indicating the tasks strongly require fresh retrieval rather than parametric recall.

Questions demand broad cross-site evidence aggregation (average 4.2 distinct root domains; >90% require ≥3 domains) and exhibit high structural complexity (≈8 nodes in reasoning graphs), making single-source or shallow-search strategies systematically insufficient.

nodes.title: Personalization Meets Safety:Mechanisms,Risks,and Mitigations in Personalized LLMs
affiliations: China Mobile Jiutian Artificial Intelligence Technology (Beijing) Co., Ltd.
Xinjiang Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry
nodes.one_liner: Personalization isn’t just a UX feature—it changes what “safe” means per user and creates long-horizon, memory-driven failure modes that current benchmarks largely miss.
points: Personalized user context can materially improve safety outcomes, with a 43.2% safety-score gain reported across seven sensitive domains—implying that “one-size-fits-all” safety evaluation systematically underestimates both risks and mitigation potential in deployed assistants.

Personalization expands the attack surface by turning prompts, retrieval corpora, and long-term memory into persistent leakage and manipulation channels (e.g., retrieval-to-prompt PII exposure, poisoning, and indirect prompt injection) that can steer behavior without changing model weights.

Parametric personalization (fine-tuning/PEFT/RL-based) increases irreversibility and fragility by entangling user data with weights, enabling membership inference and adapter backdoors while risking alignment drift even from benign user-data adaptation.

nodes.title: AI Scientists Are Only as Good as Their Evidence: A Stratified Ablation of Proprietary Data and Reasoning Skills in Drug-Asset Valuation
nodes.one_liner: Reasoning scaffolds mostly fix analyst calibration, while proprietary evidence determines the upper bound of what an AI Scientist can know and thus decide in long-tail, high-stakes domains.
points: Adding structured reasoning and public APIs improved recommendation calibration (tier-in-range 0.80→0.89) and objectivity (3.16→3.30/4) but did not materially increase access to the competitive landscape (curated-gold recall only 0.25→0.38).

Proprietary commercial-intelligence access nearly closed the factual gap, raising curated competitive-record recall to 0.96 (vs 0.25/0.38) and long-tail program recall to 0.93 (vs 0.26/0.30), implying most diligence-critical evidence is effectively unreachable from web+public sources alone.

Raw report-level verdict quality was similar without proprietary data (DQ 7.01 vs 6.96/10), but when penalized for missing evidence (DQ×coverage) the informed decision quality jumped to 7.43 with proprietary data versus 1.76/2.57, and even a hypothetically perfect non-proprietary report would be capped at 3.83 by coverage limits.

nodes.title: Improving Adversarial Transferability on Vision-Language Pre-training Models via Surrogate-Specific Bias Correction
nodes.one_liner: Using a weak-semantic reference image to estimate and subtract surrogate-specific gradient components materially improves black-box transfer attacks on VLP models and even carries over to closed-source MLLMs.
points: DeBias-Attack’s bias-guided gradient correction raises heterogeneous black-box transfer ASR on Flickr30K from ALBEF→CLIP ViT to 70.06%/73.26% (TR/IR) and ALBEF→CLIP CNN to 70.88%/74.96%, outperforming the prior best (GLEAM) by up to 7.07 points in TR and 8.77 points in IR.

Transfer gains generalize across datasets and architectures, with MSCOCO showing ALBEF→CLIP ViT ASR improving to 83.90%/86.33% and CLIP CNN→CLIP ViT improving to 85.23%/87.75%, indicating the method reduces surrogate overfitting especially when crossing model families.

Adversarial pairs generated for retrieval transfer to other tasks and MLLMs: on ITR→IC, CIDEr drops from 133.3 to 56.3 and SPICE from 23.8 to 11.9, while on MLLM captioning transfer DeBias-Attack increases ASR from 52.71% to 67.71% on Claude-3.5 and from 58.70% to 67.46% on GPT-4o.

nodes.title: Context-Based Adversarial Attacks on AI Code Generators: Vulnerability Analysis and Implications
affiliations: The Beacom College of Computer & Cyber Sciences
nodes.one_liner: A small amount of adversarial “helpful” context (docs/comments/examples) can reliably steer code LLMs into emitting exploitable vulnerabilities, and the same attack prompts tend to work across models—making upstream context hygiene and real-time screening a priority.
points: Contextual prompt manipulation at inference time raises vulnerable-code generation from 3.5% to 37.4% (a 10.7× increase; χ²=847.3, p<0.001), showing that “benign” comments/docs/identifiers can function as a high-leverage supply-chain attack surface for AI-assisted development.

Direct-instruction context is the most dangerous trigger (mean ASR 55%), reaching 100% success on GPT-3.5-Turbo and 80% on GPT-4, implying that instruction-following behavior can be inverted into reliable vulnerability induction even when baseline vulnerable output is 0%.

Attacks generalize across architectures with 60–100% transferability (83% of model pairs have TR>0.60), while a dual-layer prompt+code defense detects 89.1% of attacks at 0.3% false positives with ~520 ms latency, indicating practical real-time IDE gating is feasible but must be broadly deployed to avoid cross-tool spillover.

nodes.title: Model Multiplicity for Adversarial Detection in Small Language Model Training on Edge Devices
nodes.one_liner: Using disagreement between concurrently trained small-model replicas turns slow, coordinated poisoning into a detectable system inconsistency even when clients participate intermittently.
points: Training multiple independently-sampled SLM replicas (rather than one global model) yields stable poisoning signals, with reported round-wise detection AUC typically ~0.75–0.90 versus ~0.55 for Flanders and ~0.65–0.70 for robust aggregation in backdoor settings.

Under intermittent participation (e.g., client availability q=0.2), model-multiplicity achieves near-immediate time-to-detection (≈0–1 rounds) while temporal per-client detectors lag by ~1–3+ rounds, reflecting that sparse per-client histories fundamentally limit statistical power when clients rarely reappear.

Despite maintaining multiple replicas, the multi-model approach is reported to have lower runtime and memory overhead than Flanders in the presented edge-scale simulations, implying ensemble-level consistency checks can be a cost-efficient defense for resource-constrained federated SLM training.

nodes.title: Influence Factors on RAG Poisoning
nodes.one_liner: Poisoning risk in RAG is dominated by retriever+top‑k+dataloader interactions, and “more sources” helps only when they are not all compromised.
points: Across 432 full-factorial RAG configurations, retriever architecture, dataset choice, and retrieval depth (top‑k) were the strongest drivers of poisoned-passage exposure at retrieval time, with higher k consistently increasing Poison@k.

Dense (BGE) and graph-based retrievers reduced poisoning exposure and downstream attack success relative to BM25 (e.g., Poison@k main effect: Graph −0.170, dense −0.040; ASR main effect: Graph −0.143, dense −0.073), indicating that semantic/structure-aware retrieval can materially improve robustness under the tested attack.

Poisoning impact amplified when adversarial content was replicated across knowledge bases (num poisoned databases=2 increased ASR by +0.062 and reduced correct-answer rate by −0.066), while adding an additional clean database improved correctness (+0.060) and reduced ASR (−0.044), implying redundancy helps only if at least one source remains clean.

nodes.title: Collective Hallucination in Multi-Agent LLMs:Modeling and Defense
nodes.one_liner: Multi-agent hallucinations behave like a contagion where graph structure and trust signals can push the system across an R0>1 tipping point into cascades.
points: Propagation-aware control in multi-agent LLMs cut hallucination from 0.118 to 0.072 (39% reduction) while improving accuracy from 0.79 to 0.87 and semantic consistency from 0.75 to 0.84, indicating that interaction regulation can outperform output-only mitigation in recursive settings.

Communication topology strongly determines cascade risk: under adversarial conditions, scale-free graphs produced the highest spread (AF 1.45, R0 1.21, cascade size 5.6) while ring graphs constrained diffusion (AF 1.18, R0 0.86, cascade size 2.1), implying hub-centric designs are inherently more vulnerable to collective failure.

Verifier corruption was the most destabilizing attack, pushing hallucination amplification to 1.45 versus 1.12 for coordinated attacks and 1.28 for seeded attacks, but adaptive control limited amplification to ~1.08–1.12 and drove R0 below 1 (e.g., 1.21→0.91), showing that the verification layer is a critical single point of systemic trust.

nodes.title: Clinically Grounded Privacy Evaluation of Medical LMs
nodes.one_liner: Billing-style appointment metadata can act like an extraction key that unlocks both verbatim note fragments and high-stakes diagnoses, while naive overlap scores exaggerate harm by counting templated boilerplate as “leakage.”
points: Routine encounter metadata (name, DOB, visit date, provider, location) is enough to trigger longitudinal PHI leakage, yielding verbatim content from a mean of 2.81 distinct notes per patient and sensitive-diagnosis recovery as high as AUROC 0.91 (abortion) and 0.81 (HIV) with PPV up to 0.98–1.00.

Leakage scales sharply with adversarial access: average verbatim overlap rises from 4.8% under public demographics to 85.6% when given encounter info plus chief complaint and HPI, showing that seemingly small increases in prior knowledge can unlock near-complete note reconstruction.

Exact-match memorization metrics materially overestimate patient-specific harm because 36% of overlapping tokens are templated boilerplate and 45% of memorized regions recur across multiple patients (including 4.9% appearing in >8,000 patients), so audits must separate clinically revealing, patient-unique spans from shared documentation artifacts.

nodes.title: SGTO-MAS: Secure Gorilla Troops Optimization for Multi-Agent LLM Systems
nodes.one_liner: A risk-sensitive swarm optimizer can match full 20-agent activation performance while using ~4 agents and materially lowering adversarial exposure through trust-weighted consensus.
points: Across 500 runs, the optimizer selected compact teams (mean 4.04 of 20 agents; range 4–6) while maintaining stable decision performance (score 0.5281±0.0173) and high agreement (trust-weighted consensus 0.8764±0.0312), implying most multi-agent gains come from structured selection rather than scaling agent count.

Security-aware optimization reduced risk substantially versus common multi-agent baselines, achieving 0.301 risk at 24.09s average runtime compared to AutoGen-style (0.412 risk, 27.54s), CAMEL-style (0.385, 28.12s), and debate MAS (0.372, 29.07s), indicating adaptive selection can cut exposure without sacrificing accuracy (0.842±0.014).

Ablations show robustness hinges on aggregation and trust: removing consensus weighting caused the largest fitness drop (−10.4%) with higher risk (+20%), while removing the trust model cut score by −5.9% and raised risk by +13.3%, suggesting defense-in-depth should prioritize trust calibration plus robust, attack-aware consensus.

nodes.title: Now You (Still) See Me: Detecting Evasive Steganographic Payloads in LLMs
nodes.one_liner: Adaptive fine-tuning can hide prompt-secret steganography from standard activation probes, but tightening the evaluation distribution’s linguistic slack reliably makes the hidden payload visible again.
points: Adversarially fine-tuned steganographic trojans maintained 58–79% exact-match recovery of 32-bit prompt-borne secrets while evading both linear ridge probes and held-out 2-layer MLP activation probes across five open-weight LLMs, indicating activation-based extractability monitoring is not robust to adaptive attackers.

Evasion incurred only 1–8% average capability degradation across six standard benchmarks while bit accuracy remained ~98.5%, showing covert exfiltration channels can persist with minimal user-visible performance loss in realistic deployment settings.

Constraining paraphrastic “slack” via a theory-guided recontextualization evaluation distribution restored detectability for both ridge and MLP probes on all evasive trojans without breaking the covert channel, implying defenders can regain monitoring power by auditing on controlled prompt distributions that reduce residual degrees of freedom.

nodes.title: Comparative Analysis of Inference-Time Defense Methods for Multimodal Large Language Models
nodes.one_liner: Inference-time defenses for multimodal models are highly non-monotonic—combining “more defense” can make systems unusable via near-total benign refusal, so deployments need adaptive, model- and attack-aware defense selection rather than fixed pipelines.
points: Across 8 InternVL/Qwen-VL models and 7 benchmarks (9,000 samples), no inference-time defense consistently dominated—effectiveness depended strongly on the model’s baseline safety and the attack class, with some defenses even reducing harmless rate below the undefended baseline (e.g., AdaShield on InternVL2.5-4B: HR 0.802→0.721).

Stacking defenses was operationally catastrophic for utility: the full SmoothVLM+AdaShield+RapGuard pipeline caused 97–100% over-refusal on 500 benign MMBench queries for every model, and SmoothVLM alone produced 99.2–100% over-refusal, implying that “max safety via refusal” is a practical failure mode of naive defense composition.

A lightweight safety prompt preserved usability (0.0–18.2% over-refusal; 5/8 models <7%) while still improving safety on typographic attacks (e.g., InternVL2.5-4B HR 0.802→0.923), and preliminary whitebox PGD on one informative model showed text-level defenses can fully suppress a gradient-based visual attack (ASR 25%→0% on Qwen2.5-VL-7B, n=20).

nodes.title: Distilling Safe LLM Systems via Soft Prompts for On Device Settings
nodes.one_liner: A practical path to on-device LLM safety: compress guard-rail behavior into tiny soft prompts that keep most of the safety benefit while avoiding the dual-model 2× inference cost.
points: Distilling guard-model safety into ~100 learned soft prompt vectors preserves single-pass inference while delivering large safety gains (e.g., Llama3-1B SGS on HarmBench rises from 71% to 99% with a guard model, and soft-prompt distillation recovers much of that improvement without deploying the guard at runtime).

Soft prompts trained via TV/KL distillation outperform equally parameter-budgeted LoRA adapters and steering vectors, with reported safety gaps exceeding 20 percentage points in SGS in some settings, indicating input-conditioning is a more reliable safety control than weight- or activation-level edits under tight edge constraints.

On-device overhead is minimal relative to dual-model systems: soft-prompt distillation adds <1% memory and typically <10–15% FLOPs/token (e.g., Llama3-1B 2.15→2.35 GFLOPs/token) versus ~2× compute and doubled memory when running an LLM plus a 1B guard model in-the-loop (2.15→4.60 GFLOPs/token).

nodes.title: ALIGNBEAM : Inference-Time Alignment Transfer via Cross-Vocabulary Logit Mixing
nodes.one_liner: A training-free logit-translation bridge lets a small aligned anchor steer a different-model-family specialist toward refusal, transferring safety at deployment time with most of the gain coming from just a few initial tokens.
points: Cross-vocabulary inference-time alignment transfer can recover most refusal behavior lost to domain fine-tuning, raising macro-average string-match refusal from 38.1%→91.5% on AdvBench (+53.4 pp) and 25.9%→76.4% on HarmBench-Std (+50.5 pp) across five model pairs without changing either model’s weights.

Safety steering is highly front-loaded: mixing only the first three decoding steps captures +61.9 pp (>92%) of the combined HarmBench+AdvBench safety gain, implying that shallow prefix interventions can deliver most of the benefit with bounded latency.

Compared with moderation filters, the method maintains a better benign-safety balance (e.g., on Llama-3.1-8B: OR-Bench-Hard over-refusal 22.3% vs. LlamaGuard ~34.5%, and JBB-Benign 26.0% vs. 38–39%) while keeping task accuracy essentially unchanged (−0.4 pp on both GSM8K and MedQA).

nodes.title: Defending Against Malicious Finetuning by Scaling Train-time Adversarial Attacks
affiliations: Institute for Interdisciplinary Information Sciences, Tsinghua University
nodes.one_liner: Scaling the attacker in the training loop (more inner steps, not bigger step size) produces materially stronger tamper-resistance to full-parameter finetuning without sacrificing downstream utility.
points: One-step inner-loop adversarial alignment (as used in prior meta-learning/SAM-style defenses) fails under full-parameter malicious finetuning, while explicitly simulating multi-step train-time attacks (e.g., k1≈300) sharply improves post-attack robustness.

Patcher cuts jailbreak Attack Success Rate by 67.5%/53.4%/54.8% versus the next-best baseline when attacked with Beavertails-unsafe across AdvBench/Beavertails/HEx-PHI, and still reduces ASR by 35.8% (PKU-SafeRLHF-unsafe) and 19.8% (Toxic-DPO) on average in out-of-distribution attacks.

The parallelized variant preserves most of the safety gains while reducing wall-clock time by 31.1% versus non-parallel Patcher (1.55h vs 2.25h), trading off higher total multi-GPU memory footprint (≈90.97GB) rather than per-GPU pressure.

nodes.title: Scenario-based Probing and Steering Cultural Values in Large Language Models--Extended Version
nodes.one_liner: Token-level forced-choice dilemmas plus activation steering can move an LLM around the World Values Survey cultural map, but the two axes stay tightly coupled—so “dialing culture” is feasible yet not cleanly separable.
points: Scenario-based forced-choice probing achieved high item validity (296/300 scenarios passed alignment/neutrality/realism checks, 98.7%) and showed human construct grounding (6/10 value constructs significantly linked to Vietnamese respondents’ matched scenario choices), indicating token-probability probes can reveal latent value signals that survey-style prompting often masks.

Direct survey prompting produced strong evaluation artifacts—e.g., Llama refusal rates rose from 10% (basic persona) to 30% (advanced persona) and 40% of Qwen answers collapsed to a mid-scale band (5–6/10)—causing models to cluster toward a Western-centric region and making scenario-based logit probing a more discriminative measurement for cultural alignment.

Activation steering and hybrid prompt+activation steering shifted models toward target country coordinates without retraining but exposed a structural control limit: cross-axis entanglement remained high (entanglement ratio E≈0.72–0.81 with vector-only steering, improving to ≈0.53–0.74 with hybrid), mirroring a country-level WVS axis correlation (r=0.474) and implying that independently steering cultural dimensions is intrinsically constrained while task accuracy drops were moderate (e.g., Global-MMLU Qwen 0.734→0.704; Gemma 0.608→0.530).

nodes.title: From Holistic Evaluation to Structured Criteria: Rubrics Across the Evolving LLM Landscape
nodes.one_liner: Rubrics are emerging as the common interface that turns fuzzy quality judgments into auditable, trainable signals—but their generation, execution biases, and manipulability now look like core alignment risks.
points: Treating rubrics as explicit, decomposable, verifiable criteria (rather than holistic scalar scores) enables dimension-level diagnosis and denser supervision signals that better handle open-ended, non-verifiable tasks and long-horizon agent behavior.

Rubric quality is a first-order safety/reliability dependency: swapping model-generated rubrics for human-written ones yields about a 27% average accuracy lift for judge decisions, while naive rubrics can drop judge accuracy from 55.6% to 42.9% (13 points below a no-rubric baseline), meaning low-quality rubrics can be actively harmful.

Rubrics introduce a new attack surface where subtle criterion edits can stealthily induce preference drift with up to a 27.9% target-domain accuracy loss while preserving aggregate benchmark performance, and once such rubrics are used for post-training the resulting behavioral shift can become effectively irreversible.

nodes.title: The Cold-Start Safety Gap in LLM Agents
nodes.one_liner: Agent safety is not stationary—benign tool-use history “warms up” latent safety behavior, making cold-start turns the most attackable phase and suggesting deployment should treat first-turn sessions as a distinct threat surface.
points: Tool-calling LLM agents show a pronounced “cold-start safety gap,” with safety improving monotonically by +9 to +52 percentage points from depth D=0 to D=20 across 7 models (e.g., Llama-3.1-8B: 5.7%→57.8%; Gemma4-26B: 82.9%→91.8%), implying first-turn interactions are a high-risk window for harmful tool use.

Internal-state probes separate safe vs unsafe outcomes with >0.9 accuracy and show hidden states migrating across a linear safety boundary as benign agentic context accumulates, indicating depth-driven safety gains reflect a real shift in latent “safety mode,” not just superficial refusals.

A practical mitigation is session warm-up: completing ~5–10 benign tool-use tasks before exposure meaningfully boosts safety and generalizes to AgentHarm (+23% on average) and Agent Safety Bench (+8%), while preserving utility only when the warm-up uses full real interaction (whereas safety fine-tuning can crater tool utility, e.g., BFCL 64%→17% and API-Bank 85.6%→64.8%).

nodes.title: PolicyGuard: Towards Test-time and Step-level Adversary Defense for Reinforcement Learning Agent
nodes.one_liner: A black-box RL backdoor defense that flags compromised actions in real time by treating triggered behavior as epistemic-uncertainty spikes rather than trying to recover the trigger.
points: PolicyGuard enables online, step-level backdoor detection for black-box RL policies by using GP posterior variance over pseudo-trajectories, achieving average AUROC 0.856 on perturbation triggers and 0.859 on adversary-agent triggers across seven games.

Under practical “hard-coded” backdoors that bypass learned parameters, optimization/probability-based baselines collapse to near-random detection (AUROC ≈0.47–0.50), while PolicyGuard remains robust (AUROC 0.868 for perturbation-based and 0.878 for adversary-agent attacks), indicating behavior-manifold deviation is a stronger signal than trigger reverse-engineering.

Detection generalizes and is relatively stable to trigger variations (e.g., pixel patches 3×3 to 6×6 and adversary trigger lengths 5–20 steps keep AUROC typically ≥0.85 in key tasks), with a measurable robustness boundary where random input noise above ~0.2 magnitude causes notable degradation, especially for MLP-encoded state pipelines.

nodes.title: The Internet of Agentic AI: Communication, Coordination, and Collective Intelligence at Scale
nodes.one_liner: The most interesting takeaway is the attempt to treat agent networks like an Internet-layer problem—standardized discovery, identity, protocol negotiation, and governance—so collective intelligence can emerge without surrendering control or security.
points: Internet-scale agent ecosystems require a layered interoperability stack—connectivity, identity/trust, capability discovery, task semantics, governance, and incentives—because transport alone cannot prevent impersonation, unauthorized delegation, or unverifiable task completion.

Decentralized and hybrid coordination patterns (e.g., coalition formation with local “exit-and-join” decisions plus admission constraints) are positioned as the main mechanism for “controlled emergence,” aiming to capture self-organization benefits while bounding unsafe or infeasible reallocations in safety-critical workflows.

Scalability hinges on reducing coordination overhead via parallelism and heterogeneous compute mapping, with a cited DGX Spark evaluation showing 4 concurrent agents taking ~2.6× (not 4×) the time of one agent, implying that high-performance interconnects and orchestration can materially mitigate multi-agent latency growth.

nodes.title: AgentCanary: A Security Evaluation Framework for Autonomous AI Agents in Real Executable Environments
nodes.one_liner: AgentCanary demonstrates that agent security failures are primarily system-compromise problems driven by persistent state and tool supply-chain attacks, not just prompt-level refusal.
points: Across 12 frontier agent backbones, unsafe outcomes vary dramatically by attack surface—mean unsafe-outcome rate is 9% for indirect prompt injection but jumps to 42% for memory contamination, 51% for long-horizon progressive attacks, and 62% for skill poisoning—showing that persistent state and compromised tools dominate real agent risk.

Script-camouflaged poisoned skills (malicious logic moved into executable scripts) cause a systemic collapse in defenses, e.g., Qwen3.5-397B’s unsafe-outcome rate rises from 13.7% to 76.5% and Kimi-K2.5’s from 29.4% to 82.4%, implying that manifest-level vetting is insufficient against supply-chain style agent attacks.

Security often looks good while being brittle: in contextual indirect prompt injection, multiple models achieve ~95–100 outcome safety but very low security awareness (e.g., Claude Sonnet 4.5 OSS 95.6–97.0 vs SAS 13.2–22.3), meaning agents may avoid harm incidentally without recognizing the attack mechanism and thus can fail under adaptive variants.

nodes.title: Can Open-Source LLM Agents Replace Static Application Security Testing Tools? An Empirical Assessment
nodes.one_liner: General-purpose open-source LLM agents are currently neither accurate nor fast enough to replace static analyzers for SAST, and their high-confidence hallucinations create a net-negative workflow.
points: Across three open-source Ollama-hosted models (gemma3, llama3.1, qwen2.5), an LLM-agent SAST pipeline never exceeded 0.25 recall on medium/high findings and often fell near zero, indicating it misses most issues that a conventional tool flags.

False-positive behavior dominated the agent outputs, with reported false-positive rates ranging roughly 40%–89% across repositories/models (composite scores all negative), making findings operationally noisy and prone to analyst time-waste or misguided “fixes.”

Runtime overhead was prohibitive: while Bandit completed scans in under a minute per repo, the agent took ~1–4 hours per repository (≈30–90 seconds per file), making it impractical for CI/CD gating or rapid developer feedback loops.

nodes.title: An AI Security Agent for University ACMIS: Multi-Vector Threat Detection and Automated Response
nodes.one_liner: Interesting because it treats a university ACMIS as a multi-surface system (auth, authorization, finance, behavior, health) and couples high-F1 detection with sub-second automated response plus abuse-aware password recovery.
points: A multi-layer agent combining LSTM session modeling, sliding-window velocity thresholds, and a user–device–IP graph model reached a 0.91 macro-average F1 on nine ACMIS threat categories versus 0.49 for a rule-based IDS baseline, indicating behavior- and context-driven detection is essential for university systems where many attacks lack fixed signatures.

Time-to-mitigate was operationally viable, with critical-tier automated actions (e.g., lockdown/freeze/quarantine) completing under 300 ms at the 95th percentile, supporting preemptive containment before fast-moving account-takeover and fraud workflows can complete.

A password-recovery NLP chatbot achieved 96.4% identity-verification accuracy and detected mass-reset abuse at 94.1% with a 3.8% false-positive rate on high-volume legitimate periods, showing that account recovery can be both user-friendly and instrumented as an active security control.

nodes.title: MAStrike: Shapley-Guided Collusive Red-Teaming on Multi-Agent Systems
nodes.one_liner: Agent-level Shapley attribution can be repurposed from interpretability into an attack planner that reliably finds small, high-synergy colluding coalitions which defeat distributed safety checks in hierarchical LLM agent systems.
points: Shapley-guided coalition selection plus closed-loop coordinated injections achieves high multi-agent Attack Success Rates with only k=2 compromised agents, reaching 61.8% on Claude Opus 4.7 and 55.6% on GPT-5.5 while heuristic and reflection baselines are near-zero in most settings.

Multi-agent capability and safety are brittle across enterprise workflows: benign task success varies widely by domain (average BSR 72.3% Gemini 3.1 Pro vs 69.6% Claude Opus 4.7 vs 64.8% GPT-5.5) and collapses in some CRM workflows (e.g., 0–17.5% for GPT-5.5 in several categories), implying that coordination-heavy settings both reduce utility and expand exploitability.

Vulnerability is sparse and interaction-driven: Shapley attributions show most agents have near-zero marginal impact while a small task-dependent subset dominates, and pairwise interaction indices can be strongly positive or negative so picking individually “important” agents can still yield low ASR without synergy-aware coalition optimization.

nodes.title: Observability for Delegated Execution in Agentic AI Systems
nodes.one_liner: Delegated authority is a different graph than execution causality, and binding delegation context at tool-invocation time is what turns agent forensics from guesswork into a well-posed query problem.
points: Delegation-scoped execution is formally non-identifiable from standard audit logs and distributed traces—two incompatible delegation assignments can produce identical event streams and causal graphs—so any post-hoc reconstruction based on trace/session IDs or time windows is inherently heuristic under overlap, retries, and multi-agent fanout.

In large synthetic stress tests (10,000 users; 100,000 agents; 0.2–2.0M delegations/day), correlation baselines exhibited extreme ambiguity (median up to 10,856 consistent groupings and up to 107,718 at p95) and low tail recall (p05 as low as 0.03), while explicit delegation binding achieved unambiguous reconstruction (Amb=1) and perfect recall (Rec=1) for covered events.

Authority and causality diverge heavily in realistic agent workflows (a single delegation spanned 35–55 causal components at median, p95 up to 64), implying that treating a trace/span as the unit of accountability systematically breaks investigation, whereas a delegation-aware CIM plus gateway reduced analyst query construction from 6–14 correlation/normalization steps to 1–3 direct predicates with modest overhead (+247 bytes/event and 0.29 ms p95 gateway latency).

nodes.title: Closing the Sim-to-Real Gap: An Evaluation Framework for Autonomous Cyber Defense Configuration of Commercial EDR
nodes.one_liner: Realistic closed-loop evaluation of autonomous defense agents must treat commercial EDRs as evolving, partially unobservable autonomous co-agents rather than passive policy engines.
points: Across 20 commercial-tool pentests (2,388 attacker events and ~207k Defender XDR alerts), observed prevention was dominated by vendor-autonomous Automated Investigation & Response (AIR), with operator-deployed ASR rules contributing only 0.7%–1.8% of blocks overall while AIR accounted for 28.6%–100%, implying benchmark scores can mostly reflect the EDR’s black-box autonomy rather than the defense agent’s tuning decisions.

Telemetry artifacts materially distort per-policy evaluation: the LSASS ASR rule fired 55 times yet aligned to only 15% of 370 credential-dump actions and was mostly triggered by benign processes (69% svchost.exe), while ASR timestamps showed 8–24 minute median delays and no blocks within ±60s, indicating SOC-optimized logging/throttling can masquerade as poor control efficacy (or hide real efficacy) unless attribution is process- and PID-aware.

Attacker-side ground truth revealed measurement incompleteness in the defender’s API surfaces: 15 attacker-attributed 'EDR intervention' failures occurred (0.6% of actions) and 13% of these had no corresponding named-block telemetry in Defender’s standard event streams, demonstrating that relying on a single vendor telemetry table can undercount true blocks and bias comparative evaluations.

nodes.title: Beyond Runtime Enforcement: Shield Synthesis as Defensibility Analysis for Adversarial Networks
affiliations: King Fahd University of Petroleum and Minerals
nodes.one_liner: Treating shield synthesis as an offline architectural diagnostic (verdict + attractor geometry + operational fingerprint) exposes security-critical design differences that runtime-style “safe policy” framing can completely miss.
points: A dual-specification safety-game construction yields a provable defensibility verdict (initial state in the winning region) by seeding defender-unsafe states as the attractor base while filtering attacker moves by an attacker-constraint automaton, avoiding the trivial collapse that would occur if attacker-violations were treated as unsafe.

In the reference network product game (150,000 states), only 23,730 states (15.82%) are in the defender’s winning region while the attractor converges in just 3 non-trivial iterations with 88.4% of absorbed (non-trivial) states in shell-1, indicating a “cliff-like” safety margin where most losing states are one optimal attacker move from violation.

Formal defensibility and operational effectiveness decouple sharply: two topology tweaks with nearly unchanged winning-region sizes (~15.5–15.9% vs 15.8% baseline) produce radically different post-convergence outcomes (DDR 22.7% fully-connected vs 80.7% with a single VPN-bypass edge removed), showing that existence of a safe strategy does not predict how well defense performs under adaptive play.

nodes.title: MPC-Patch-Bench: Security-Aware LLM Code Patch for Multi-Party Computation
nodes.one_liner: Security-aware verification for MPC flips the usual code-repair story: a sizable fraction of “passing” LLM patches are still cryptographically unsafe or numerically unfaithful.
points: A new repository-level benchmark for MPC code repair yields 205 executable, security-verified tasks distilled from 7,305 PRs across five MPC frameworks, demonstrating that standard SWE-bench-style filtering would otherwise retain only 42 tasks (~5× fewer).

Top models achieve only 22.9% functional fix rate (47/205), and security-aware verification drops the best verified-safe rate to 17.1% (35/205), showing that plain unit tests materially overestimate reliability in privacy-preserving codebases.

Across evaluated models, 11%–40% of functionally passing patches are rejected for MPC-specific security or numerical-fidelity issues (e.g., unsafe reveals, illegal public/private casts, precision drift), enough to reorder the model leaderboard under safety-aware scoring.

nodes.title: Semantic Quorum Assurance: Collective Certification for Non-Deterministic AI Infrastructure
nodes.one_liner: Turns non-deterministic LLM ops into a cryptographically enforced, diversity-aware “semantic admission control” layer that dramatically lowers unsafe changes with only seconds of overhead—until common-mode failures dominate.
points: A diverse, risk-adaptive validator quorum with a sovereign execution gate cut unsafe infrastructure-mutation approvals from 18.5% (single-agent validation) to 0.3% on held-out safe/unsafe trials (excluding ambiguous cases), implying multi-agent semantic admission can reduce catastrophic agent actions by ~98% relative to self-validation.

Median end-to-end validation latency stayed within 1.45–4.12 seconds (low→high risk buckets) while scaling quorum sizes from 2 to 7 validators, showing control-plane semantic gating can be practical for slow-path mutations without human-review delays.

Safety depends strongly on independence assumptions: in a correlated-failure stress test, unsafe approval rose from 0.1% at ρ=0 to 8.4% at ρ=1.0, indicating that model-family/archetype diversity and correlation-aware quorum selection are critical and must be continuously recalibrated as models and prompts drift.

nodes.title: Understanding and mitigating the risks of OpenClaw for non-technical users: A practical guide with Skill
affiliations: Guangzhou Institute of Science and Technology
Science and Education Evaluation Lab, Guangzhou Institute of Science and Technology
nodes.one_liner: The key insight is that the biggest safety win for non-technical users comes from operational guardrails (least privilege, plugin vetting, sandboxing, confirmations, credential hygiene, and spend caps) rather than model-only fixes.
points: OpenClaw’s risk profile is amplified by its high-privilege OS/tool access, with real incidents including CVE-2026-25253 enabling one-click remote code execution via a crafted link that leaks a control token and can lead to credential theft and disk encryption.

Ecosystem supply-chain defenses are brittle: in a study of 67,453 ClawHub skills, over 80% of flagged skills were detected by only one of three scanners and just 0.69% were flagged by all three, implying single-scanner vetting is structurally insufficient for skill marketplaces.

Agent-centric attacks can outperform classic prompt injection defenses, with persistence-style trojan backdoors reported to succeed 95.5% of the time even against models resilient to single-turn injections, and token-drain attacks shown to drive up to 9× higher inference spend via cascading retries.

nodes.title: A Five-Plane Reference Architecture for Runtime Governance of Production AI Agents
nodes.one_liner: The interesting move is treating agent security as runtime governance of delegated action—plan-aware, stateful, capability-attenuated, and evidence-first—rather than trying to “fix the model.”
points: A five-plane runtime governance design centralizes a single intent-and-authority adjudication (“reasoning plane”) and projects enforceable directives to network, identity, endpoint, and data planes, replacing federated per-plane authorizations that miss plan- and session-level risk.

Stop-anywhere mediation exposes seven interception points across an agent loop and expands outcomes beyond allow/deny into six interruption primitives (pause, escalate, narrow, modify, defer, rollback), enabling policy to block, rewrite, attenuate, or compensate actions before and after commits.

Composite principals with mandatory capability attenuation bound delegated authority to the intersection of capabilities along a signed delegation chain (with per-capability TTL), providing structural protection against delegation abuse and confused-deputy-style authority escalation while producing tamper-evident, reconstructible evidence records.

nodes.title: Towards Responsibly Non-Compliant Machines
nodes.one_liner: The interesting move is treating refusal as a governance interface—justifications plus override-and-liability workflows—rather than a blanket safety/alignment behavior.
points: Responsible machine non-compliance is framed as requiring (i) an explicit justification for refusal, (ii) a defined pathway for human override when appropriate, and (iii) tracking of security risk and liability transfer when humans force compliance.

Non-compliance cases are organized into feasibility limits (not overrideable), safety conflicts split across user/environment/machine harm with different override rules, normative conflicts (unethical/antisocial/illegal) that can be overridden only with informed acceptance of liability, and efficiency/task-priority refusals that are overrideable mainly in emergencies.

Three engineering strategies are proposed—rule-listed “deliberate” refusal sets, thresholded “predictable” check pipelines (feasible→safe→normative→efficient), and “learnt” refusal policies that infer when to trigger deeper refusal reasoning—highlighting the operational tradeoff between constant scrutiny of commands and timely autonomy.

nodes.title: Byzantine Cheap Talk: Adversarial Resilience and Topology Effects in LLM Coordination Games
affiliations: Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence (MBZUAI)
nodes.one_liner: Seemingly benign design choices—letting agents know the network topology or trusting cheap-talk—can deterministically destroy LLM coordination and create stable, exploitable behavioral types across model families.
points: In a 4-player Stag Hunt with cheap-talk, introducing a single deterministic Byzantine agent (signals “stag” then plays Hare) drove group cooperation to 0% (from 77% baseline), while non-Byzantine agents still played Stag 50%±4% of the time—showing fast betrayal detection but persistent collective failure under unanimity payoffs.

Restricting communication visibility collapses coordination only when agents are told about the topology (explicit ring: 5%±5% group cooperation; explicit star: 0%±0%), whereas applying the same restrictions without disclosure preserves near-perfect cooperation (silent ring: 100%±0%; silent star: 80%±13%), implying meta-reasoning about hidden players—not information loss—triggers breakdowns.

Across six model families and multiple cohorts/group sizes, agents reliably fell into Defection-Prone vs Cooperation-Persistent archetypes after betrayal, producing large exploitation gaps (hard Byzantine: DP earns 2.42/round vs CP 0.45/round, a 5.4× difference) and indicating an “alignment tax” where more cooperative policies become systematically disadvantageous under adversarial peers.

nodes.title: Sovereign Assurance Boundary: Certificate-Bound Admission for Agentic Infrastructure
nodes.one_liner: The core idea is to replace ambient agent permissions with short-lived, revocable certificates that cryptographically bind “why this is allowed” (evidence + policy epoch + approvals) to “what will execute” (a typed contract) at runtime.
points: A runtime “airlock→broker” boundary turns agent proposals into typed execution contracts that only execute when accompanied by a signed, evidence- and policy-version–bound certificate, preventing non-deterministic reasoning from directly mutating production state even if the agent is compromised.

A Go prototype evaluated over 2,500 admission attempts (500 labeled contracts) achieved 96.8% routing accuracy versus expert-labeled certification paths while reducing required human approvals by 68.2%, suggesting consequence-aware escalation can preserve oversight without making all changes queue for humans.

Measured overheads were modest for the fast path (PolicyOnly admission p50 1.84 ms; broker verification p50 0.15 ms) while higher-assurance paths added validator latency (SQA p50/p95 185/380 ms), with an unsafe admission rate of 0.4% and per-action ledger storage of 3.4 KB enabling revocation and replayable audits.

nodes.title: Agents All the Way Down; A Methodology for Building Custom AI Agents from Substrate to Production
nodes.one_liner: A pragmatic agent-engineering playbook that turns 'agents building agents' into a repeatable dev+QA loop by shipping the agent as a CLI and testing it with another agent.
points: A five-phase, end-to-end workflow for custom agent engineering is specified with two one-time preconditions (LLM-as-component “substrate” plus core building blocks) and a repeatable lifecycle loop (prototype → ship-as-CLI → agent-tests-agent) intended to run indefinitely as the agent evolves.

Packaging agents as a CLI (“Turtle pattern”) makes multi-agent orchestration a composition problem rather than a framework problem, because invoking another agent becomes operationally equivalent to invoking any other command-line tool.

Practical overhead measurements cited for MCP vs CLI indicate extreme context-tax scenarios—e.g., ~35× more tokens with reduced completion (100%→72%), ~44,026 vs ~1,365 tokens for a matched GitHub task, and ~55,000 registry tokens for 93 tools vs ~200 for gh—implying that large tool registries can dominate cost, latency, and reliability unless aggressively scoped.

nodes.title: RedAct: Redacting Agent Capability Traces for Procedural Skill Protection
affiliations: Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
University of Chinese Academy of Sciences
nodes.one_liner: Agent traces should be treated as security interfaces: they leak reusable procedural know-how unless selectively rewritten, and watermarkable behaviors can later flag downstream reuse with low false alarms.
points: Publicly released agent execution traces enable substantial black-box procedural skill theft, with normalized skill transfer (NST) of 44.7–67.1% from raw traces across synthesis/evolution/retrieval reuse—recovering much of the oracle-skill utility without access to private skill files or model weights.

Selective key-item–guided trace rewriting suppresses transferable capability below the no-skill baseline, dropping reuse performance (SSR) from 71.8–73.7% on raw traces to 65.5–67.5% on protected traces and driving NST to non-positive values (down to −29.4%, at most −5.9%).

Behavioral watermarking provides high-confidence reuse evidence without breaking task semantics, achieving 93.6–100.0% true detection with ≤1.9% false alarms for standalone hooks (while contextual hooks keep 0% false alarms but lower detectability at 16.4–32.2%).

nodes.title: OpenPCC: Open and Confidential LLM Serving on Commodity TEEs
nodes.one_liner: It shows that open, deployable confidential LLM inference can be practical on off-the-shelf CPU/GPU TEEs if you treat attestation as a cached session primitive and cryptographically bind keys to measurements.
points: An end-to-end confidential LLM serving stack on commodity Intel TDX + NVIDIA H100 Confidential Computing achieves a median 6.73% p50 time-to-first-token (TTFT) overhead versus a non-TEE “plain served” baseline, with TTFT overhead ranging from 0.4% to 28.9% depending on prompt/batch size.

Composite CPU+GPU attestation is the dominant cost at ~2.03 seconds on the warm path, but caching/amortization reduces this to ~21.9 ms/request at 100 requests per token lifetime and <11 ms/request at ≥200 requests, while per-request cryptography remains ~1.92 ms total (ECDH+HKDF+AES-GCM+verification).

Binding the inference node’s ECDH public key into the attested report (rather than using a provider-run CA) prevents man-in-the-middle key substitution while enabling “separation of trust” by anchoring verification in third-party TEE vendor attestation services instead of the cloud operator.

nodes.title: Sim2Schedule: A Simulator-Guided LLM Framework for Autonomous Open-Pit Mine Scheduling
affiliations: Quantum Communications and Computing Research Center
nodes.one_liner: Hard constraints enforced by a simulator turn an off-the-shelf, locally run LLM into a near-optimal long-horizon industrial scheduler without training, while avoiding MILP’s exponential runtime wall.
points: A simulator-constrained, zero-shot LLM agent generates fully feasible open-pit extraction/processing schedules and achieves 94–99% of MILP-optimal NPV while scaling roughly linearly in runtime as instance size grows.

Context is decisive: providing live mine-state and feasible-action context lifts the LLM to ~94.5% of MILP NPV on a 27-block case (vs ~92.6% for no-context/greedy at $19,062 vs $20,579), indicating that without structured state the model collapses to short-sighted heuristics.

Compared to MILP’s exponential growth (e.g., ~12,603s at N=45 even with a 5–6% termination gap), the best local LLM run stays within ~1–1.2% of the MILP-reported NPV at N=45 while using ~15% of the computation time, suggesting a practical path for long-horizon scheduling when re-optimization latency is operationally unacceptable.

nodes.title: Shape Formation for the Cooperative Transportation of Arbitrary Objects Using Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
nodes.one_liner: A decentralized MARL policy can implicitly approximate MILP-optimal, torque-balanced support placements for arbitrarily shaped, non-uniform-mass objects—something geometry-uniform formation methods systematically miss.
points: Across 150 post-evaluation runs per setting, the learned MAPPO policies achieved high formation success in varied conditions—E1–E4 success rates stayed ~92–100% for 3–20 robots—indicating robust generalization to clutter and shifted centers of mass.

Object cavities and highly irregular geometry materially degrade formation reliability: success in E5 dropped to 73.67% (N=3) and 84–93% (N=5–20), showing that feasible-support-region fragmentation is a primary failure driver for decentralized formation.

Against geometry-only and random baselines on shifted-CoM objects, the learned policy produced near-optimization-quality formations (e.g., average L1 cost 0.03 for N=3 and 1.11 for N=20) while maintaining 90–97% success, versus DuD+EM at 14–96% success and much higher L1 cost (0.25 to 3.23), demonstrating that explicitly load-aware objectives are crucial for non-uniform mass distribution.

nodes.title: SwarmSense-DNN: A Trustworthy and Decentralized Neural Framework for Proactive Anomaly Defense in Consumer IoT
nodes.one_liner: Swarm-style trust and pheromone signaling can replace naive FedAvg with a markedly more bandwidth-efficient, fault-tolerant anomaly defense that still holds up under differential privacy.
points: A decentralized swarm-coordinated hierarchical federated framework achieved 95.44% average anomaly-detection accuracy across five IoT security benchmarks (AUC 0.967), outperforming the strongest baseline by ~5.26 percentage points.

Pheromone/trust-weighted selective sharing cut per-node communication from 2160 MB/h (FedAvg-AD) to 585 MB/h (72.9% reduction) and reached convergence in 32 rounds versus 98–125 for leading baselines (≈64% faster than the best competitor).

With differential privacy enabled, performance remained practical at ε=1.0 (93.2% accuracy) while reducing measured leakage from 0.847 to 0.089 and increasing attack resistance to 94.3%, and the system retained 87.9% accuracy under 40% node failures with recovery as fast as 2.3 rounds.
